Transcript o f the Oral Commentary  
to Ngawang Palden ’s The Illumination of the Texts o f Tantra   
by Geshe Chonyi  
 
Root text : The Illumination of the Texts of Tantra: The Principles of the Grounds and Paths of 
the Four Great Secret Classes of Tantra by Ngawang Palden , translation and copyright Voula 
Zarpani and Ian Coug hlan, 2005.  FPMT Basic Program material, edition 2005.  
 
 LESSON NO: 1     DATE: 17
TH FEBRUARY 2009  
 
There is a Tibetan saying, “A man must have a mind just as a pig must have a nose.”  What this 
means is that as human beings with minds, we have the   ability to distinguish between what is 
right and what is wrong. To be a human being, we must have this ability. In a similar way, the pig must have a pig’s nose to be a real pig.  Having this ability to distinguish between right and wrong means we can deve lop a good and kind heart and make our human existence more 
meaningful.  
 There is no limit to the development of our ability to think and analyse. There is so much to think about. In the teachings of the Buddha, we have the stages of the path that are sha red 
with the persons of small capacity which are taught to those whose mental powers and abilities are not so well developed. For those whose minds are more developed, then the path 
that is shared with the persons of middling capacity is revealed. When the  mind improves, the 
Mahayana path is revealed. When the mind is developed even further, the path of tantra is 
revealed to such practitioners.  
 When we set our minds on something that is meaningful, that accords with reality and is purposeful, we can devel op our understanding of such objects or topics. There is no limit to our 
understanding of these objects/topics as we can  reflect on them in so many different ways.  
 This is one of the reasons why whatever teachings we receive should fit the level of our m inds. 
The same applies to the practice of tantra. In order to receive teachings on tantra, one has to receive an initiation. When the teachings don’t fit the level of our minds, our mental capacity, 
then receiving teachings on tantra may be more harmful th an beneficial for our minds.  
  
Geshe -la: There is another phrase in Tibetan, “The frog in the well who died of a heart attack.” 
There is once a businessman who went for a business trip across the seas. When he returned from his trip, he bought a tortoise. He put the tortoise inside a well together with a frog which he already owned. One day, the frog and the tortoise had this conversation.  
Frog: Where did you come from? Tortoise: I came from a big ocean.  
Frog: How big is your ocean? Is it that big? (The fro g indicated the width with its two fingers).  
Tortoise: No, no, no. It is bigger than that.  
Frog: (The frog then widened his fingers) This big?  
 
Lesson 1              Page 1 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
Tortoise: Bigger than that.  
Frog: (The frog then fully stretched out both of its arms) That big?  
Tortoise: No, no, no. It is much, much bigger than that. It is millions of times bigger than that!  
 
The frog, upon hearing this, died of a heart attack! This is because what the tortoise had told the frog could not fit its mind.  
 
That is why when you hear teachings on tantra and if they cannot fit your mind, then you may 
get a heart attack or you may criticise tantra, thinking that what you heard is impossible, simply because it cannot fit your mind. You may think, “How is that possible?” because you don’t have enough k nowledge of tantra. Just by hearing a word here and there, you may think 
that tantra is not the teachings of the Buddha, that it is useless or confusing it with Hindu tantra or karma yoga. That is not beneficial.  
 Lama Tsongkhapa said in the Foundation of All Good Qualities: “Having become a pure vessel 
by training in the general path,/Please bless me to enter/The holy gateway of the fortunate ones:/The supreme vajra vehicle.”  This is why  we should practise to train our minds well in the 
shared paths.  
 Today’s session is only an introduction but from the next lesson onwards, only those who have received the appropriate great initiations can listen to these teachings. You can listen to the 
entire teachings only if you have received the initiation of highest yoga tantra (HYT). If you 
have received the great initiation of action tantra, then you can listen to the teachings on 
action tantra. But when we move on to the HYT section, you are not allowed to listen to the 
teachings. Please observe this tradition. It  will be detrimental to both the teacher and the 
student if this tradition is not observed.  
 To those students who have attended the Basic Program on a regular basis but have not received any initiations, there is not much thing we can do about that now. However, I would like to suggest that you study the Lam Rim text every Tuesday and Thursday for two hours at the same time as these classes. If you are able to schedule that time during the class days by reading 5 to 10 pages for the next three months, it will be very beneficial. If you can study the Lam Rim in such a manner, it will help you to gain an understanding of the Lam Rim. It would be very helpful if you can reflect on and contemplate what you have read.  
 
Whether you gain an understanding of tantra or whether your tantric practice goes well 
depends on how well you know the sutric teachings and how well you practise them. Good understanding of tantra and good practice of tantra can only come about on the basis of having good understanding of the sutric teachings and practising them well, especially the three principal  aspects of the path: renunciation, bodhicitta and the correct view.    The teachings of the Buddha have two divisions: (1) the scriptural teachings and (2) the realisational teachings . The scriptural teachings have three divisions which are known as the 
three scriptural collections (or the Three Baskets). They are:  
 1. the vinaya, the main subject matter which is the higher training in ethics;  
 
Lesson 1              Page 2 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
2. the sutras, the main subject matter which is  the higher training in concentration and  
3. the Abhidharma, the main subject matter which is the higher training in wisdom.  
 
The three scriptural collections can also be condensed into two: (1) the Hinayana scriptural collections and (2) the Mahayana scrip tural collections.  
 The Mahayana scriptural collections have two divisions: (1) the scriptural collections of the 
Mahayana causal perfection vehicle and (2) the scriptural collections of the Mahayana 
resultant vajra vehicle.  
 The realisational teachings are the practice of the realisations of the three  higher trainings which are the subject matter of the three scriptural collections.  
In short, the realisational teachings are the three higher trainings. Elaborating further on the realisational teachings,  then there are the realisational teachings of the three vehicles : 
 1. the realisational teachings of the hearers vehicle, which can be divided into the five paths of the realisations of the hearers.  
2. the realisational teachings of the solitary realisers vehicle, which can be divided into the five paths of the realisations of the solitary realisers.  
3. the realisational teachings of the Mahayana vehicle, which can be divided into the five paths of the realisations of the Mahayanists.  
 This then is what constitutes  the teachings of the Buddha, that is, the scriptural and the 
realisational teachings.  
 What does it mean then “to uphold the teachings of the Buddha”? That means  we should uphold the scriptural teachings of the Buddha as well as the  realisational teachings of the Buddha:  
 
• Upholding the scriptural teachings of the Buddha means studying the three  collections and 
sharing our understanding of them with others.  
• Upholding the realisational teachings of the Buddha means to engage in the practice of the 
three hi gher trainings and when they take root in our minds, that is upholding the 
realisational teachings of the Buddha.   
 We all accept that the teachings of the Buddha are very good and beneficial and that there is a need to preserve those teachings so that they can last for a very long time. In order to do this, we have to learn and understand those teachings. On the basis of that understanding, if we can develop the three higher trainings, the realisations of the paths in our minds, that would be even better.  Since we all recognise the value and benefit of the Buddhadharma, we all have 
this responsibility to preserve the teachings of the Buddha. This is something we should keep in our minds at all times.  
 We have to understand what is really meant when we say we want to preserve, uphold or spread the teachings of the Buddha. His Holiness the Dalai Lama always mentions in his teachings that preserving the teachings of the Buddha is not building new prayer halls or more 
 
Lesson 1              Page 3 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
monasteries. Nor does it mean the presence of many Sangha  members. The actual meaning of 
the preservation of the Buddhadharma is preserving both the scriptural and the realisational 
teachings through studying, learning and sharing them with others and by gaining the realisations.  
 The purpose of practising the teachings of the Buddha is to correct our own mistakes and improve the states of our minds. It is not for the sake of correcting the faults and mistakes of 
others. It is important therefore that whatever we practise should not become the condition 
for minding other people’s business. We should remember this when we listen to and practise the teachings.  
 The beginning of the Lam Rim teachings deals with how we should listen to the teachings by abandoning the three faults of the vessel, that is , (1) being upside down, (2) though held right 
side up, being dirty and (3) though clean, having a leaky bottom. Abandoning the fault of the contaminated vessel means to listen to the teachings with a correct and pure motivation. Otherwise, we will not ben efit from those teachings even though the teachings are pure. The 
best motivation is what I have mentioned earlier, that is, to learn the teachings in order to correct our mistakes and to improve ourselves. We should reflect on the teachings so that they have some impact on our minds so that our minds can change for the better. This is something 
we have to do on our own. When our minds improve,  even by a little bit, we will naturally feel happy,  enthusiasm will arise and we will want to learn more and ref lect more on the teachings.  
 Whatever I have mentioned so far is not something that you do not know or is unaware of. I brought these points up as reminders for you. There are students, especially those who have 
attended many teachings, who are unaffected  by the teachings. The teachings do not benefit 
their minds.  Rather they think,  “I  have heard this before. I already know all this.”  Because of that  attitude, they do not pay much attention to the teachings even though they are physically present. If they go on like that, then they will not derive any benefit from the teachings. This is 
something we have to be aware of and to make sure that this does not happen to us. As I mentioned earlier, changing our minds can only happen when we reflect on the tea chings from 
our own side. Nobody else can do this for us. Our minds will not change simply by listening to the teachings alone. We have to reflect on and analyse them. Only then can change happen. This is another thing that we have to remember all the time . 
 The realisational teachings have two divisions:  
  
1. The realisational teachings of the Lesser Vehicle (the Hinayana)  
2. The realisational teachings of the Great Vehicle (the Mahayana)  
 The Hinayana and the Mahayana are differentiated by the path followed and the method.  There is no practice of bodhicitta (or mind generation) in the Hinayana. Developing bodhicitta exists only in the Mahayana.   In terms of the three vehicles, there are the (1) small vehicle, (2) the middling vehicle and (3) the great vehicle. T he small and middling vehicles are subsumed under the Lesser Vehicle (the 
Hinayana). Therefore, the Hinayana has two divisions:  
 
Lesson 1              Page 4 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
(1) the hearers path and  
(2) the solitary realisers path.  
 
The differences between the hearers path and solitary realisers pat h lie in terms of:  
 
• their ability to bring to culmination their familiarisation with the path.  
• the faculty of their trainees (i.e. some have sharper faculties than others) and  
• the goal of their attainment i.e. nirvana. (One’s state of liberation is higher than the other).  
 When we look at the realisational teachings of the Mahayana, there are two divisions:  
 (1) the Mahayana causal perfection vehicle and  
(2) the Mahayana resul tant vajra vehicle  
.  
These two are not differentiated by their views of reality (emptiness) but are differentiated on the basis of method.  
 The view of reality that is taught in both the Mahayana vehicles is the same, that is, the emptiness asserted by the  Consequence Middle Way School (CMWS). The main goal of the 
Mahayanists is to achieve the welfare of others. Their aspiration to attain enlightenment is necessary for them to accomplish their true goal which is to achieve the welfare of others.  
 
The enligh tenment they are seeking consists of two bodies: (1) the truth body (or dharmakaya) 
and (2) the form body (or rupakhaya). It is through the form body that the Buddha works for 
the benefit of others by appearing to the objects that are to be subdued (i.e. s entient beings) in 
the aspect of the form body. The Mahayanists therefore primarily aspire to achieve the form 
body because it is through the appearance of the form body that the welfare of others can be accomplished. In this context, enlightenment specifi cally refers to the form body. It is not that 
the Mahayanists do not seek to achieve the truth body. In fact, the form body cannot be achieved without achieving the truth body because these two bodies are attained simultaneously. But their primary aim is t o achieve the form body because it is through the 
form body that they can work for the benefit of sentient being.  
 Both the Mahayana causal perfection vehicle and the Mahayana resultant vajra vehicle assert that it is through completing the collection of wisdom that  one attains the truth body and it is through the completion of the accumulation of merit that one achieves the form body. As 
mentioned earlier, these two vehicles are also not differentiated by their view of emptiness.  
 
The difference comes i n their assertions regarding the causes for achieving the form body. The 
Mahayana resultant vajra vehicle has the presentation of the uncommon causes for achieving the form body of the Buddha which is not found in the Mahayana causal perfection vehicle and  
constituted a special method at the time of the cause (i.e., when one has still to attain buddhahood) whereby one meditates by visualising oneself as the deity.  
 The Mahayana causal perfection vehicle (Mahayana sutra) teaches the meditation on emptiness  which is similar in aspect to the goal of the dharmakaya which is to be achieved at 
 
Lesson 1              Page 5 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
the time of the result. But this vehicle does not teach the meditation that is similar in aspect to 
the rupakhaya that one will achieve at the time of result. This meditation is only found in the Mahayana resultant vajra vehicle (tantra) which involves visualizing oneself as the deity which is similar in aspect to the form body that one will achieve at the time of result. The difference between these two Mahayana vehicles therefore is primarily based on the method which refers to the cause for achieving the form body of the Buddha.  
 
Why is the Mahayana causal perfection vehicle and Mahayana resultant vajra vehicle so called?  
 
• For the former vehicle, at the time when one is training on the path, one trains only in the 
mind generation (bodhicitta) and the six perfections and these are posited as the causes for 
achieving the form body. According to this vehicle, at the time of the cause while one is still at the path, one does not meditate on the aspects of the four complete purities that exist at the 
time of the result. Therefore this Mahayana path is called the causal perfection vehicle  
 
• The Mahayana resultant vajra vehicle is also referred to as the Vajrayana, Secret Vajrayan a, 
Mantrayana, Secret Mantrayana or Methodyana. The Mahayana resultant vajra vehicle teaches meditation that can be done while one is still on the path at the time of the cause. One meditates on the four complete purities that exist at the time of enlighte nment (i.e. the 
time of the result). Because of this meditation technique, it is called the Mahayana resultant  
vajra vehicle.  
 
• In the term, “Vajrayana,” vajra refers to the inseparability of method and wisdom for in the 
practice of this path, both method a nd wisdom can be found in one mind. For example, while 
one visualises oneself as the deity, at the same time, with the same mind, one realises that the deity’s body is empty of inherent existence. In this way, both method and wisdom can be found in a singl e consciousness. Therefore, this is also called the inseparable yoga of method 
and wisdom. Such a path that is the inseparability of method and wisdom is not found in the Mahayana causal perfection vehicle. There is discussion in this vehicle on how method  is 
conjoined with wisdom and vice versa and how they both complement one another but this method and wisdom are in two separate minds, that is, both method and wisdom are not found in one mind.  
 
• The term, “Methodyana” (or method vehicle) refers to the fact that the Vajrayana possesses 
a method of achieving the form body of the Buddha which is superior to the method taught in the causal perfection vehicle .  
 
• The Vajrayana is also called Secret Mant ra because we should keep such teachings and 
practice secret and not reveal them to unsuitable vessels or  recipients. The Sanskrit term mantra means protecting the mind. What are we protecting the mind from? In Je Tsongkhapa’s text, it is mentioned that w e are protecting the mind from the suffering of 
samsara and the lower realms.  In the writings of the Seventh Dalai Lama, Kelsang  Gyatso, he 
explains that mantra means protecting the mind from ordinary appearance and grasping.  
 
• The Mahayana sutra, the cau sal perfection vehicle is also referred to as the path that is 
 
Lesson 1              Page 6 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
separated from desire (or attachment). The Mahayana tantra, the resultant Vajra vehicle is 
referred to as the path that is with attachment. As to how the resultant Vajra vehicle is the path tha t is with desire and on how it is related to the four classes of tantra, we will elaborate 
further in the next lesson.

LESSON NO: 2     DATE: 19TH FEBRAURY 2009  
 
Shakyamuni Buddha said, “Listen well, listen thoroughly and hold it in your mind.” What the 
Buddha said is extremely important. The essence is that you have made your way to the centre, 
putting up with difficulties and so forth, thinking, “I am here to lis ten to the teachings.” Since 
you are already physically here, you may as well be mentally present instead of letting your 
mind wander elsewhere. You should pay attention to the teachings. Otherwise, you are 
probably not going to learn anything.  
 If possib le, we should reflect on the benefits of listening to Dharma teachings, such as how it 
plants positive imprints on our mental continua. You should listen to the teachings voluntarily, with a happy mind from your own side.  Tantra is more profound than sutra so we should 
remind ourselves how fortunate we are to have this opportunity to learn about it. Please keep 
this in mind.  
 
*******  
In the previous lesson, we mentioned that there are two yanas (vehicles): the Hinayana and 
Mahayana, both of which have two  divisions.  
 The Hinayana has two divisions: (1) the hearers vehicle and (2) the solitary realisers vehicle. 
Why is this so? The differentiation does not lie in their view of reality (the wisdom aspect) but 
rather with the method. What is this method? The  solitary realisers are able to accumulate 
merit over a longer period of time than the hearers who do not have this ability.  
 The Mahayana also has two divisions:  
1. the Mahayana causal perfection vehicle  (Paramitayana or Mahayana Sutra)  
2. the Mahayana resulta nt Vajrayana (vajra vehicle)  
These two are also not differentiated by their views of reality (emptiness) but they are differentiated with regards to the collection of merit which is the cause for actualising the Buddha’s form body.  
 
The resultant vajra veh icle teaches a method for attaining the form body that is more profound 
than that taught in the causal perfection vehicle. In the causal perfection vehicle, it is taught that through familiarisation with the wisdom directly perceiving emptiness, one accumu lates 
the collection of wisdom leading to the attainment of the truth body. Through the practice of 
bodhicitta together with the six perfections, one accumulates the collection of merit which leads to the attainment of form body.   The explanation of how the form body is achieved in the Mahayana sutra was covered in the previous module on the Uttaratantra. The bodhisattva on the tenth ground achieves the 
mental body adorned with the signs and exemplifications of a buddha and that is the 
substantial cause fo r achieving the form body of the Buddha. We should analyse whether this is 
the case or not in reality.   This position is not accepted from the tantric perspective, which asserts that the mental body 
 
Lesson 2            Page 8 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
of the tenth level bodhisattva that is adorned with the signs and exemplifications of a buddha 
is not the substantial cause for the form body of the Buddha. It is only in tantra that there is an uncommon cause for achieving the Buddha’s form body which means tantra has a superior method for achieving the form b ody of the Buddha and is therefore superior to sutra.  
 The final conclusion is that it is only in tantra that we are able to find the correct explanation for the substantial cause of the Buddha’s form body. That is what makes tantra really precious 
and more exalted than sutra. This is the conclusion but proponents of the Mahayana sutra may 
object, “It is not that we do not know the cause to achieve the Buddha’s form body. We know the cause to be the mental body of the tenth level bodhisattva that is adorned with the major 
marks and minor signs.”  
 I brought up this qualm so that you can squeeze your brain a little bit. It is pointless to immediately give you the answer. When a qualm is raised, instead of the teacher giving the answer right away, you should th ink about it. After thinking about it for a few days and if you 
still cannot get the answer, then you can ask the teacher. At that time when the teacher gives the answer, the answer will stay in your mind forever. If you do not think about it, the teacher’ s  
answer may stay in your mind only for maybe one or two days. It will never stick in your mind.  
 The main point is that tantra has the uncommon explanation on how to actualise the Buddha’s form body which the Mahayana sutra does not.  The Mahayana tantr a is superior to sutra 
because of this superior explanation on how to achieve the Buddha’s form body. You have to prove to yourself why the Mahayana sutra’s explanation on the method of achieving the Buddha’s form body is inadequate. We are not saying that the Mahayana sutra does not teach 
the method for achieving the Buddha’s form body but from the perspective of tantra, that explanation is incorrect and inadequate.  
In this context, there are two parts in the explanation of the principles of the grounds a nd 
paths of the four great secret classes of tantra:  
1 A brief teaching of the way to progress on the path of the three lower classes of 
tantra  
2 A more detailed explanation of the way to progress on the path of the Highest Yoga 
Tantra  
THE THREE LOWER TANTRAS  
 
1 A BRIEF TEACHING OF THE WAY TO PROGRESS ON THE PATH OF THE T HREE LOWER 
CLASSES OF TANTRA  
has two divisions:  
1 The actual lower classes of tantra  
2 The way of positing the five paths and the  ten grounds for them.  
 
1.1 THE ACTUAL LOWER CLA SSES OF  TANTRA  
This has three divisions:

1 The way to progress on the path of Action Tantra  
2 The way to progress on the path of Performance Tantra  
3 The way to progress on the path of Yoga Tantra  
 
ACTION TANTRA  
 
1.1.1  THE WAY TO PROGRESS ON THE PATH OF ACTIO N TANTRA  
has four divisions:  
1 Becoming a vessel suitable to meditate on the path.  
2 Keeping the vows and commitments pure, after becoming a vessel.  
3 The way to perform prior approximation, while abiding in the commitments.  
4 The way to establish the siddhi s after renderin g the approximation serviceable  
 
1.1.1.1   BECOMING A VESSEL SU ITABLE TO MEDITATE O N THE PATH  
 
How does one become a suitable vessel to receive such teachings or practices?   As is stated in the Great and Condensed Stages of the Path, (first ) one trains one’s (mental) 
continuum well in the common paths. Then through relying on a qualified vajra master in the way that it is explained in the classes of Tantra, one enters in the mandala of any of the three lineages of Action Tantra and receives the empowerment.  
 There are different ways of relying on the guru. One way of relying on the guru is explained in the vinaya, the code of conduct for the Sangha. Disciples are taught to regard their teachers to be like the Buddha himself by cultivating faith in and respect towards them. This is the same 
approach to be followed by Hinayana trainees.  
 
A superior manner of relying on the guru is taught in the Mahayana sutra.  It is insufficient to 
view the guru to be like  the Buddha but one should generate the attitude that the guru is  the 
actual Buddha and on that basis, one cultivates faith in and respect towards him. Such guru devotion is based on the recognition that the nature of the guru is the Buddha and not that th e 
guru is taking the physical aspect of a buddha.  
 An even more advanced manner of relying on the guru is taught in the resultant Vajrayana. In tantric practice, one trains not only to regard the nature of the guru to be Buddha but even in his physical ap pearance, one trains to see the guru as having the pure aspect of Vajradhara. 
This is the correct manner of relying on the guru and is required for tantric practice. We can see here how the practice of guru devotion is progressively more profound and diffe rent within 
the Hinayana, Mahayana sutric and Mahayana tantric vehicles.   
 
On that basis, in order to practice Action Tantra, one enters into the mandala of Action Tantra 
in any of the three lineages:

Three 
Lineages  Level  Illustration of 
deity  One recognises the lineage of the deity 
through identifying the   
adornment of the lord of lineage:  
Tathagata 
lineage  Superior  Namgyalma  The lord of the lineage is Vairocana  
Lotus lineage  Middling  Chenrezig  The lord of the lineage is Amitabha  
Vajra line age Lower  Sangye  
Mitrugpa  The lord of the lineage is Akshobhya  
 For example, Chenrezig is adorned with Amitabha as the lord of the lineage. That deity is therefore classified to belong to the lotus lineage of Action Tantra. This is just an introduction to the three lineages of Action Tantra. There are further subdivisions within these three lineages.  
 In Lama Tsongkhapa’s Great Stages of Mantra, which is the tantric equivalent to the Lamrim 
Chenmo , he says that  someone who has entered the mandala and received the initiation of a 
deity of the Tathagata lineage, that pe rson is also allowed to do the approximation retreats and 
practices of the deities of the Lotus lineage and Vajra lineage even though that person has not received the great initiations of those lineages.   
 
If a person has received the great initiation and  entered into the mandala of a deity of the 
Lotus lineage of Action Tantra, that person can also do the retreats and practices of the deities 
of the Vajra lineage of Action Tantra, that is, the lineage of the lower lever but not the lineage of the level above it.  
 If a person has received the great initiation and entered into the mandala of the deity of the Vajra lineage of Action Tantra, that person is only allowed to do the retreats and practices of the deities of that lineage.  
 Can a person who has rece ived the great initiation of Highest Yoga Tantra do the practices of all 
the tantras below it without having received the great initiation of those tantras? As mentioned earlier, a person who has received the great initiation of the Tathagata lineage can 
do the practices of the Lotus and Vajra lineages even without having received the great 
initiation of the relevant deities. If we extend this logic, then we have the following qualm: Can a person who has received the great initiation of a Performance Tantra  deity do the practices 
of Action Tantra deities without having received the great initiation of those deities? This is a qualm that is not explicitly mentioned anywhere. If I do get a chance to ask someone about this, I will clarify this point later.  
  Interpreter: Ven. Tenzin Gyurme

LESSON NO: 3     DATE: 24TH FEBRAURY 2009  
 
Many conducive factors have to be present before one can listen to the teachings. When they 
are present, then the teacher is able to teach and the students also have the opportunity to 
listen to the teachings. It is not so easy for all these factors to come about. Sometimes, one 
cannot find any listeners and there are times when people cannot find a teacher. There is a 
story of a Ganden Tripa, the throne holder who is deemed to be a repre sentative of Lama 
Tsongkhapa. Therefore, he must be very learned but no -one came to listen to his teachings. So 
he had to perform a ritual which involved waving a wand to trap all the positive conditions. He 
shouted,  “May you bring the fortunate disciples  to me.”  
 From the side of the student, it may not be conducive if there are only two or three persons 
attending class. Normally, it is more encouraging when you see more people in class. This also 
encourages oneself to continue the class as a group. It does help. But if you come to a class 
with only a few people,  it may not be so helpful to one’s mind. We are all from the same class. 
We are part of the Basic Program. On an individual level, when we see our classmates, we 
should understand that they are a  kind of support to our studies and are one of the conducive 
factors for us to continue in our studies. Since that is the case, then it is important to have 
mutual respect for one another.  
 
At this present moment, we are experiencing the situation in whic h all the conducive factors 
and conditions are there for us to study. Since the conditions are there, then we have to try as 
much as possible to attend classes and while we are here, we should try as much as possible to listen attentively. Also, we should read the text.   
It is important to make it a point to attend the classes regularly without missing any of the classes. However, if one really cannot put aside the time on a particular day, then of course, nothing can be done. But we should keep the goal o f wanting to study in our minds and with 
this goal in mind, we have to put in the effort to come to classes continuously, without missing any of the classes. If you don’t come to classes continuously, on a regular basis but only attend classes from time to  time, I think it is difficult to learn anything.  
 
Geshe -la: Maybe I should just tell you my own experience. When I was studying in Sera Je, I 
almost never missed  a class, debate session or puja, except when I was very sick and could not 
move. Then that is  a different story.  
 
One time I went for a holiday in Nepal to visit my parents and I couldn’t get back in time for the classes and I had to miss classes for one particular subject. I knew all the other subjects quite well, but somehow I was not good at t hat particular subject. I feel I still don’t know and was not 
comfortable with that subject. Even though I continued with the class and listened to the 
teacher,  somehow I cannot really grasp and still have my doubts with that subject.  Sometimes 
I  regret  missing those classes. I can still remember  that.  
 
 
Lesson  3                           Page 13 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
Different people may have different ways of studying. For me, I must listen, I must debate and 
discuss. Normally, I don’t listen to the recordings and also, I don’t really keep notes. My way of learning is through listening and discussing. My point is that because of having missed the 
classes for that particular subject, I am still ‘blur’ (unclear) about it. To me, it is extremely important that if I want to learn, I shouldn’t miss class. This is my own experience.  
 It is important to come to class continuously. Not only that, you need to read and you also need 
to discuss. Only after that will you have some kind of understanding and you won’t forget the 
subject easily. If you just listen but don’t discuss or don’t rea d again, you may understand it 
today and tomorrow but after two or three days later, you would forget what you had learnt or what you had heard. To study, it is good to listen and then read and then to have discussions. That is important. If you do that co ntinuously, I think you will have some kind of stable 
understanding.  
 Also, I see some people writing lots of notes. I am not saying this is not good as different people may have different methods. But for me, this is useless. You can’t concentrate fully as you write. You won’t be able to  concentrate well. After writing the notes, you go back and maybe study them for a few times. But normally, we write the notes and then close [the book] and then we write again in the next class and close [the book]. Some times it is good to keep some remarks or 
some kind of short notes or points. Then it is beneficial.   
I am just talking about my own experiences. That doesn’t mean that it is good for everyone as 
everyone has their different methods of learning.  Whatever works best for you, try and learn that way.  
 
**************************  
 In what mandala is the empowerment bestowed? It is permitted to bestow the empowerment either in a mandala made of colored particles or one painted on cloth and so forth or for some special persons in an absorption mandala.  
 A sand mandala, painted cloth or photocopied picture of the mandala is commonly used. When the person conferring the initiation has attained stable concentration, without having to rely on a basis of attainment ei ther in a form of a sand or painted mandala, he can visualise the 
entire mandala by using his powers of concentration. On the basis of that visualised mandala, 
the vajra master confers the initiation upon his disciples. But this is not something that is 
commonly done. The concentration mandala is only used or reserved for special persons. There 
is also a three -dimensional erected mandala but generally speaking, initiations are not 
conferred using this kind of mandala. I think the Kalachakra initiation is th e only exception.  
 There is no body mandala and so forth, because in the case of the three lower classes of tantra, the conventions of the body mandala, relative bodhichitta mandala, lotus -of-the mother 
mandala and the ultimate bodhichitta mandala are not  mentioned. The reason for this is that 
these four (types of mandalas) are attributes of Highest Yoga Tantra only.  
 
 
Lesson  3                           Page 14 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
The body mandala does not refer merely to placing different deities on different parts of the 
body. For example in the practice of Vajrabhai rava (Yamantaka), there is a visualisation of 
placing various deities on different parts of the body. But it is not said that Yamantaka has the practice of the body mandala. In the Guhyasamaja practice, there is a practice of the body mandala, but there is n’t an initiation into the body mandala of Guhyasamaja whereas in the 
Heruka Chakrasamvara practice, there is an initiation into the body mandala of Heruka. What then does the practice of body mandala mean? An actual practice of the body mandala 
involves visualising different parts of the body transforming into different parts of the celestial 
mansion. That is what is  meant by the practice of body mandala.   The relative or conventional bodhichitta mandala is only found in highest yoga tantra and refers to  the secret substances of the father and mother.  
 The bhaga mandala refers to the secret lotus of the mother.  
 The ultimate bodhichitta mandala refers to the final unification of no more learning.  
 Generally when we hear the word “mandala,” we usually h ave the idea that it refers to the 
celestial mansion with its four doors and four sides. But this is not necessarily so. Rather the word “mandala” literally means the place from which you take the essence.   
What empowerments are bestowed? Only the vase em powerment (is bestowed). The three 
higher empowerments do not exist in the three lower classes of tantra.  
 
The three higher initiations are:  
1. the secret initiation  
2. the wisdom initiation  
3. the word initiation  
 
There is difference in the number of empowerments (given) in the four classes of Tantra. Action Tantra merely has the water and crown empowerments plus the final (empowerment of) ritual objects. Performance Tantra has as well as the first two (empowerments ), just the 
vajra, bell and name empowerments (which constitute) the five knowledge empowerments plus the final (empowerment of) ritual objects. Yoga Tantra has as well as those, just the vajra master empowerment plus the final (empowerment of) ritual obje cts. Highest Yoga Tantra has 
as well as those, the three higher empowerments, (thus making) all four empowerments. In the specific Highest Yoga Tantra, the Kalachakra Tantra, there are seven empowerments as if entering childhood, the four higher empowerments, the four greatly higher empowerments, and the empowerment of the great vajra master lord, making sixteen empowerments.  
 Still, the Vajra Rosary of Mandala Rites states that in Action and Performance Tantras, the five knowledge empowerments are bestowed.  
 There are four classes of Tantra:  
1. Action Tantra  
2. Performance Tantra   
 
Lesson  3                           Page 15 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
3. Yoga Tantra and  
4. Highest Yoga Tantra  
There is a difference in the number of initiations given in the four classes of tantra. For 
example, in Action Tantra, only the water and crown initiations are given together with the final empowerment of ritual objects, for example, one is given the seven  royal possessions of 
the wheel turning kings and then the eight auspicious signs.  
 
Final empowerment of Ritual Objects (mtha’ rten): A type of empowerment given for breath 
and auspiciousness. It is bestowed by cards bearing pictures of deities, mandalas, the seven precious royal emblems, the eight auspicious signs, the eight auspicious substances etc . 
 “Still, the Vajra Rosary of Mandala Rites states  that in Action and Performance Tantras, the five 
knowledge empowerments are bestowed”: It is mentioned earl ier in Action Tantra, it is 
sufficient to confer the water and crown initiations but  it is not incorrect also to bestow the vajra, bell and name initiations in the Action Tantra initiation.  
 1.1.1.2  KEEPING THE VOWS AND  COMMITMENTS PURE AFT ER BECOMING A  VESSEL  
 If one is a suitable basis for the individual emancipation vows, one should take any of the seven types of vows or at the very least, take on the ethics of abandoning the ten nonvirtuous actions as a basis.  
 The outline states that after becoming a suitable vessel, one should keep the vows and commitments purely. If one is a suitable basis for the pratimoksha vows, then one should take 
any of the seven types of vows. The vows have eight categories but only seven is enumerated here. The seven types of pratimoksha vows that are enumerated here are the vows of the:  
1) fully ordained monk  
2) fully ordained nun  
3) novice monk  
4) novice nun  5) probational nun (trainee nun)  
6) layman   7) lay woman  
 The eighth category of pratimoksha vows  is the near abiding vows which refer to the 
pratimoksha vows that are taken for only a day, that is, they last only  for a day and night. I guess because it is only taken for a day, that is why is it is not counted here as one of the vows that is to be co nsidered.  
 Ideally a person who wishes to take a tantric initiation should have taken one of these seven types of pratimoksha vows. You should be someone who falls into one of these categories. If not, at the very least, the basis for taking an initiation  should be the practice of ethics, that is, 
abandoning the ten non -virtuous actions.  
 
 
Lesson  3                           Page 16 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
The vows that are to be kept at the time of the empowerment are only the bodhisattva vows 
because, in (the case of) Action and Performance Tantra, there are no tantric v ows to be kept. 
Due to this, the root downfalls to be guarded (against) are none other than the bodhisattva root downfalls. Nevertheless, there are many specific mantra commitments to be kept. This is understood from the quotation of the well accomplished tantras in the Great Stages of Mantra.  
 As for the root downfalls, one should look them up in the Central Path to Enlightenment . 
Having come to know these, one must definitely put effort into not being polluted by the root downfalls. Even if one gets polluted by other faults, one must not leave it as it is without thinking anything about it. One needs to restore at night those downfalls that occurred during the day and to restore in the day, (down)falls that occurred during the night and to purify them thro ugh confession as is mentioned in the Great Stages of Mantra.  
 
Up to this point, at the very least, you should know that:  
• in Action and Performance Tantras, the initiation is conferred using either the sand mandala 
(more specifically, a mandala made of  coloured particles) or a cloth mandala.  
• the two initiations that are conferred in Action and Performance Tantras are the wa ter and 
crown initiations.  
• the vows that are taken for the initiations of Action and Performance Tantras are the 
bodhisattvas vows and what have to be guarded against are the bodhisattva root downfalls.  
 
What is the nature (entity) of an initiation? The in itiation consists of two entities: - 
1. the entity of consciousness and  
2. the entity of the non -associated compositional factors  
 In Action Tantra, in the water initiation, the water is the initiation substance that is poured into the vase. On the basis of coming into physical contact with the vase and water, one generates the wisdom of great bliss in one’s mental continuum. This gen eration of the wisdom of great 
bliss is the initiation of the entity of consciousness. This water initiation purifies the mental stains and  leaves the potential in the mind to actualise the dharmakaya (truth body). Leaving this imprint in the mind is the initiation of the entity of the non -associated compositional 
factors.  
 Whether or not we actually receive the initiation depends on whether we understand what the vajra master is saying. When the vajra master introduces and gives the initiation, we need t o 
understand what he is saying. For example, when we are given the vase and water, the vajra 
master says, “When the vase and the initiation substances touch you, think that you are 
experiencing great bliss.” This is done through belief. This belief is the entity of consciousness.  
 When you generate the thought, “I am now generating great bliss,” this plants an imprint in your mind that leaves a potential in the mind to actualise the dharmakaya. This imprint is the entity of the non- associated compositional factors.  
 There may be many people present at an initiation but everyone receives the initiation at different levels. Only the best disciples generate the wisdom of great bliss in their continua. 
 
Lesson  3                           Page 17 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
The rest of the disciples will receive the initiation by thinking that such bliss is generated. At the 
very least, at the most basic entry level, in order to receive the initiation, when the vajra master introduces the great initiation, when he says, “T his is the initiation being given. Now 
you have received this initiation,” one must understand what the vajra master is saying and 
generate the belief and think, “This is happening. I am receiving the initiation. I am experiencing this bliss.” If this is not done, then one does not receive the initiation.  
 
This is a  funny story. A man came with a big box of butter to an initiation. At the end of the 
initiation, he brought this box of butter to the Lama and told the Lama, “Now, Lama, please 
visualise that I am offering this box of butter to you and also visualise that  you have received 
this butter.” After saying this to the Lama, he left with the box of butter!  
 As mentioned earlier, there are four classes of Tantra:  
• In Tantra, there is the method of transforming desire into the path and the four Tantric 
classes are differentiated by the various means in which desire is taken into the path.  
• In the Hinayana, no exception is made for the desire or attachment which is seen only as an 
object of abandonment. The Hinayanists are taught the methods to abandon desire completely.  
● In the causal perfection vehicle (Mahayana Sutra), although there is no mention of taking 
desire into the path, nevertheless desire is not viewed as a bad thing and there are exceptions where desire can be used as a tool to benefit sentient beings.  
• In Mahayana Tantra, by taking desire into the path, desire is eliminated in  Tantric practice. 
An analogy would be the example of an insect that is born from the wood which ends up eating the entire piece of wood. Likewise, through the Tantric path that is generated from desire, that path overcomes  desire.  
 The four different ways in which desire is taken into the path are as follows:   
In Action Tantra , the trainee generates desire from gazing at a visualised goddess and then 
brings that desire into the p ath, the entity of which is non- dual profundity and clarity. The 
object here is not an actual goddess but a visualised one.  
 The term, “bringing or transforming desire into the path” should be analysed for its actual meaning. Does desire itself actually t ransform into the path? I guess the meaning is that 
through the desire that arises from gazing at the visualised goddess, one experiences bliss. One then transforms that bliss into the path. The trainee who is practising Action Tantra would only have the a bility to employ this method of transforming the desire that arises from gazing at the 
visualised goddess into the path.   
  In Performance Tantra , in addition to bringing the desire that arises from gazing at the 
visualised goddess into the path, the trai nee is also able to bring the desire that arises from 
smiling at the visualised goddess into the path.  
 In Yoga Tantra, in addition to bringing the desire that arises from gazing or smiling at the 
visualised goddess into the path, the trainee is also able to bring the desire that arises from holding hands with or embracing the visualised goddess into the path.  
 
Lesson  3                           Page 18 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
In the three lower classes of Tantra, the desire that is brought into the path is in relation to a visualised goddess, not a real living person.  
 In Highest Yoga Tantra, the object is not a visualised goddess but an actual person. The trainee 
has the ability to bring the desire that arises from sexual union (literally, the coming together of the two sexual organs) with a real woman into the path.  
 One has to understand the above points carefully, especially with regards to the bringing of the desire that arises from sexual union into the path in relation to the practice of Highest Yoga Tantra. Otherwise, there may be the danger of equating this prac tice with ordinary sexual 
activity.  
 We should understand that the suitable basis for receiving the initiation is that one should be observing one of the seven types of pratimoksha vows. This is very clear. If that is not possible, in order for one to rec eive the initiation, at the very least one must avoid the ten non -virtuous 
actions. Further explanation on this particular point will come later. The main point is to avoid the danger of having the wrong idea that ordinary sexual relationships can be relat ed to the 
practise of Highest Yoga Tantra. It is not like that at all.  
 As mentioned earlier, the suitable basis for receiving the initiation is to keep one of the seven 
types of pratimoksha vows. The supreme pratimoksha vows are the vows of the fully ordained 
monk. This means that a celibate ordained monk does not engage in any sexual activity. If that person engages in sexual activity, then that person would not become a suitable basis for 
receiving the initiation.  
 One of the reasons for the four cla sses of Tantra is because of the different ways of bringing 
desire into the path. There are also different ways to explain the reasons for the four classes of Tantra. We will cover them later.  
 What is the actual meaning then of “transforming desire into the path?” Does this mean that the desire is actually generated into the entity of the path? If that is the case, are we saying that that desire will carry on to enlightenment?  
 Question: During a Kalachakra initiation, it seems that I heard His Holiness t he Dalai Lama tell 
the Theravadan monks that they should not take the initiation as it may cause them to break their vows. Would this be correct?  
Answer:  I don’t think it has to do with breaking the vows if indeed His Holiness actually 
mentioned such a thi ng. I guess it is from the perspective of their familiarity and affinity with 
the Theravadan tradition that may make them  unsuitable vessels as it might be difficult for them to accept such practices.   When we look at the vinaya that is practiced in the Theravadan and the Mahayana traditions, apart from some minor differences, the precepts and the vows are exactly the same. It does not mean that there is some special allowance for those practicing in the Mahayana tradition. 
 
Lesson  3                           Page 19 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
The fully ordained monk who is practicing Tantra also needs to keep the vinaya, that is, all the 
vows purely.  There is no exception.  
 Question: Who would be a suitable recipient for the Kalachakra initiation?  
Answer : It is the same for any initiation. As mentioned clearly in many text s, the person who 
would be a suitable recipient of Tantric initiations should be a bodhisattva i.e. someone who has bodhicitta. For someone who aspires to take a Highest Yoga Tantra initiation, t he basis is to 
keep any of the seven types of pratimoksha vow s. The very basic foundation is one must avoid 
the ten non -virtuous actions and one must have the ability to keep the bodhisattva vows.   
 For those taking Highest Yoga Tantra initiation, in addition to the above, one must keep the Tantric vows purely. If one is able to fulfil all these criteria, one is qualified to take the 
Kalachakra initiation or any other Highest Yoga Tantra initiation.

FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
LESSON NO: 4     DATE: 26TH FEBRUARY 2009  
 
In the last class, we mentioned that an  Action Tantra initiation is conferred on the basis of 
either a sand or cloth mandala. The initiations that are conferred are the water and crown 
initiations. The vows that are taken during the initiation are the bodhisattva vows after which the downfalls to be guarded against are the bodhisattva root downfalls, of which there are 18 
and 46 transgressions or faults.  
 
We also looked at the entity/nature of the initiation. The entities are the entity of 
consciousness a nd the entity of the non -associated compositional factors. This is something 
that you must keep in mind because whether you  actually receive the initiation or not does depend on whether you understand the vajra master. When he is giving the initiation, yo u 
should at least generate the thought and belief, “Now I am receiving this initiation. Now I have received the initiation.” This is the basic minimum without which it is difficult to say that you 
have received the initiation.  
 
We take on vows and commitments (or samaya in Sanskrit, sometimes also  translated as 
secret pledge in English) during the initiation. Is there any difference between vows and 
commitments? In one of Lama Tsongkhapa’s  texts explaining the meaning of empowerments, 
he says that vows a nd samaya are one entity but two isolates, that is, they are one but they can 
be differentiated conceptually.  
 “Samaya” refers to something that you do not transgress, that is, you do not go beyond the established boundary. “Vow” refers to not doing something that is not good. Samaya and vow are mutually inclusive but they are not the same. We have discussed the difference between 
one and different some time ago. For example, “impermanent” and “product”  refer to the 
same thing. Someone who has an alias in addition to his name means he has two names but 
they both refer to the same person.   In the last lesson, we looked at the four classes of Tantra and how in all of them,  desire is taken into the path. However, the way this is done varies. The purpose of using desire on the 
path is to eliminate desire. That is the goal. All the four classes teach the inseparability of deity 
yoga and the meditation on emptiness:   
• In order for the Action Tantra trainee to generate the inseparable yoga of the meditation on 
emptiness and the visualisation of oneself as the deity, that trainee has to rely on external 
activities such as washing, eating pure foods and performing different hand mudras.  
• In Performance Tantra, there is a balance between such external activities and internal 
meditation.  
• In Yoga Tantra, there is only a slight emphasis on external activities. The main focus is on the 
inner yoga of meditation on emptiness together with the visualisation of oneself as the deity.  
• In Highest Yoga Tantra, the trainee does not  rely on external activities but completely 
focuses on the inner yoga with the practice of meditation on emptiness and deity yoga.  
 
Is it all right for someone who is not a suitable receptacle to receive the initiation or someone 
 
Lesson  4                           Page 21 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
who does not aspire to receive the initiation to enter or to see the mandala? Sometimes, this 
question arises. The text indicates very clearly that such people are allowed to enter and see the mandala. Simply by entering and seeing the mandala, one is able to purify obscurations  and 
negativities that have been accumulated over many eons. It also places  positive i mprints on 
that person’s mind to practice Tantra in the future. This is something that you should remember.  
 
1.1.1.3  THE WAY TO PERFORM P RIOR APPROXIMATION W HILE ABIDING  IN THE 
COMMITMENTS  
 
There are two (types of absorption):  
 
1. Absorption with recitation  
2. Absorption not reliant on recitation ( Pg. 3 of the root text )  
 In the beginning, one must train one’s mind on the shared path. On that basis, one enters the mandala and receives the initiation. After having received the initiation, one must protect all the vows and samaya that one has taken. Only after this does one perform the approximation (or retreat).  
 1.1.1.3.1   ABSORPTION WITH RECI TATION  
 One generates oneself as the deity by means of the six deities
1 and having invited the wisdom 
beings (to the place) in front of oneself, one makes offerings and so forth. One recites mantra 
without deterioration of the four branches2, through combining life -force exertion and 
retention. ( Pg. 4 of the root text)  
 
The four branches are:  
1. the self basis  
2. the other basis  
3. the mind basis and  
4. the sound basis.  
On the basis of these four branches, one “ recites mantra without deterioration of the four 
branches through combining life -force exertion and retention .” 
 
Though the same term ‘life -force exertion’ is explained in the three lower classes of tantra, in 
the cycle of the Arya system of Guhyasamaja and in the Wheel of Time (Kalachakra), the 
meaning (in each case) is different.  
 
1 Six deities: 1) empty deity ( stong pa’ i lha ), 2) letter deity (yi ge ’i lha ), 3) sound deity ( sgra ’i lha ), 4) form deity 
(gzugs kyi lha), 5) seal  deity ( phyag rgya’ i lha ) and 6) sign deity ( mtshan ma1i lha)  
2 Four branches of recitation (in the context of Action Tantra): 1) repetition while concentrating on the basis of 
sound ( sgr’'i gsi la gzhol ba), 2) repetition while concentrating on the basis of mind ( sems kyi gzhi la gzhol ba), 3) 
repetition while concentrating on the basis of an other ( gzhan gyi gzhi la gzhol ba) and 4) repetition while 
concentration on the basis of oneself ( bdag gi gzhi la gzhol ba).  
 
 
Lesson  4                           Page 22 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment                                                                   
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
The meaning of ‘life -force exertion’ as explained in the context of the three lower classes of 
Tantra exists because the wind (that circulates) in exhalation and inhalation through the doors 
of the senses and so forth, and the pores of head and body hairs is the ‘life force’. ‘Exertion’ is when mindful of other focal objects, one is mindful of being distracted to them. When these two are held interna lly without external dispersal, this is the meaning of retaining ‘life -force 
exertion’. ( Pg. 4 of the root text )  
 
We now look at the meaning of the phrase “life -force exertion” in the context of the three 
lower classes of Tantra.  
• “Life -force” refers to th e subtle wind that moves in and out of the body through the hair 
pores, sense organs and so forth.  
• “Exertion” refers to the distracted mind that wanders to all sorts of different objects.  
 
“Retaining life -force exertion ” means to retain that subtle wind internally. With respect to 
exertion, it means to keep the mind inward by focusing on some object. In essence, it means not letting the wind out of the body but retaining it internally within the body and not letting the mind be distracted with discursive thoughts but retaining it internally by focusing on some object.  
 By combining life -force exertion and retention, one then recites the mantra without 
deterioration of the four branches in Action Tantra.   The first of the four branches is the basis of sel f. This means one generates oneself as the deity 
by means of the six deities.  Here we are talking about the process of deity yoga in Action 
Tantra which will be helpful in future when we do retreat. The process of generating oneself as the deity in Action Tantra is  always by way of the six deities.  
  
The first of the six deities is the ultimate deity  (or the empty deity). When we generate 
ourselves as the deity in Action Tantra, we meditate on the emptiness of the person who is meditating and the emptine ss of the deity which is the object of our cultivation, i.e., reflecting 
on the meditator and that which is being meditated upon as empty of inherent existence. These two are equal in the sense of not existing inherently. Within that emptiness of inherent existence, these two are inseparable in nature.   From this we can see how the foundation of Tantric practice is completely dependent on the practice of Sutra. This is because whatever deity one meditates on in Tantric practice, the meditation always start s with the meditation on the emptiness of inherent existence. The 
meditation is done through purifying everything into emptiness and then reflecting on the emptiness of inherent existence. We do not meditate on an inherently existent deity!  
 
The emptiness that is meditated upon at the beginning of all deity practices is the emptiness 
that is clarified by Nagarjuna and asserted by the Consequence Middle Way School. Tantra 
does not teach an emptiness that is more profound than that taught in Sutra. Therefore,  in 
order for us to do pure Tantric practice, we must first study and understand emptiness and realise it. Whether Tantra becomes profound or not depends entirely on whether we have, as our foundation, the understanding of Sutra.  
 
Lesson  4                           Page 23 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
We have to understand why deity yoga is said to be very powerful. It is because the meditation on emptiness is always done at the beginning of the practice of deity yoga. That meditation on emptiness is the antidote to our negative emotions and is how our negative emotions can be  
weakened.  
 If we practise deity yoga without any understanding of emptiness and then visualise the 
ordinary self as the deity thinking that it is this ordinary body which transforms into the deity, 
that practice will never help us to weaken our negative emotions as it cannot act as an antidote to our negative emotions. In fact, there is a danger that such a practice can become a condition for negative emotions such as pride to arise. It is only the practice of the meditation on emptiness that makes deity yoga an antidote to negative emotions.  
 With respect to the view of emptiness one is supposed to meditate on in  Tantric practice, one can meditate on emptiness in accordance with the Mind Only School or the Middle Way School. The point is that deity yoga is done on the basis of an ascertainment of the view of emptiness. On that basis, one generates oneself as the deity.   So, in the ultimate deity, one meditates on the emptiness of oneself who is  meditating on the deity as well as the emptiness of the dei ty that one is meditating upon. These two are equal in 
the sense of being empty of inherent existence. The mind (or wisdom) that ascertains the 
emptiness of the self and the emptiness of the deity is transformed into  the aspect of a full 
moon disc.   
In this particular text, the order of the six deities is that the letter deity follows the empty deity. 
However, in practice, the empty deity is followed by the sound deity. We visualise, resonating 
in space, the sound of the mantra of the deity. Although in t he aspect of sound, in reality, it is 
the deity we are meditating on taking the aspect of sound. We concentrate on that sound.  
 Next is the letter deity . Here, we visualise the sound of the mantra taking on the form of the 
letters of the mantra which settles on the moon disc we had visualised earlier.  
 We visualise light rays emitting from the letters of the mantra. At the tip of each light ray is the deity we are meditating on. These deities work for the welfare of sentient beings, purifying 
their negative karma and so forth. On  some of the tips of these light rays, countless offerings 
are made to all the buddhas and bodhisattvas. Then, all the light rays that are emanated 
absorb  back into the letters of the mantra on the moon disc. The moon disc together with the syllables then transform into the deity that we are meditating on, e.g., in the practice of Chenrezig, we transform ourselves into Chenrezig This is the fourth deity, the form deity .  
  Next is the seal deity  (or mudra deity). Having completely transformed into the deity that we 
are meditating on, we perform the respective hand gesture (or mudra) in accordance with the mudra associated with tha t particular lineage of Action Tantra. In the abbreviated way, we 
touch the three different points of our body with the relevant mudra. In the extensive way, we touch the seven points of our body and in the middling way, we touch the five different points 
 
Lesson  4                           Page 24 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
of our body in accordance with Lama Tsongkhapa’s Great Stages of Mantra, which are:  
1. the heart  
2. the point between the eyebrows  
3. the throat  
4. the right shoulder  
5. the left shoulder  
The purpose of touching these different points of the body with the mudra is to bl ess ourselves.  
 
At this point, there is the practice of invoking the wisdom being to merge with oneself as the deity. This is followed by inviting the initiation deities to confer the initiation upon oneself as the deity. This is one way of doing it but, in another tradition, it is said that it is not necessary to do this. We should  follow the text of whatever deity approximation we are doing. In the practice text, there is usually the invocation of the wisdom being that dissolves into oneself and then inviting the initiation deities to confer empowerment on  oneself as the deity.  
 Next is the practice of the sign deity . Having completed the above visualisations, one generates 
clear appearance of oneself as the deity and, at the same time, cultivates divin e pride and, 
without any distraction, one thinks, “I am the actual deity.”  
 This is how one generates  oneself as the deity by means of the six deities  in Action Tantra. 
From here, we can see that the deities do not necessarily refer to the deities we usually think 
of such as having faces and arms and so forth.   
 
We are doing the approximation of the deity on the basis of the self generation practice and 
then generating the clear appearance and cultivating the divine pride of ourselves as a deity. We focus  on that as long as possible. When we become tired, in order to rest, we visualise a 
mantra garland in our hearts and visualise light rays emitting from the mantra garland going out to work for the welfare of sentient beings and making offerings to all the  buddhas and 
bodhisattvas. With this visualisation, we recite the mantra as many times as we wish.  
 In the process of generating ourselves as the deity, we have invoked and absorbed the wisdom being into ourselves So, before we end the session, we make re quests to the wisdom being 
who is abiding at our heart. We visualize the wisdom being leaving our heart and abiding in the space in front of us. We make offerings, offer praises and make requests such as, “Please bestow upon me and all sentient beings all the common and supreme attainments.” We then 
recite the hundred syllables mantra to pacify any faults and omissions we had made during the practice.  Then we visualise the wisdom being returning to his natural abode.  
We hold onto the divine pride of onese lf as the commitment (or samaya) being and then we 
can end the session.   
 In most of the deity practices of Action Tantra, there isn’t any practice of close dissolution though this practice does appear in some Action Tantra texts. There is such a practice  in the 
deity practices of Highest Yoga Tantra at the end of the sadhana.  
 There is a reason for bringing up this question of whether there is the practice of close dissolution. Sometimes, people do ask, “When I have generated myself as a deity, how do I 
 
Lesson  4                           Page 25 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
apologise for the mistakes and purify them?” Here, in the practice of Action Tantra, we 
visualise the wisdom being leaving from the heart of oneself as the deity and abiding in the space in front of us. It is in relation to that wisdom being that we then r equest for forgiveness 
for any mistakes made during the practice.  
 What we have discussed so far is the way of doing the approximation of the deity by depending on self generation alone, i.e., the self basis.  Here, in Action Tantra, it is taught that practising 
the self generation is sufficient for the approximation of the deity. It is not necessary to do the 
practice of the front generation.   It is important for you to remember of what we have discussed today because the same questions arise all the time. “How should I do the retreat? What should I visualise? What are the practices to be done during the retreat?” and so forth. We have discussed all this today. There is nothing more than that. Of course there are different ways of reciting the mantra in relation to the four branches and so far we have only covered the first branch, the self basis.

LESSON NO: 5     DATE: 3RD MARCH 2009  
 
We usually recite prayers before each class, starting with the prayers on prostrations and so 
forth to the founder, Guru Shakyamuni Buddha and remembering the qualities of his holy body, 
speech and mind. In this way,  we cultivate and generate faith in and  respect for the Buddha.  
 
It is important that this happens while reciting these prayers. When we can do this well, even 
for a short time while reciting these prayers, we will be able to accumulate an immeasurable 
amount of merit very easily. This is some thing we should understand and remember and 
therefore, we should recite these prayers as well as we can.  
 
Do not commit any non- virtuous actions,  
Perform only perfect virtuous actions,  
Subdue your mind thoroughly –  
This is the teaching of the Buddha.  
 A star, a visual aberration, a flame of a lamp,  
An illusion, a drop of dew, a bubble,  
A dream, a flash of lightning, a cloud –   
See conditioned things as such!  
 
These two verses are the essence of the Buddha’s teachings. Relating the first  verse to the paths  of the persons of the three capacities, “ Do not commit any non- virtuous actions”  shows 
the path of the persons of small capacity and “ Perform only perfect virtuous actions ” shows the 
path of the persons of middling capacity. Here, the word “ perfect ” refer s to virtues that are the 
causes of liberation, that is, virtues created that are conjoined with the motivation of renunciation.  
 “Subdue your mind thoroughly ” indicates the path of the persons of great capacity and refers 
to purifying the mind of the afflictions together with their predispositions, i.e., eliminating the two obstructions which will subdue the mind thoroughly. This line can relate to both the causal 
perfection vehicle (Mahayan Sutra) and the resultant vajra vehicle (Tantra).  
 
Through these  three lines of this verse, we can understand all the paths of the persons of small, 
middling and great capacities. They also include both the Mahayana Sutra and Tantra. All of 
these are the teachings of the Buddha.  
 
 We will discuss the meaning of the nex t verse in the future. That verse indicates the practice of 
emptiness and the reflection on impermanence. One then concludes with the dedication 
prayer that starts, “ Through these merits may sentient beings attain the rank of all seeing…. ”  
 According to t radition, a mandala offering is made to request for the teachings as, in general, 
teachings are only granted upon request. Particularly here, in order to receive Mahayana
