This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
teachings, especially the Tantric teachings, the teachings can only be granted upon m aking 
request three times. At the time of making the mandala offering, one can visualise offering the 
mandala to Guru Shakyamuni Buddha. If you are able to do this, you will be able to accumulate an immeasurable amount of merit.  
 The point here is to encourage you so that whenever you recite such prayers or when you are making mandala offerings, you do so mindfully while reflecting on their meaning. Then, they 
will be very beneficial.   
 
*****************  
We have been looking at the recitation of mantra  without deterioration of the four branches. 
The first branch is the self basis where one generates oneself as the deity through the process 
of the six deities. This process is the same in both Action and Performance Tantra. When one is doing the approxima tion of the deity, one performs the approximation by doing the self 
generation practice by means of the six deities. Remembering this point would be beneficial. When we can understand how the practice of self generation is done by means of the six deities,  then whatever practice text on Action Tantra we take up, we would be able to 
understand it fairly easily because it is all there in the text.  
 The last deity of the practice of self generation that is done by means of the six deities is the sign deity. H aving generated oneself completely as the deity, one then generates clear 
appearance of oneself as the deity and, at the same time, cultivates the divine pride of oneself as the deity. When we say we perform an approximation of deity, it is through this cl ear 
appearance and divine pride that we approximate (become closer to) the deity.  
 One cultivates the divine pride and clear appearance of oneself as the deity through combining it with retaining the life -force exertion, i.e., by restraining the wind from  leaving the body 
through the hair pores and so forth and  cutting off all discursive thoughts. We should try to achieve such a clear appearance of ourselves as the deity that it feels like we are ‘seeing’ it directly with our mental eyes, so to speak, and  that we are able to touch it.  
 Cultivating the clear appearance and divine pride of oneself as the deity is the main practice during the approximation of the deity. When we are exhausted from the practice, we can rest by reciting the mantra of the deity.   
  
The next branch is the other basis  which refers to the front generation. We visualise the 
inestimable mansion in the space in front of us. (This is often translated as the celestial mansion but the correct translation is inestimable mansion).  
Inside t he mansion, one generates the commitment (or samaya) being and then invokes the 
wisdom beings which enter and dissolve into the commitment being. If the practice of generating the commitment being is not in the text, then one can proceed directly to the invocation of the wisdom beings to take their places inside the mansion and one then makes 
offerings and praises to them. This practice is usually found in the relevant texts. Many of you have done the Nyung -na practice so you should remember the practice of  the front generation 
that is done in that practice. One then make offerings and praises to the front generation deity with the practice of the seven limbs and so forth.  
 
Lesson  5                           P age 28 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
The next branch is the mind basis which refers to visualising a moon disc at the hear t of oneself 
as the deity and at the heart of the front generation deity. On those moon discs, we place the mantra  garland that we are going to recite. The letters of the mantra on the moon discs is the 
sound basis .  
 On page 4 of the root text, it says, “One recites mantra without deterioration of the four 
branches” .  The recitation of the mantra is done with all the four branches:  
1) the self basis, where we generate ourselves as the deity;  
2) the other basis, where the front generation of the deity is done;   
3) the mind basis, where in the hearts of ourselves as the deity and the front generation deity, 
we visualise a moon disc; and  
4) the sound basis, where on both of these moon discs, we visualise the letters of the mantra that we are going to recite.  
 Here, we  are talking about the way to recite the mantra through the four branches. It is not 
necessary that all mantra recitations have to be done in this way. But because we are 
discussing the recitation of mantra through the four branches, we need to know what t he four 
branches are.  
 There are two ways of performing the recitation (or repetition) of the mantra:  
1) Reciting the mantra by observing the form of the letters of the mantra.  
1. Recitation observing the form of the letters at the heart of the deity in front and 2. Recitation observing the form of the letters at one’s own heart  
2) Reciting the mantra by observing the sound of the mantra.  
 When we say recitation observing the form of the letters, what exactly is the letter? Is the letter the sound or not?   Geshela: What do you think? What is a letter? What is ‘A’? Is ‘A’ shape?. The sound ‘A,’ is that ‘A’?  When you write ‘A’ like that, is that ‘A’ or is that the sound ‘A’? When you say form, there 
are many different kinds of form….Seems like you still have n’t realised ‘A’, isn’t it? I also don’t 
know what is ‘A’.  
 When impermanent phenomena are divided, there is form (or matter). When form is divided, 
there are five: visual, sound, smell, taste, touch. These five are all forms.  
 
RECITING THE MANTRA BY OBSE RVING THE FORM OF TH E LETTERS OF THE MAN TRA 
Recitation observing the form of the letters at the heart of the deity in front  
During this recitation, we focus on the front generation deity in the heart of which  there is a moon disc. On that moon disc are th e letters of the mantra. By  observing these three together, 
we then recite the mantra.  
 There are two ways in which the mantra can be recited: (1) mental repetition, i.e., silently or (2) whispered repetition, i.e., softly with sound. The main purpose for  mantra repetition through 
the four branches is to develop single -pointed concentration. To develop concentration, we 
 
Lesson  5                           P age 29 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
need to restrain the life -force exertion. Here we do the mental repetition  by observing the 
form of the letters. We do so through reciting  the mantra mentally and combining it with the 
life-force exertion and retention to stop the movement of the wind.  
 
With respect to the wind in the body, there is the wind in the upper and lower parts of the body. We seek to control these winds by gatheri ng them at the navel and then making them 
remain there as long as possible. Whilst  retaining the winds at the navel, one focuses on the 
front generation deity together with the moon disc at its heart together with the letters of the 
mantra on the moon dis c. By observing these three while retaining the winds at the navel, one 
performs the mental repetition.  
 In Tantric teachings, the wind (or subtle energy of the body) is the mount of the consciousness, meaning the consciousness is the rider and the wind is  like the horse. If we are unable to 
control and stop the movement of this subtle energy, then it is very difficult to stop discursive thoughts and distractions from arising. That is why we have to do this practice of life -force 
exertion and retention. In the beginning, one is only able to retain the wind for a short time but with practice, this duration can be extended. This can be achieved slowly and through such practice, one would be able to develop single -pointed concentration. When the repetition is 
done through restraining the life -force exertion, there is no room for whispered repetition. 
One can only perform the mental repetition.  
 
RECITING THE MANTRA BY OBSERVING THE FORM OF THE LETTERS OF THE MANTRA  
Recitation observing the form of the letters inside one’s heart.  
When we do this recitation in connection with the four branches, we go through the process 
described earlier.   
 When we inhale, we visualise that the moon disc with the letters of the mantra in the heart of the front generation deity comes into our heart with our breath. We then stop breathing and hold our breath. While doing this, we observe the moon disc and the l etters of the mantra and 
perform the mental recitation. The moon disc and the letters of the mantra are the two objects of observation.  
 When we are unable to hold our breath any longer, we exhale and visualise that the moon disc together with the letters  of the mantra at one’s own heart go back to the heart of the front 
generation deity. Then the process is repeated.  
   RECITING THE MANTRA BY OBSERVING THE SOU ND OF THE MANTRA  
Here, the objects of observation are not the deity’s body, the moon disc or the letters of the mantra. Rather the object of observation in this practice is the sound of the mantra which we observe as we perform the mental repetition.   
  
When we perform th is repetition observing the sound of the mantra, we are reciting the 
mantra mentally in our mind. We visualise the sound of the mantra resonating when we recite the mantra mentally whilst restraining the wind.

Three ways of  
reciting the mantra  Objects o f observation

MANTRA:

of the letters at the heart of the front

letters of the mantra on it  
Reciting the mantra by  observing the form 
of the letters at one’s own heart  The moon disc and the letters of the mantra on 
it (Note: we do not focus on ourselves as the 
deity)  
 
RECITING THE MANTRA BY OBSERVING 
THE SOUND OF THE MAN TRA  
The sound of the mantra  
The object of observation becomes progressively subtler and this helps in developing single -
pointed concentration.  
 
Summary  
When we perform the recitation of mantra through the four branches:  
1. First we generate ourselves as the de ity by means of the six deities. That is the self 
basis.  
2. After that, we visualise the deity in front of us, i.e., the front generation deity. That is 
the other basis .  
3. Once that is done, we visualise a moon disc at the heart of ourselves as the deity and 
the heart of the front generation deity. That is the mind basis.  
4. When that is done, we visualise the mantra garland on the moon disc at one’s heart and the heart of the front generation deity. That is the sound basis.  
When these four branches are complete d, then we perform the repetition of mantra:  
1. First, we repeat the mantra by observing the form of the letters at the heart of the front generation deity.   
2. When that is done well, we repeat the mantra by observing the form of the letters at our own heart.  
3. Only when that is done well, then we move on to the repetition observing the sound of the mantra.  
The latter practices should only be done when the earlier ones are performed well.  
 Between the whispered and mental recitations, we should first perform th e whispered 
recitation followed by the mental recitation. When reciting the mantra, we should avoid the 
following faults:  1. one should not recite the mantra too quickly  
2. one should not recite the mantra too slowly  
3. one should not recite the mantra loudly  
4. one should not recite the mantra so softly that you yourself cannot hear what you are 
 
Lesson  5                           P age 31 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
reciting  
5. one should not recite the mantra while talking  
6. the mantra should not be recited when the mind is distracted  
7. one should recite the mantra by pronouncing it cle arly.  
We should avoid these faults while reciting the mantra which are mentioned in The Tantra 
Requested by Subahu.  
 
The Susiddhi Tantra mentions the number of recitations that are required when one is 
performing the approximation of the deity.  
• For mant ras with 15 syllables or less, each syllable has to be recited 100,000 times. For 
example, if the mantra consists of ten syllables, one would have to recite 10 x 100,000 
mantras, i.e., one million recitations.  In the approximation of Tara whose mantra has  10 
syllables, we have to recite 1 million Tara mantras. If we are performing the approximation of Chenrezig and using the six -syllable mantra, OM MANI PADME HUM, then we have to do 
600,000 mantras.  
• For mantras with 16 to 32 syllables, the mantra has to b e recited 300,000 times.   
• For mantras with more than 32 syllables, e.g., for the approximation of Sangye Migtukpa, 
10,000 recitations can be counted as an approximation.  
 Action Tantra emphasises external activities especially cleanliness. So, if during the course of 
the mantra recitation one falls asleep, sneezes, yawns or breaks wind, then one would have to stop the recitation and one cannot count the mantras that one has accumulated. One would 
have to perform the ritual of blessing and cleansing onesel f. After that is done, one can then 
start the recitation again. At the end of the session, with the mudra of the vase, one offers the 
root virtues accumulated from the recitation to the deity. One leaves one’s rosary in front of 
the deity without bringing it elsewhere and stops the session.  
 Question:  
Why do we visualise the moon and not the sun disc at the heart?  
Answer :  In the mind basis, we visualise the moon disc whether it is in the heart of the deity in 
front or oneself as the deity. The entity of the moon disc is  one’s mind which we visualise in the form of a moon disc.   In certain texts, the deity has three seats: the lotus, moon and sun discs. In that context, the lotus symbolises renunciation, the moon disc symbolises bodhichitta and the sun dis c 
symbolises the wisdom realising emptiness. This is to make us understand that the deity who is 
seated on these three seats has brought to culmination these three realisations, i.e., the deity 
has completely realised renunciation, bodhichitta and the wisdom realizing emptiness.  
Question: When is the whispered recitation done?  
Answer : Whispered recitation is done in all the forms of repetition that we have discussed so 
far. We have been discussing the different forms of recitation mainly from the perspective of mental recitation. Actually, whispered recitation should be done first before the mental recitation.  
 There is the whispered repetition observing the form of the letters at the heart of the deity in front, observing the form of letters at one’s heart and observing the sound of the mantra.  
 
Lesson  5                           P age 32 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
Question:  With regards to the number of mantras that have to be accumulated in retreat, do 
we divide them between whispered and mental recitations?  
Answer : The counting is done on the basis of the whispered recitations. We do not count the 
mental recitations. The main purpose of doing the men tal recitations is to develop single -
pointed concentration.

LESSON NO: 6     DATE: 5TH MARCH 2009  
 
A star, a visual aberration, a flame of a lamp,  
An illusion, a drop of dew, or a bubbl e, 
A dream, a flash of lightning, a cloud –  
See conditioned things as such!  
 
“A star ”: We should reflect on its nature which is the collection of both  appearance and 
emptiness. We can validly posit that the two truths exist in all phenomena. On the basis of any given phenomenon, there is an aspect of it that appears to the mind which is not polluted by 
grasping at true existence and at the same time, there is another aspect of that same object 
that appears to the mind that is polluted by grasping at true existence.  
 When we look at an object, e.g., a vase, how does it appear?  When a person looks at the vase, 
the vase can appear in a particular way to that person’s consciousness that is not affected by 
grasping at true existence, i.e., that consciousness is uncontaminated.  The same vase can appear differently to a consciousness that is affected by grasping at true existence, that is, a 
contaminated mind. So, one mind is affected by ignorance and the other is not.  When these 
two minds look at the same vase, the vase appears differently to them. Do these two ways of 
appearance exist in rea lity? Yes.  
 
According to the Consequence Middle Way School (CMWS), the uncontaminated wisdom or the wisdom of meditative equipoise is the wisdom that directly perceives emptiness. When this wisdom directly realising the emptiness of the vase looks at the vase, only the emptiness of that vase appears to that wisdom. That emptiness of the vase is the ultimate truth.  
 The star is used to show how an uncontaminated mind or the wisdom that directly perceives emptiness perceives its object. The star is always t here in the sky but during daylight, when the 
sun is shining brightly, we do not see it.   But that does not mean the star is not there. The star does exist. In the same way, when the wisdom that directly perceives emptiness (the wisdom of  meditative equi poise) sees only emptiness, conventionalities do not appear to that wisdom. 
What appears to that wisdom is none other than the emptiness of the object of negation, 
which is a non -affirming negation. Conventionalities  do not appear to the perspective of th e 
wisdom that directly realises emptiness. Only emptiness appears and this is the ultimate truth . 
 
When the sun sets, all the stars shine brightly. This is like conventionality appearing to the conventional mind. All phenomena appear to this conventional m ind that is polluted by 
ignorance. The conventional mind (which is a mistaken mind) is polluted by ignorance but it is a valid cognition. The phenomena that are posited by conventional valid cognisers are called the conventional truth.   The two truths (ul timate truth and conventional truth) exist together on any given 
phenomenon. The ultimate truth is the phenomenon that appears to the wisdom that directly perceives emptiness. The conventional truth is the phenomenon that appears to the 
 
Lesson  6                           Page 34 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
conventional valid cogniser. Depending on which mind is looking at the particular phenomenon, 
either ultimate truth or conventional truth is perceived. Both the vase and the emptiness of the vase are of one entity. You will not be able to posit a vase that is separate from t he vase’s 
emptiness nor can you posit an emptiness of the vase that is separate from the vase. In the same way, when we reflect on the meaning of the star, it shows us how in any given phenomenon, both appearance (conventional truth) and emptiness (ultimat e truth) exist 
together as one collection.  
 Answer to question: According to the CMWS, except for the wisdom that directly realises emptiness, all other consciousnesses in the continua of sentient beings are necessarily mistaken. The consciousness that po sits conventional truth is the conventional valid cogniser.  
 
***************  
We have completed the explanation of four -branched repetition. Now I will give you a 
summary so that you can reflect on this in an easy way. Please try to keep this process in yo ur 
head when we go through the steps. Thinking and analysing are very important, especially in tantric practice where you need to use your mind for thinking, reflection and for doing retreat. Once you are clear about the process, it would be easier for you to practise it. A lot of people 
ask, “What should I think about or visualise when I am doing such a practice?” What we have discussed so far is what should be done.  
 
In the four branches of repetition, there are:  
1) the self basis  
2) the other basis  
3) the mind basis  
4) the sound basis  
 
1) The self basis  refers to how to one generates oneself as the deity.  
a. At the beginning of generating oneself as a deity, we have to reflect on the meaning of 
emptiness as clearly as we can. We meditate on how oneself as the meditator and the object that is being meditated upon, the deity, are both equal in the sense of not e xisting inherently.  
b. The mind that ascertains the meaning of emptiness is transformed into a moon disc. The 
entity of this moon disc is one’s mind in the aspect of a moon disc.  
c. We then visualise in the space above the moon disc, say, if we are doing the  Chenrezig 
practice, the sound of the mantra, Om Mani Padme Hum reverberating in the space. The 
entity of this sound is the deity Chenrezig in the aspect of the sound of the mantra resonating 
in space.  
d. That sound then settles on the moon disc in the form of the letters of the mantra in the 
relevant colour, e.g. gold.  
e. We now visualise light rays emitting from the letters of the mantra touching and purifying 
sentient beings of their negativities and obscurations.  
f. We also visualise some of these light rays making offerings to all the buddhas and 
bodhisattvas.  
g. All the light rays that were emitted now gather back into the letters of the mantra.  
h. The letters of the mantras together with the moon disc then completely transform into 
oneself as the deity Chenrezig with one face and four arms.  
 
Lesson  6                           Page 35 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
i. After one is transformed into the deity, one blesses oneself with the respective mudra of the 
lineage, touching the five points –  the heart, the space between the eyebrows, the throat, the 
right shoulder and left shoulder. One  then thinks, “Now I have become the actual deity, 
Chenrezig.”  
j. Then one focuses on the appearance of oneself as the deity as clearly as possible.  
Now go through the whole process in your mind –  what is the first thing to be done, what 
follows after that a nd so forth. This is how one performs the self generation.  
 
2) The  other basis  refers to how we generate the deity in front. We will not go into the details 
here as they are very clearly indicated in the practice text.  
• In the beginning, we generate the ma ndala in front.  
• in the centre of the mandala, we generate the deity using the same process as that 
explained for self -generation.  
• When the deity is generated in the centre of the mandala, one then thinks that the actual 
deity is residing there.   
 3) The  mind basis  refers to visualising a moon disc at the heart of ourselves as a deity.  
 4) The sound basis  refers to visualising the letters of the mantra on the moon disc. We also 
visualise at the heart of the deity in front, there is a moon disc and on tha t moon disc, there are 
the letters of the mantra.  
 
Next we do the repetition of the mantra.  
● First, we focus on the front generation deity. At the heart of that deity is a   moon disc and on 
that moon disc are the letters of the mantra to be recited. These are the three objects of 
observation. We focus on these three objects  together – the front  generation of the deity, the 
moon disc at its heart and the letters of the mantra on the moon disc.  
• If we are doing the whispered repetition, we recite the mantra softly while focusing on 
these three objects and the recitation single -pointedly and without distraction.  
• When that recitation is stable and done well, we switch to the mental recitation which is 
done in silence. While focusing on those three objects, we perform the mental recitation while restraining the life -force exertion by holding the wind at the navel. We perform the 
mental recitation for as long as we are able to hold the wind.  
• The next practice becomes subtler and is done to help us to develop single -pointed 
concentration: We focus on the moon disc and the letters of the mantra at the he art of the 
front generation deity. When we inhale, we visualise the moon disc and the letters of the 
mantra at the heart of the front generation deity entering us together with the breath. Both 
the moon disc and the letters of the mantra settle at our hear t.  
• Then one focuses the mind single -pointedly on that while holding the wind and performs 
the mental recitation.  
• This next practice is even subtler: The object of observation here is only the sound of the 
mantra at one’s heart. We don’t even think of the  moon disc and the letters of the mantra.  
• While focusing on the sound of the mantra at one’s heart, we should do whispered 
repetition.  
• When that is done well, then we switch to mental recitation while holding the wind.  
 
Lesson  6                           Page 36 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
What I have just described is ano ther way of explaining  what we have been discussing so far. 
When we just listen to all this information, we may get confused and mix everything up. So it is important to think through the whole process to make it clear in our minds.  
 If you have done the Chenrezig practice and look at the Nyung -na sadhana, a lot of what we 
have discussed appear explicitly in there. Our greatest fault is that whether we are doing 
retreat or our daily prayers, we just look at and recite the words quickly without taking the 
time to reflect on them.  If we did reflect on them, then we would definitely benefit because 
we would experience the flavour of the practice and our interest in the practice would grow. Then, naturally, we would want to do the practice and not feel that do ing our daily prayers is a 
burden.  
 This method of recitation of mantra together with the four branches is primarily aimed at developing single -pointed concentration, especially calm abiding. It is clearly mentioned in all 
the texts that the development of calm abiding is extremely important. We need the 
realisation of calm abiding in order to realise emptiness. We free ourselves from samsara through the union of calm abiding and special insight realising emptiness.  
 At the end of the session, how do we stop the repetition? During the practice, we had generated the deity in front, the moon disc at its heart together with letters of the mantra and 
the sound of the mantra reverberating from those  letters. What follows is the dissolution 
process using the exa mple of the Chenrezig practice:  
•  In the centre of the mandala is Chenrezig  
• At the heart of Chenrezig is the moon disc together with the letters of the mantra Om Mani 
Padme Hum.  
• The sound of the mantra is reverberating.  
• The sound of the mantra dissolves i nto the letters of the mantra  
• The letters of the mantra dissolves into the moon disc  
• The moon disc dissolves into the body of the front generation deity.    
• We now focus on the body of the front generation deity.  
• We then absorb the front generation Chenrezig into ourselves. There is no longer any front 
generation deity.  
• What is left now is oneself as the deity. One’s body dissolves into the letters of the mantra 
at one’s heart  
• The letters of the mantras at one’s heart dissolves and becomes the entity of the sound of 
the mantra reverberating at the level of one’s heart.  
• One focuses on the emptiness of that sound and think that it does not exist inherently. 
What appears is emptiness.  
•  ● Within the state of emptine ss focusing on the emptiness of the sound of the   mantras, 
one arises as the deity. It is also said that, within the state of emptiness, one can arise as one’s normal self. The deity or oneself is like an illusion.  
•  
There are two (types of absorption):

1 Absorption with recitation  
2 Absorption not reliant on recitation  
We have now completed ‘ Absorption with recitation.’   
 
Answer to question:  It will be ideal if you can visualise the letters in Tibetan since it is meant as 
a medium for the transmission o f the teachings of the Buddha. It does make a difference as 
there is a continuation of the blessings (when we visualise in Tibetan).  However, Geshe -la does 
not see any reason to suggest that it would be inappropriate to visualise the letters in English.  
 
Answer to question:  The concept of the central channel only appears in Highest Yoga Tantra. 
The wind is to be held at the navel but it is difficult to talk about how to hold the wind at the 
navel and the duration of doing so.  
 
Question: Does the mental re petition while observing the sound of the mantra immediately 
precede the attainment of calm abiding?  
Answer:  No. There is still the concentration (absorption) of abiding on fire and the 
concentration of abiding on sound which come under the concentration w ithout recitation. 
When the concentration of abiding on sound is brought to culmination, then that is when calm 
abiding is achieved.  
 
Points regarding translation:  
• In the Tibetan text, “up to” means the concentration of abiding on sound have to be 
included. On page 4 of the root text (page 4): “ One establishes calm -abiding prior to  the 
absorption that abides on sound, and from the limit of sound one meditates on insight” . If the 
English translation seems to exclude the absorption that abides on sound, then that is incorrect because it should include  the concentration abiding on sound. When that is brought 
to culmination, then one achieves calm abiding. For example, when w e say from the eighth 
ground and below, that means it includes the eighth ground. Similarly, “prior to the absorption that abides on sound,” should mean it includes the absorption that abides on sound.  
• Fourth paragraph on page 3 of the root text :  “This is understood from the quotation of the 
well accomplished tantras in the Great Stages of Mantra. ” The correct translation should be: 
“This is understood from the quotation of The Susiddhi Tantra (that is quoted) in the Great 
Stages of Mantra’.  
• “Absorption ” can also be translated as “concentration.” Therefore “ Absorption not reliant on 
recitation” can also be understood to be “Concentration without recitation.”  
 1.1.1.3.2  ABSORPTION NOT RELIA NT ON RECITATION  
 (One) meditates on:  
1. Absorption that abides on fire 
2. Absorption that abides on sound  
3. Absorption that bestows emancipation at the limit of sound  
 When the concentration with recitation is performed well, one moves on to concentration without recitation.  
 
Lesson  6                           Page 38 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
1. Absorption that abides on fire  
One generates oneself in the aspect of the deity. Then one visualises at one’s heart a small tongue of flame and inside that flame is the sound of the mantra reverberating. In entity, that aspect of sound is one’s mind that understands emptiness. In this a bsorption abiding on fire, 
one does not perform any kind of repetition, neither whispered nor mental. One focuses solely on the tongue of flame together with the sound of the mantra reverberating whilst holding the 
wind.  
 
2. Absorption that abides on soun d  
One visualises oneself as the deity and a small moon disc at one’s heart. Inside that moon disc is a smaller version of the deity. The moon disc does not need to always be visualised as lying down flat. Here, the deity is inside the moon disc so we can visualise the moon disc upright like an orb of light. Inside the heart of that smaller deity is a tongue of flame. Inside that tongue of flame is the sound of the mantra reverberating. We then focus single -pointedly on the sound of 
the mantra inside the to ngue of flame at the heart of the smaller deity.   
 In the above concentrations abiding on fire and on sound, the manner of focussing on the sound of the mantra is not the same as the earlier practice of the repetition observing the sound of the mantra. The earlier practice falls under “absorption with recitation,” be it whispered or mental. In the latter practices, we focus on the sound as if we are listening to the 
sound. We are not whispering or reciting it mentally with our own voices.  
 When the concentration abiding on sound is fully completed, that is when we develop calm 
abiding. What is the measure of having achieved calm abiding?  The measure of having achieved calm abiding is when we are able to focus our minds single -pointedly on a chosen 
object for one -sixth of a day, i.e., four  hours. Then that would mean we have attained calm 
abiding.  
 Answer to question: It all depends on the level and the realisations of the individual. If one hasn’t achieved the earlier realisations, it is impossible to  meditate on the later steps. For example, depending on one’s state of mind, if one is able to meditate on bodhicitta then one can start immediately with that meditation without having to go through the paths of the small and medium capacities.  It depends o n where one is. If you know how to read, you don’t have 
to start again from the alphabet.  
 Answer to question: Won’t one face the same problem if one chooses the deity’s body as the object of observation for the development of calm abiding, with its head, hair, eyes, hands and so forth? When you have accomplished calm abiding with the deity’s body as the object, ho w 
does that deity’s body appear to you? Do the different body parts appear to you sequentially or does every part of the deity’s body appears to you at one go?  
 It is the same with using the sound of the mantra as the object of observation for developing calm abiding. It is mentioned in the texts that all the letters of the mantra appear at one time. When we develop calm abiding with respect to the mandala with the 32 deities of Guhyasamaja, all the deities appear simultaneously, not separately. I think th at is how the 
 
Lesson  6                           Page 39 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
sound of the mantra will appear to the mind that has achieved calm abiding when focusing on 
the mantra. I remember seeing this in one text.

FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
LESSON NO: 7     DATE: 17TH MARCH 2009  
 
Before the beginning of class, we recite prayers of homage to the Buddha and the Three Jewels. 
I would like to talk a little bit about the benefits of doing such prayers.  
 
Imagine that you practise charity by making offerings of divine garme nts and food, equal to the 
number of grains of sands in the Ganges’ River, to each and everyone of the numberless sentient beings every day for an eon. We will probably agree that the merit accumulated by 
doing this will be unbelievable. Yet, if we offer a single meal  to a lay person living in the lay 
vows, the merit from that will be much greater.  
 
Furthermore, that merit will be much greater, at each level, if we make the same offering of a 
meal to a fully ordained monk (i.e., offering to a gelong generates more merit than offering to 
the lay person), a stream -enterer, a once -returner, a non -returne r up to a solitary realiser arhat.   
 Now imagine that we offer divine garments and food to a group of solitary realiser arhats equal in numbers to the particles in the ten directions everyday for eons. The merit accumulated 
would be even greater. But if w e compare those merits to the benefit of just hearing the name, 
“Buddha” or by seeing a painted image or statue of a buddha, there is more merit generated by those latter actions. It goes without saying then that the merit that we accumulate from 
putting o ur palms together and reciting verses of praise to the Buddha or making offerings to 
him when we see his holy form will be greater still.  
 All the physical and the verbal actions that we perform in relation to the Buddha such as seeing his representation,  reciting praises, making prostrations and offerings to him generate an 
immense amount of merit. But these are only physical and verbal actions. The mental actions done in relation to the Buddha,  such as generating single -pointed faith in him, carry even more 
merit.  
 Imagine that we fill all the world systems of the ten directions with precious jewels and offer all these to the buddhas equal in number to the grains of sand in the Ganges River for an eon. 
Even these merits are less than the merit of   gene rating single -pointed faith in the Buddha. 
This means that the virtuous activities of the mind in relation to the Buddha bring about much more powerful virtues.  
 
 In the same way, based on this line of reasoning, due to our faith and belief in the Buddha,  
when we study and analyse his teachings, we can understand the benefit and the amount of merit we accumulate.  
 
When we understand the merit we can accumulate in relation to the Buddha through our 
physical, verbal and mental actions, then it makes a differ ence whenever we make offerings 
and recite these prayers and verses of homage to the Buddha and the  Three Jewels. When we understand the benefit, we will be enthusiastic and will naturally want to engage in such activities.  
 
Lesson  7                           Page 41 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
What is the benefit of hearin g or reciting the name of the Buddha just once? It is said that that 
has  immense benefit particularly when we can develop single -pointed faith in him. The merit 
accumulated will be very great. The more merit one accumulates, at the same time, the more obscurations one purifies.  
 One should then understand how by just coming to class, one could receive so many benefits 
simply by reciting these prayers at the beginning of class. The benefits are really so 
inconceivable that they cannot possibly fit our minds!  
 I have mentioned before that the benefits we get from seeing a representation of the holy body of the Buddha, be it a painting, photo or statue is equal to the benefit of coming into contact with the Buddha’s teachings. However, the Buddha’s speech is said to be more precious than his holy body. If we can study the teachings of the Buddha and then develop some conviction or faith in his teachings, the merit we accumulate will be much greater.  
 Therefore, it is important for us to remember the benefits we will get from each and every single word of the teachings that we read. When we understand  the benefits, we can also understand how extremely fortunate we are for having such an opportunity and how we have fulfilled the purpose of our taking a human re birth. Then, naturally, we will feel happy.  
 
Sometimes when we hear such explanation of the benefits, we may think, “Can  it be true? 
How is that possible?” When that happens, when we over -analyse things, we will not be able to 
develop faith and that is no t good. In general, critical analysis is very important but too much 
analysis can become an obstacle that prevents us from developing faith.  
 It does not mean that someone who is intelligent and has good analytical abilities will necessarily receive such benefits because he may not necessarily have faith. Another person who is less intelligent, who does not analyse too much but because of his faith, he engages in such virtuous practices, that person will receive all the benefits of those practices. So too much thinking sometimes can obstruct the generation of faith.  
 I thought it will be beneficial for you to understand the benefits of reciting these prayers that we do before class. During the recitation, it will be very good if you are able to remember to develop faith in the Buddha by remembering the qualities of his holy body, speech and mind.  
 1.1.1.3.2  ABSORPTION NOT RELIANT ON RECITATION cont’d  
 (One) meditates on:  
1. Absorption that abides on fire  
2. Absorption that abides on sound  
3. Absorption that bestows emancipation at the limit of sound  
 One establishes calm- abiding prior to the absorption that abides on sound, and from the limit 
of sound one meditates on insight. If there is too much analysis by analytical wisdom, the prior 
 
Lesson  7                           Page 42 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
factor of abidance is destroyed. Due to that, one attains the union of calm -abiding and insight 
comprehending emptiness by combining analysis and placement.  
 
“One establishes calm -abiding prior to the absorption that abides on sound”: The English 
translation may give you the idea that calm abiding is established before  the concentration 
abiding on sound. That is incorrect.  Calm abiding is established when one has actualised the concentration by abiding on sound.  
 “And from the limit  (or end)  of sound one meditates on insight,” means we engage in the last 
concentration to develop special insight. We achieve calm abiding by actualising the absorptions that abide on fire and sound. But those realisations alone cannot liberate us from samsa ra. That is why we need the third concentration, the absorption that bestows 
emancipation at the limit of sound (also translated as ‘the concentration bestowing liberation at the end of sound.’) “ Emancipation” here refers to liberating ourselves from the t wo 
obstructions to achieving the truth body (dharmakaya). In order to do this, we need the realisation of special insight, to realise emptiness. That is why we need the third concentration: “the absorption that bestows emancipation at the limit of sound.”  
 “If there is too much analysis by analytical wisdom, the prior factor of abidance is destroyed. Due to that, one attains the union of calm -abiding and (special)  insight comprehending 
emptiness by combining analysis and placement”:  The teachings on emptine ss, such as the 
scriptures on the Middle Way, indicate we first need to identify correctly the objects of negation, the self of persons and the self of phenomena. We then have to understand how all phenomena do not exist inherently and are not ultimately e stablished. How do we do that? We 
arrive at that conclusion -  that phenomena are not inherently existent –  through analysis and 
we meditate on that conclusion.  However when there is too much analysis, the mind will move away from the object of observation . Faults such as distraction and excitement will then arise.  
 It is through analysis that one arrives at the ascertainment of the meaning of emptiness. When that ascertainment is achieved, we place our minds single - pointedly on the meaning of 
emptiness that we have ascertained. However, if the factor of calm abiding of that placement meditation becomes too strong,  that is a fault because the wisdom of individual analysis that analyses the meaning of emptiness weakens. At such a time,  there is the danger of laxity and dullness arising in the mind.  
 
It is mentioned in the short edition of The Stages of Meditation that when the special insight 
focussing on emptiness becomes too strong, the factor of calm abiding will weaken. When this 
happens, it is likened  to putting a butter lamp in the wind. The mind becomes disturbed and 
will move from the object and when this happens, one no longer sees emptiness clearly. So, when special insight becomes too strong, one needs to cultivate calm abiding. On the other hand , when calm abiding becomes too strong, one is likened to a person who is sleeping. 
Again, one will not be able to see emptiness clearly. At that time, one needs to meditate on special insight.  
 Faults will arise when either calm abiding or special insigh t is stronger than the other. 
Therefore, these two factors should be meditated upon equally, like ploughing the land with 
 
Lesson  7                           Page 43 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
two oxen that are tied together so that they can move together. This is the meaning of, “ Due to 
that, one attains the union of calm -abiding and insight comprehending emptiness by combining 
analysis and placement.”   
 
When one obtains insight comprehending emptiness, one transfers to the Path of Preparation and when one perceptually comprehends emptiness, one transfers to the Path of Seeing and so on, similar to the Perfection Vehicle.  
 However if one seeks to accomplish the limitless feats of special siddhis as stated in the Action and Performance Tantras, one needs as pre -requisites the four branches of recitation and the 
absorptions of just those three. The Great Stages of Mantra states that to establish many minor siddhis it is not definite that those are required.  (Pg. 4 of the root text )  
 “The four branches of recitation” should be understood as one noun phrase by itself. “The 
absorptions of just those three”  refers to the three concentrations (i.e., the concentrations of 
abiding in fire and sound and the concentration bestowing liberation at the end of sound). Collectively, these three concentrations are known as the concentrations of the three principles.  
 If we want to actualise the limitless feats of special attainment (the siddhis) in Action Tantra and Performance Tantra, this must be preceded by the concentration with repetition and the 
concentration without repetition.  
 
Question: What are “ the limitless feats of special siddhis”?  
Answer : Siddhis is the Sanskrit word for attainments.  
 The entire path of Action Tantra can be condensed into three forms of meditation: - 
1) Meditation on the deity’s body (Mahamudra) to actualise the Buddha’s holy body  
2) Meditation on the deity’s speech (Mantra) to actualise the Buddha’s holy speech  
3) Meditation on the deity’s mind (Suchness) to actualise the Buddha’s holy mind  
 
● Mahamudra is basically the meditation on the six deities.  
 ● The meditation on the deity’s speech (Mantra) is the concentration with repetition (i.e., the 
repetition observing the form of the letters and the repetition observing the sound of the 
mantra) but this is not considered to be the main practice. The main practice for the 
meditation on the deity’s speech (Mantra) is:  
1) The concentration abiding in fire  
2) The concentration abiding in sound  
 ● The concentration bestowing liberation at the end of sound is meditated upon to achieve the 
Buddha’s mind, the dharmakaya.  
 The concentration focusing on the deity’s body is considered to be a coarse concentration. The meditation on the deity’s speech is considered to be subtler while the concentration in the meditation on the deity’s mind is considered to be even subtler tha n that.  
 
Lesson  7                           Page 44 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
With regards to the concentrations with repetition, mental recitation is considered to be subtler than whispered recitation, which is considered  coarse. But the concentrations without repetition is considered to be even subtler than those two.  
 The meditation on the deity’s body is also known as deity yoga . The meditation on the deity’s 
speech is known as mantra yoga which refers  to all the different forms of repetition up to the 
concentration of abiding in sound. These two yogas are classified  as yoga with signs . The 
practice of deity yoga and mantra yoga are done in conjunction with the mind realising emptiness and are the principal practices of yoga with signs.   The concentration bestowing liberation at the end of sound comes under yoga with out signs . 
At the time of this concentration, there is the factor of appearance (which is the meditation on the deity’s body) and the factor of meditation on emptiness. In this context, the meditation on emptiness is considered to be more important so this   concentration is referred to as the yoga 
without signs.  
 Many masters hold different views with regards to the meaning of yoga with signs and yoga without signs. Where are the boundaries between the two? Some classify the yoga with signs as the yoga that is not conjoined with the meditation on emptiness and the yoga without signs 
as the yoga that is conjoined with the meditation on emptiness. This is one of the examples of 
the different views.  
 
1.1.2.3.1  YOGA WITH SIGNS  
 There is recitation with the four external branches, recitation with the four internal branches, 
whispered and mental repetition without deterioration of each set of these four branches, and retaining life -force exertion during mental repetition.  
 1.1.2.3.2  YOGA WITHOUT SIGNS  
 When one attains calm- abiding focusing on the body of the deity and so forth, one meditates 
on emptiness by combining it with deity yoga. One achieves the union of calm -abiding and 
insight comprehending emptiness through combining analysis and placement. ( Pg. 7 of the root 
text)  
 There is a discussion on yoga with sig ns and yoga without signs on pg. 7 of the root text, 
showing many qualms and also the responses on this issue. As I have always said, you should read the text, even a little bit, before coming to class. It makes a difference. But if you don’t have time, th en that is a different matter.  
 The way to progress on the Path of Action Tantra has  four  divisions:  
1 Becoming a vessel suitable to meditate on the path.  
2 Keeping the vows and commitments pure, after becoming a vessel.  
3 The way to perform prior approx imation, while abiding in the commitments.  
4 The way to establish the siddhis after rendering the approximation serviceable. ( Pg. 2 of the

root text ) 
 
We have completed the first three sections. Next is the way to establish the siddhis after rendering the approximation serviceable.  
 1.1.1.4   THE WAY TO ATTAIN TH E SIDDHIS  
There are three (types) of siddhis: (1) the supreme, (2) the medium and (3) the least and there 
are many ways of categorizing them in three.  
By means of nature, the supreme (siddhis) are knowledge bearing
3, higher perception and 
knowing all the treatises4. The middling (siddhis) are: invisibility, taking the essence5 and swift 
footedness. The least (siddhis) are subduing others, killing, expelling and so forth.  
By means of signs, there are  three: substances blazing (with fire), emitting smoke and heat.  
By means of basis, there are siddhis of body6, of substances7 and of resources8. 
By means of the speaker, there are (siddhis achieved through) mantras (pronounced) by arya 
beings, deities and  those upon the earth9. 
By means of lineage, the Great Stages of Mantra states:  
The actions of pacification10, increase11 and wrath12 will be achieved, in order, by (those 
belonging to) the Tathagata lineage, the Lotus lineage and the Vajra lineage. This is how the supreme, middling and least siddhis are attained.  
 
And:  
Also, in every lineage there are three (types of) siddhis and three  (types of actions:) 
pacification, increase and wrath. From these, the Lord of each Lineage, the Consort of the Lineage and the Wrathful (Deities) of the Lineage perform the three (actions of) pacification, (increase, wrath) and so forth.  
3 Knowledge- bearing (rig ’dzin) : the supreme siddhi of being able to proceed to a pure land with one's usual  
body. [Yoga Of Tibet]  
4 Knowing all the treatises: the supreme siddhi of knowing all the treatises immediately upon 
reading them. [YOT]  
5 Taking the essence (bcud len) : the medium siddhi of empowering a pill so that all nourishment can be derived 
from it. [YOT]  
6 Siddhis of body: enhancing the body so that it becomes free from disease, youthful, etc. [Yoga of Tibet]  
7 Siddhis of substances: using substances such as leaves, swords and so fo rth for gaining siddhis.[YOT]  
8 Siddhis of resources: changing ordinary resources into marvellous ones. [YOT]  
9 Those above the earth (sa bla’ i sngags) : Yakshas, Smell -Eaters, etc. [YOT]  
10 Actions of pacification (zhi ba) : include pacifying untimely death, illnesses, epidemics, harmers, and  
contagion. [YOT]  
11 Actions of increase (rgays pa) : include increase of life span, youth, magnificence, power, qualities [of  
realisation] etc. [YOT]  
12 Wrathful actions (drag po) : include killing, expelling, [confusing], and  so forth [of harmful beings]. [YOT]

One scholar says: Trainees of Action Tantra with sharp faculties who accomplish the siddhi of 
knowledge -bearer for remaining alive for man y aeons, can become enlightened in one life -time.  
Ngawang Palden: Such a thing is not stated clearly in the Stages of Mantra and if one accepts the statement of the General (Presentation of the) Classes of Tantra, which states:  
In the Action and Performance Tantras there is no sys tem of enlightenment separate 
from that (presented) in the Perfection system.  
One should examine the assertion of needing to attain enlightenment as a being of the Dense Array Akanishta Buddha Field. ( Pg. 5 -6 of the root text )  
The “Dense Array Akanishta Buddha Field”  is basically one of the pure lands called Akanishta.  
 “In the Action and Performance Tantras there is no sys tem of enlightenment separate from that 
(presented) in the Perfection system.”  Is there any fault i n this statement? One should think 
about this.   “In every lineage there are three (types of) siddhis and three (types of actions:)” refers to the 
fact that there are three types of siddhis and actions in each of the lineages in Action Tantra -  
the Tathaga ta Lineage, the Lotus Lineage and the Vajra Lineage.

LESSON NO: 8     DATE: 19TH MARCH 200 9  
 I mentioned in the last class the great benefit of simply hearing the name of the Buddha. By applying logic and reasoning, we can infer that even more benefit can be derived from studying and reflecting on the meaning of the Buddha’ s teachings. Therefore, we should understand 
that studying the teachings of the Buddha is extremely meaningful and beneficial and we 
should do so with great enthusiasm and delight, seeing how fortunate we are to have  this 
opportunity now to listen to teac hings on such a profound subject. Then, we will listen to and 
study the teachings with a happy mind.   Sometimes, one neglects listening to, studying and reflecting on the teachings but such studies and reflection are extremely important. There are the three wisdoms that come from the threefold activities of hearing, reflection and meditation: the wisdom that comes from hearing, the wisdom that comes from reflection and the wisdom that comes from meditation.  
 In order to develop the wisdom that comes from hearing, we must first hear and study the teachings. The more we do this, the more we develop the wisdom that comes from hearing. Likewise, the wisdom that comes from reflection arises from the activity of reflection. The more reflection and analysis we do,     more wisdom that comes from reflection is generated.  
 
What is the object of meditation? That will be the object that has been ascertained by the 
wisdom that comes from reflection. The wisdom that comes from meditation is generated by the meditation on  that object of ascertainment.  
 The more developed the wisdom that comes from hearing, the stronger the wisdom that comes from reflection. The greater the wisdom that comes from reflection, likewise, the stronger will be the wisdom that comes from meditat ion. 
 Then this question may arise, “So what if these wisdoms are more developed?” The stronger one’s wisdom that comes from meditation, the greater will be one’s ability to meditate from many various angles and perspectives, which will give one a more powerful tool to eliminate one’s faults and to develop good qualities. We should ask ourselves, from time to time, what are the threefold  activities, why are they so important and how do they benefit us? We have to  understand their importance because the n we will be motivated to study, reflect and 
meditate.  
 This is a common mistake that we often make. As mentioned by Choden Rinpoche recently in his Lam Rim teachings at ABC, we think that the Dharma  we study is different from the Dharma that we practise.  We see these two as separate. That is wrong. The very thing we have 
learnt and studied is the very thing that we put into our practice. The thing that we put into our practice is the very thing that we have learnt. These two are actually one. Many people come to classes thinking it is about studying for knowledge or intellectual stimulation and that it has nothing to do with their practice. That is a real mistake. So it is very important that we do not let this happen to us.

*************  
In Action Tantra there are three lineages:  
 
THREE LINEAGES  RANK  ACTION  LORD  MOTHER  WRATHFUL  
Tathagata  Supreme  Pacification  Manjushri  Marici  Achala  
Lotus  Middling  Increase  Chenrezig  Tara  Hayagriva  
Vajra  Least  Wrath  Vajrapani  Analapramohani  Amritakundali  
 
The actions of pacification13, increase14 and wrath15 will be achieved, in order, by (those 
belonging to) the Tathagata lineage, the Lotus lineage and the Vajra lineage. This is how the 
supreme, middling and least siddhis are attained. ( Pg. 5 of the root text )  
 
Why is the Tathagata lineage supreme, the Lotus lineage middling and the Vajra lineage the 
least? If that is the case, is there some difference in the power of the deities of the three 
respective lineages? Aren’t their realisations the same?  
The deities o f these three lineages are all buddhas and their realisations must be equal. But the 
supreme, middling, least siddhis are attained in relation to the three lineages. Since there are 
differences in the siddhis attained depending on the lineage, then we have  to say that there 
must be some difference in their realisations. This is a question for further discussion in our  
Sunday class.  
 
TATHAGATA LINEAGE  
Supreme siddhi /action of pacification  Achieved by lord of  the lineage  Manjushri  
Middling siddhi/action of increase  Achieved by consort of the lineage  Goddess Marici  
Least siddhi/action of wrath  Achieved by wrathful deity of the lineage  Achala  
 
(Please refer to the tables above for the three siddhis in the Tathagata lineage and the Lords, 
consorts and wrathful deities of the Lotus and Vajra lineages).  
 
There are many other divisions in the three lineages which we will not cover here as they are quite complicated. There is a trinity known as the protector of the three lineages who are 
usually depicted in thangkas. They are Manjushri, Chenrezig and Vajrapani. They are the protectors of the three lineages because they are the Lords of their respectiv e lineages: 
Manjushri is the Lord of the Tathagata lineage, Chenrezig is the Lord of the Lotus lineage and Vajrapani is the Lord of the Vajra lineage.  
 A question that often comes up is how one knows whether one’s approximation retreat has been successful or done well.  According to the Susiddhi Tantra, which is an Action Tantra, the 
signs of the approximation retreat having hit the mark are when, during the retreat:  
• one’s experience of hunger, thirst and general suffering subside  
13 Actions of pacification (zhi ba) : include pacifying untimely death, illnesses, epidemics, harmers, and 
contagion. [YOT]  
14 Actions of increase (rgays pa) : include increase of life span, youth, magnificence, power, qualities [of  
realisation] etc. [YOT]  
15 Wrathful actions (drag po) : include killing, expelling, [confusing], and so forth [of harmful beings]. [YOT]  
 
Lesson  8                           Page 49

Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
• if one is sick, one’s sickness becomes less serious  
• one feels very happy  
• one’s physical appearance becomes more majestic  
• one experiences good dreams  
• one is eager and happy to recite the mantra  
• one’s body or the retreat room is infused with a pleasant smell  
• one develops strong  faith in the deity that is being practised in the retreat  
 
Another Action Tantra text called The Concentration Continuation mentions the following signs 
of an unsuccessful retreat when one experiences the following during the retreat:  
• more hunger and thi rst 
• the mind is disturbed or becomes unhappy  
• one has doubts questioning, “Why am I doing this retreat?”  
• lack of real desire to practise, meditate or recite mantras  
• experiencing bad dreams  
• one wants to engage in idle gossip  
 In short, when one feels happy during the retreat and enjoys the retreat, then that is the sign 
that the retreat is going well. However, when one feels miserable or unhappy, then that is the 
sign the retreat has not gone well.  
 We should distinguish betwe en what is meant by: (1) one is entering into Tantra and (2) one is 
entering the Tantric path. There is a lot of debate on this issue. Are they referring to the same 
thing or are they different?  
 Entering into Tantra  
The doorway into Tantra is by taking th e initiation. What is the meaning of “to receive an 
initiation”? The entity of an initiation has two parts: consciousness and the non- associated 
compositional factors. At the time of the initiation, one has to understand what is being said, what is being c onferred, what to meditate on and so forth for one to receive the initiation. If 
one has received the initiation properly, then one has entered into Tantra.  
 Entering the Tantric Path 
One must enter a path. We will discuss this in the context of the path o f Action Tantra.  
First one enters into Action Tantra by receiving an Action Tantra initiation. Then comes the 
time when one develops the uncontrived bodhicitta by thinking, “I must achieve enlightenment for the benefit of all sentient beings.” At this tim e, one enters the path of accumulation of 
Action Tantra. What constitutes this path? It starts from that beginning up to the concentration abiding in sound.  
 In the practice of Action Tantra, when one achieves the meditative stabilisation which is the union of calm abiding and special insight, that is when one   enters the path of preparation  of 
Action Tantra, which means one has actualised yoga without signs.  
 We had seen how the path of Action Tantra is divided into the two yogas: (1) the yoga with 
 
Lesson  8                           Page 50 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
signs and (2) the yoga without signs. The yoga with signs has many divisions that constitute the 
path of accumulation. When one achieves the yoga without signs for the first time, then one 
achieves the path of preparation of Action Tantra.   At the yoga withou t signs, which is the yoga of non- dual profundity and clarity, when that 
wisdom directly perceives emptiness, one has entered the path of seeing  of Action Tantra. The 
direct perception of emptiness by this wisdom of non- dual profundity and clarity acts as an 
antidote to the intellectually acquired afflictive obstructions:  
• When one achieves the wisdom of non- dual profundity and clarity that directly perceives 
emptiness for the first time, one enters the uninterrupted path of path of seeing.  
• The intellectua lly acquired afflictive obstructions are abandoned when one achieves the 
path of thorough release .  
• When one arises from that meditation, one is in post meditative equipoise , which is a 
wisdom which is neither of those two.  
• One is still on the path of seeing.  
• Through greater familiarisation with  the wisdom of non -dual profundity and clarity, which 
then acts as the antidote to the innate afflictive obstructions, one then enters the first uninterrupted path of the path of meditation.  
 There are nine leve ls of innate afflictive obstructions to be abandoned on the path of 
meditation: - 
1. three big innate afflictive obstructions  
2. three middling innate afflictive obstructions  
3. three small innate afflictive obstructions  
4.  
According to the explanation given by one scholar, Jedun Sherab Wangpo, the three big innate 
afflictive obstructions and the three middling innate afflictive obstructions are being 
abandoned sequentially from the first to the sixth grounds. When one achieves the path of 
thorough release on the sev enth ground, these afflictive obstructions are completely 
abandoned. The abandoning of the three small innate afflictive obstructions takes place simultaneously on the seventh ground.  
 On the eighth to the tenth grounds, the three levels of knowledge obstr uctions (or obstructions 
to omniscience) are abandoned. When those are abandoned, one achieves enlightenment and the four bodies of the Buddha simultaneously.
12 
 In the General (Presentation of the) Classes of Tantra composed by Khedrup Je, it  states:  
 In the Action and Performance Tantras there is no system of enlightenment separate 
from that (presented) in the Perfection system. ( Pg. 6 of the root text )  
 
For the trainees with sharp faculties, by depending on the path of Action Tantra, completing 
the four branched repetition and bringing the  concentration without repetition to culmination, they will achieve the supreme siddhi (or the siddhi of knowledge be arer). Once that siddhi is 
achieved, one has the ability to live for thousands of years and based on that lifespan, one can 
 
Lesson  8                           Page 51 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
achieve enlightenment.  
 
According to the Perfection Vehicle (Paramitayana), one achieves enlightenment in the Akanishta pure land. I s this necessarily so for the  trainee of both Action and Performance 
Tantra?  
 One should examine the assertion of needing to attain enlightenment as a being of the Dense 
Array Akanishta Buddha Field. ( Pg. 6 of the root text ) 
 
One needs to achieve enlight enment in the Akanishta pure land because the the basis for 
actualising the  sambhogakhaya is Akanishta.  We talked about the four bodies in the last module on the Ornament for Clear Realisations . There are five certainties connected with the 
complete enjo yment body or sambhogakaya, one of which is the certainty of abode, that it 
abides only in Akanishta. 
3 
 If the abode of the complete enjoyment body is necessarily Akanishta Pure Land, does that 
mean one has to be there when one first achieves enlightenmen t? If that is the case, does the 
Action Tantra trainee necessarily have to be there when he first achieves enlightenment?  Do 
you understand the reason why, in accordance with the Perfection Vehicle, the place of enlightenment is necessarily Akanishta Pure  Land and not the desire, form or formless realms?  
 
Yet according to Khedrup Je’s text, “I n the Action and Performance Tantras there is no system 
of enlightenment separate from that presented in the Perfection system.”  Is there any fault 
then in asserting that for the Action Tantra trainee, the abode of enlightenment is necessarily 
Akanishta?  
 Let us say a person from the desire realm enters into the path of Action Tantra. By actualising the yoga with signs and yoga wi thout signs, with the culmination of those concentrations, he 
achieves the supreme siddhi of knowledge bearer and has a very long life. Where does this person achieve enlightenment? Is it in the desire realm? When one practices Tantra, one achieves enlight enment in one life and one body. In the context of Action Tantra, it is not 
necessary then for that person to first achieve enlightenment in Akanishta. If that is the case, then the system of enlightenment in the Perfection Vehicle and in the Action Tantra  are 
different. Can we say then that Khedrup Je is wrong?  I brought this issue up for the purpose of discussion.   
• In the context of Action Tantra, when the trainee of Action Tantra first achieves 
enlightenment, is that place of enlightenment necessarily Akanishta?  
•  According to the Mahayana Sutra, is it possible to achieve enlightenment in one life and 
one body? When one achieves enlightenment, doesn’t one do so in one life and one body? Shakyamuni Buddha achieved enlightenment in the same life and same body, didn’t he?  
 The phrase “non -dual profundity and clarity”  is used in the first three classes of Tantra – 
Action Tantra, Performance Tantra and Yoga Tantra. What do these words mean?  
• “Profundity” refers to the wisdom realising emptiness.   
• “Clarity” re fers to the clear appearance of the deity’s body  
 
Lesson  8                           Page 52 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
• “Non -dual” means that these two are united in one causal collection, i.e. they are present in 
a single consciousness.  
 
Yoga with signs:  
There is recitation with the four external branches, recitation with the four internal branches, whispered and mental repetition without deterioration of each set of these four branches, and retaining life -force exertion during mental repetition.  
 Yoga without signs : 
When one attains calm- abiding focusing on the body of the  deity and so forth, one meditates 
on emptiness by combining it with deity yoga. One achieves the union of calm -abiding and 
insight comprehending emptiness through combining analysis and placement. ( Pg. 7 of the root 
text) 
 According to Mahayana Sutra, someone of definite Mahayana lineage will have already realised emptiness when they enter the Mahayana path. If that is the case, then for a trainee of Action Tantra, who has supposedly sharper faculties than the trainee of definite Mahayana lineage 
entering the Sutra path, that trainee will necessarily have the realisation of emptiness when he 
enters the path.  
 One can also analyse this: Does the bodhisattva on the Tantric path of accumulation possess the yoga with signs in his mental continuum? That bodhisa ttva necessarily has the realisation 
of emptiness so whatever deity yoga he practises be it visualising the deity, recitation of mantra and so forth will  necessarily be conjoined with the realisation of emptiness. That being  the case, does that bodhisattva possess the yoga with signs?   
(Concerning) the meaning of Yoga with and without Signs, the Stages of Mantra states:  
 
Yoga with Signs is deity meditation and repetition without meditation on emptiness. Yoga without Signs is deity meditation and repetition with meditation on emptiness. It is not meditation on emptiness alone. (Pg. 7 of the root text )  
 
Is there then a yoga  with signs that is devoid of meditation on emptiness? But according to the 
quotation, “ Yoga with Signs is deity meditation and repetition without meditation on 
emptiness. ”  This seems to be saying that yoga  with signs is deity meditation and repetition 
that is devoid of meditation on emptiness, i.e., there is no meditation on emptiness when one is practising yoga with signs. But yoga with signs is found on the path of accumulation. If that is the case, that will mean that the bodhisattva on the Tantric pat h of accumulation  does not 
realise emptiness. Is there anything wrong in saying that? Doesn’t the yoga of non- dual 
profundity and clarity exist on the Tantric path of accumulation?  
 The point here is to highlight that it is not so straightforward or easy to delineate between yoga with signs and yoga without signs.  
 If one performs deity meditation and repetition without meditating on emptiness, then that 
serves no purpose.  Therefore, in Tantric practice, deity yoga is always conjoined with the 
 
Lesson  8                           Page 53 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
realisation  of emptiness. If that is the case, then all Tantric practices are necessarily yoga 
without signs, which will mean that yoga with signs is unnecessary.  
 
Question: Under yoga without signs, there is the concentration bestowing liberation at the end of sound  which is a concentration that does not rely on repetition. Is there then any repetition 
of mantra during the yoga without signs?   
Answer : Yes, there can be repetition of mantra during the yoga without signs. For example, a 
trainee on the Tantric path of preparation has already achieved the yoga without signs and that 
bodhisattva can practise deity yoga, repetition of mantra and so forth.  
 In the last lesson, we discussed how the entire path of Action Tantra is condensed into the three meditations: the me ditation on the deity’s body (Mahamudra), the meditation on the 
deity’s speech (Mantra) and the meditation on the deity’s mind (Suchness).  These meditations are done sequentially. Even within repetitions, there are different levels. The meditation of the deity’s body and meditation of the deity’s speech fall under yoga with signs.

Lesson  8                           Page 54
