LESSON NO: 9     DATE: 24TH MARCH 200 9  
 We have to remind ourselves constantly of the inconceivable benefits  of listening to, studying 
and reflecting on the Buddhadharma because with that understanding, we will engage in our practice and studies with delight and enthusiasm. The more we study, the more we are able to analyse and reflect on the teachings. With mor e reflection, we can also meditate more. The 
more we are able to meditate, we will have more ways at our disposal to eliminate the different faults and to achieve all the good qualities.   Meditation is extremely important. Whether we are able to meditate or not depends on whether we have studied or reflected on the teachings. Realising the importance of studying and reflection, we should strive to put effort into these activities.  
 Studying, reflection and meditation will make a difference even to the pra ctice of taking refuge 
when we recite the refuge prayer, “I go for refuge until I am enlightened to the Buddha, the Dharma and the Sangha .”  With greater understanding of the teachings, there will be so much 
to think about and so much more benefit when we recite this prayer and it will help us with the different levels of the refuge practice.  
 It is unnecessary for me to talk about the importance of having the correct motivation for 
studying the Buddha’s teachings. The main purpose of studying the teaching s is to enable us to 
put them into practice. That is the kind of motivation we should have when we engage in 
Dharma studies.  
  
A star, a visual aberration, a flame of a lamp,  
An illusion, a drop of dew, or a bubble,  
A dream, a flash of lightning, a cloud-  
See conditioned things as such!  
 The analogy of the star illustrates the two truths: the conventional truth and the ultimate truth. Both appearance and emptiness can be found in functioning things. The object appears one way to the wisdom of meditative equipoise directly realising emptiness. The same object appears in a different way to the conventional valid cogniser, which is a conceptual consciousness. In relation to the two truths, that is how ultimate truth appears to the wisdom 
directly perceiving emptiness and how the conventional truth appears to the conventional valid 
cogniser.  
 Phenomena appear as truly existent to the conventional valid cogniser but that is not how they exist in reality. That leads us to the next analogy, the “ visual aberration .” If phenomena exist in 
the way they appear to the conventional valid cogniser, this will mean that they are truly existent, which will be the final mode of abiding of phenomena. If conventionality is the final mode of abiding of phenomena, then phenomena should be seen as such by the meditative equipoise directly realising emptiness of all the aryas. But the aryas in meditative equipoise directly perceive emptiness and do not perceive conventionality at all.  
 
 
Lesson  9                           Page 55 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
A visual aberration can be likened to someone with impaired vision who sees   before his eyes 
falling hairs, which is a mere appearance to the eye consciousness of that person. In reality, the falling hairs do not exist. If the falling hairs exist in reality, then anyone without the visual impairment will  also see them. But this is not the case.   Although phenomena are not truly existent, nevertheless, they exist as mere appearances.  It does not mean they do not exist and to understand this, we have the next analogy of “a flame 
of a lamp” which we will discuss in the next lesson. I am explaining this verse so that when we 
recite it, we can reflect on its meaning. I thought this might be helpful. It does make a difference if we know the meaning of what we are reciting.  
 Question: According to the Consequence Middle Way School, what is the difference between a mistaken and a wrong consciousness?  
Answer :  A wrong consciousness is a knower that is mistaken with respect to its object of mode 
of apprehension. A  mistaken consciousness is a knower that is mistaken with respect to its 
appearing object. For example, a conceptual consciousness apprehending a vase is a valid cogniser that is a mistaken consciousness but it is not a wrong consciousness.  
 We have now c ompleted the section on Action Tantra.  
 In the Action and Performance Tantras there is no system of enlightenment separate from that 
(presented) in the Perfection system. ( Pg. 6 of the root text )  
 
In the last lesson, we saw how the system of the enlightenment as presented in Action Tantra 
is the same as that presented in Mahayana Sutra (Paramitayana) with regards to the abandonment of the obstructions to liberation and obstructions to omniscience.  
 In Paramitayana, one needs to accumulate the collection of merit over a period of three great countless eons. In the practice of Action Tantra then, does one also need to accumulate merit over three great countless eons to achieve enlightenment? If the pa th of Action Tantra’s path is 
quicker, then the system of enlightenment between Action Tantra and Paramitayana is different, isn’t it?   
PERFORMANCE TANTRA  
 
1.1.2    THE WAY TO PROGRESS ON THE PATH OF PERFORMANCE TANTRA.  
 There are four:  
1 Becoming a suitab le vessel  
2 Keeping the vows and commitments pure  
3 The way to approximate (the deity)  
4 The way to attain the siddhis  
 
1.1.2.1     BECOMING A SUITABLE VESSEL  
As it is stated in the Performance Tantra, one becomes a vessel suitable to meditate on the

path by attaining the empowerment given in the mandala possessing the essence of great 
compassion and so forth. One requires the five Knowledge empowerments, as (one is) not 
permitted by the Water and Crown empowerments alone (to meditate on the path). Apart from that, the rest is probably similar to Action Tantra.  
1.1.2.2    KEEPING T HE VOWS AND COMMITME NTS PURE  
The root downfalls are similar to (those in) Action Tantra. Also other commitments explained in Action Tantra are probably stated to be common (to both ). 
In the Manifest Enlightenment of Vairochana
16 is stated that:  
It is necessary that one does not give up immaculate dharma and bodhichitta even for the sake of one’s life, (that one) is not miserly and does not cause harm to sentient beings, and abides in  the foundation of the training abandoning the ten non -virtuous 
actions.  
 
1.1.2.3      THE WAY TO APPROXIMA TE (THE DEITY)  
There are two:  
1 Yoga with Signs   
2 Yoga without Signs  
 
1.1.2.3.1  YOGA WITH SIGNS  
There is recitation with the four external branches,  recitation with the four internal branches, 
whispered and mental repetition without deterioration of each set of these four branches, and 
retaining life -force exertion during mental repetition. ( Pg. 6 -7 of the root text)  
The four branches are the same as  in Action Tantra, i.e., they are the self basis, other basis, 
mind basis and sound basis.  
 
In Performance Tantra, there are four external branches and four internal branches. In the practice of Tantra, whenever there is the generation of a deity, it is a lways done with an 
ascertainment of emptiness. It is that mind that realises emptiness that is generated into the entity of the deity. So, within that state of emptiness, one instantaneously arises as a deity.  
 In Performance Tantra, the deity practice is  that of Vairochanabhisambodhi, a deity of the 
Tathagata lineage of Performance Tantra, who is yellow in colour with one face, a crown protrusion, two arms with the hands in meditative equipoise,  sitting  on a lotus and moon disc in the aspect of the complete enjoyment body (sambogakhaya).  
There are 116 deities in his entourage. With the principal, there are 117 deities in the mandala. 
In this practice of Vairochanabhisambodhi:  
 
16 Perfect Enlightenment of Great Vairochana Sutra  
 
Lesson  9                           Page 57 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment                                                                   
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
FOUR EXTERNAL BRANCHES 
External self basis  
• One generates oneself as the deity in the beginning and meditates on emptiness.  
• The mind that ascertains emptiness transforms into a moon disc.  
• On top of the moon disc is the gold- coloured syllable, AH.   
• Light rays radiates from the AH and performs the two purposes, i.e.,  purifying the 
negativities and obscurations of sentient beings and making offerings to all the buddhas and bodhisattvas.  
• The light rays then gather back into the syllable AH on the moon disc and one then 
completely transforms into the  deity, Vairochanabhisambodhi.  
External other basis   
One visualises the deity Vairochanabhisambodhi in the space in front of oneself.  
External mind basis  
One visualises a moon disc, which entity is one’s own mind realising emptiness, at the heart of the deity in the space in front.  
External sound basis  
On top of the moon disc at the heart of the front generation deity, one places the mantra garland that one is going to repeat.  
These are the four external branches.   
 After one has visualised these four branches, one then maintains clear appearance of oneself as the deity and generates divine pride on the basis of that deity. When that is done well, one focuses on the mantra garland on the moon disc at the heart of the deity in front and performs 
the mental repetition of the mantra while retaining the life -force exertion.  
 The way of performing the above visualisation is similar to that of Action Tantra but the generation of the six deities is not clearly stated in the text for Performance Tantra.  
 As stated in the text, the recitation of the four external branches involves the whispered and the mental repetition, “ Whispered and mental repetition without deterioration of each set of 
these four branches, and retaining li fe-force exertion during mental repetition.”  
 FOUR INTERNAL BRANCHES Internal Self Basis  
At the beginning, one meditates on emptiness. Within the state of emptiness, one arises as a deity through any one of the following four syllables: AH (long), AH (shor t), AUM, AH.  
Internal Other Basis  
Then visualise at one’s heart standing upright like a mirror, a moon disc. On the surface of that moon disc, there is another Vairochanambhisambodhi deity similar to the self -generation deity.  
 Internal Mind Basis  
In th e heart of that deity, there is a moon disc.  
Internal Sound Basis  
On the top of that moon disc is the mantra garland of the deity.  
 
 
Lesson  9                           Page 58 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
On the basis of these four internal branches, one performs the repetition of the mantra 
progressing from whispered repetition to mental repetition. At this point in time, one strives to achieve calm abiding. Once that is achieved, one analyses the meaning of emptiness. Through the practice of alternating between analysis and placement, one develops the union of calm abiding and special insight and when that is achieved, one actualises yoga without signs.  
 Summary  
• Recitation with the four external branches.  
• Recitation with the four internal branches.  
• Whispered and mental repetition without deterioration of each set of th e four branches.  
• Retaining life -force exertion during mental repetition.  
• Actualising yoga without signs.  
 1.1.2.3.2  YOGA WITHOUT SIGNS  
 When one attains calm -abiding focusing on the body of the deity and so forth, one meditates 
on emptiness by combining it  with deity yoga. One achieves the union of calm -abiding and 
insight comprehending emptiness through combining analysis and placement. (Pg. 7 of the root text)  
 Question: What is the purpose of the four internal branches? This seems to be the main differe nce between Action and Performance Tantra.  
Answer : It isn’t quite like that. The manner of practising Action Tantra and Performance Tantra 
is almost similar but there are some slight differences in explaining the paths.  
 The four external branches are si milar to the practice of the concentration with repetition in 
Action Tantra. The four internal branches are similar to the practice of the concentration 
without repetition in Action Tantra. In  Performance Tantra, these concentrations are explained 
under the four external and internal branches.  
 Also, in Action Tantra, the process of self -generation is done through the six deities whereas in 
Performance Tantra, this is not clearly stated in the text.   
 (Concerning) the meaning of Yoga with and without Sig ns, the Stages of Mantra states:  
 
Yoga with Signs is deity meditation and repetition without meditation on emptiness. Yoga without Signs is deity meditation and repetition with meditation on emptiness. It is not meditation on emptiness alone. ( Pg. 7 of the  root text )  
 Based on this verse in Lama Tsongkhapa’s Stages of Mantra, we can identify what is yoga with signs and what is yoga without signs. Is there a yoga with signs that is deity meditation and 
repetition without meditation on emptiness?  Is there a  yoga without signs that is deity 
meditation and repetitions with meditation on emptiness?   
You cannot say that whatever is mentioned in this verse is not the intended meaning because 
this verse states what is meant by yoga with and without signs.   
 
Lesson  9                           Page 59 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
One scholar says: Deity yoga that is not qualified and (yoga that is) qualified by the 
discernment comprehending emptiness is respectively the meaning of Yoga with and (Yoga) without Signs.  
 Ngawang Palden: This should be examined because there is Yoga with Signs that is qualified by the discernment comprehending emptiness because it is said that if one meditates on 
emptiness prior to Yoga with Signs and at that time one has a strong discernment 
comprehending emptiness, the Yoga with Signs that follows after that will be qualified by it. (Pg. 7 of the root text )  
 Ngawang Palden is saying that this should be examined because there is yoga with signs that is conjoined with the mind realising emptiness. This is because it is said that when one meditates on emptine ss prior to y oga with signs  and at that time one has a strong realisation of 
emptiness, then the yoga with signs that follows will be conjoined with the mind realising emptiness.  
 That is because it is similar to what is stated in the Condensed Stages of  the Path:  
 
If one has a strong wisdom comprehending emptiness, when one is practicing generosity, prostrations, circumambulations and so forth, although the mind that is 
focused on these (activities) does not comprehend emptiness, it is not contradictory 
that it engages (in these activities) through the strength of that (comprehension of emptiness).  
Similarly, if one generates strong bodhichitta at the beginning of the session, when (one later) engages in concentration on emptiness, at that time although bodhichitta is not manifest, it is not contradictory that (concentration on emptiness) is qualified by the presence (of bodhichitta). ( Pg. 7 of the root text)  
 Ngawang Palden’s response is substantiated by the Middle Length Lam Rim  and is the main 
reason  that he states it is incorrect to differentiate yoga with signs and yoga without signs on 
the basis of the whether that yoga is conjoined with the mind realising emptiness.  
 Question: If a Hinayana arhat enters the tantric path after having been awakened by the 
Buddha and meditates on the yoga with signs, can we say that there is no realisation of 
emptiness in that arhat’s mind?  
Answer : Before we analyse, we have to be very clear as to what is yoga with and without signs. 
There is an instance of yoga with signs qualified by the meditation on emptiness and there is an 
instance of yoga without signs involving the manifestation of the deity. Is there a yoga with 
signs that realises emptiness? Is there a yoga without signs that appears as the deity?

LESSON NO: 10    DATE: 26TH MARCH 200 9  
 We usually regard relics to be very precious and holy. There are many types of relics: hair, nail, etc. as well as relics that appear after death. For example, after Shakyamuni Buddha passed away into parinirvarna, the remains of his holy body became relics.  
 
After the holy body is cremated, we get another kind of relics, mustard seed- like relics. It is said 
that seeing these relics are like seeing the Buddha himself and  considered very beneficial. This 
is why there is always so much interest from people when relics are on display.  
 Then there is a fourth type of relics which are known as the dharmakaya relics. From the perspective of Tantra, these relics refer to all th e dharanis (or retention mantras) of the four 
classes of Tantra. There are different dharanis for Highest Yoga Tantra, Yoga Tantra, Performance Tantra and Action Tantra. The dharmakhaya relic is considered to be the most superior among the four types of re lics. One of the reasons that holy objects and statues are 
seen to be so precious and as supreme objects of homage and offerings is because they are filled with the dharmakaya relics of the different dharanis of the four classes of Tantra. Since that is th e case, when one recites the different mantras (or dharanis) which are posited as the 
dharmakhaya relics, it will bring about inconceivable benefits.  
 
When one analyses this further, applying logic and reasoning, the benefits that we get from 
studying and learning about the four classes of Tantra cannot be less than the benefit of simply 
reciting the mantras. In fact, we will get even more  benefit by studying Tantra. Seeing the 
physical relics of the Buddha and holy beings can bring about so much benefit.  If we then study 
the valid treatises of the Tantric teachings of the Buddha and put them into practice, that will really be receiving the benefit of meeting the dharmakaya relics.   The Buddha’s main purpose in leaving behind relics was to benefit the dis ciples. Think about 
this: what is it that actually transforms our minds? It is the activities of learning and studying and then reflecting on the teachings of the Buddha. So we can say that these teachings are the dharmakaya relics.   Following this line o f reasoning, we can understand that we get inconceivable benefits and 
merits by studying the teachings of the Buddha. We will accumulate more merit when we 
reflect on those teachings. Then, if we were to meditate on the teachings, we will accumulate 
even m ore merit. Therefore we should make as much effort as possible to meet with the 
dharmakaya relics,  the teachings of the Buddha. We need to understand and remember the benefits of listening to, studying and reflecting on the teachings. We will then  engage  in our 
studies with enthusiasm.   It is possible that some students think that coming to class for two hours is a waste of time. It is good if you can understand what is being taught during class but even if you do not understand anything, that doesn’t me an that you have wasted your time. It is not like that because when 
you spend two to three hours looking at physical relics, you don’t consider that a waste of time.

Over the years during this Basic Program, I have been encouraging and reminding you of the importance of studying and reflecting on the teachings. I have  constantly mentioned the importance of studying. Many of you have heeded my advice and have tried your best to study. I think that is my responsibility as a teacher. From my side, I have t o do that. The fact that 
students have responded to my encouragement to study fulfil the purpose of my teaching here.  
 
***********************************  
 We have started the section on Performance Tantra. In that practice, there are the four -
external -branched repetition and the four -internal- branched repetition. Bringing these to 
culmination, one achieves calm abiding. After one achieves calm abiding, one moves on to achieve the union of calm abiding and special insight focussing on emptiness.   I had for gotten to explain the ways to distinguish between Sutra and Tantra. They are primarily 
differentiated by the existence of a meditation on the path that accords with the four complete purities that exist at the time of the result. The presence of such a meditation determines whether the practice is Tantra or not. The four complete purities  are: 
1) The complete purity of the abode , which refers to meditation on the inestimable mansion 
that is the reflective appearance of the enlightened wisdom of the deity  
2) The complete purity of the body , referring to the deity’s holy body that is adorned with the 
signs and exemplifications  
3) The complete purity of the enjoyments , referring to the offering substances that are the 
reflective appearance of the enlightened wisdom, i. e., the offering substances that arise 
from the wisdom of the buddha ground.  
4) The complete purity of the actions or enlightened deeds , referring to the uninterrupted 
continuous activities of the Buddha that exist until the end of samsara.  
In essence, the r esultant four complete purities refer to the Buddha’s abode, the Buddha’s holy 
body, the enjoyments experienced by the Buddha and the Buddha’s activities.   Geshe -la: I am not sure what the Buddha eats. Do you think the Buddha eats? When Nepali 
Buddhists m ake offerings to the Buddha’s statue, they bring the food in a plate and then they 
will put the food into the mouth of the statue of the Buddha!  
 
In Tantra, even before one achieves enlightenment, one can engage on the path in meditations 
that accord with the aspects of the four complete purities of the resultant stage:  
● For example, one meditates on where one lives as the inestimable mansion of a deity. That 
meditation accords with the aspects of the complete purity of the environment which exist at the time of result.  
● In order to achieve the complete purity of the body which exists at the time of the result, 
while one is on the path, one practises deity yoga, visualising oneself as the deity. One meditates on emptiness and then, within the state of em ptiness, one arises as the deity. In the 
case of Action Tantra, one arises as the deity by means of the six deities. In Performance Tantra, one does so through the three rituals.  
 
Lesson  10           Page 62 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
● In the practice of Tantra, one generates oneself as the deity and then mak es offerings to that 
self-generation. The offerings are also generated from within the state of emptiness. This 
practice accords with the aspects of the complete purity of the enjoyments which exist at the 
time of the result.  
● For the meditation of the pr actice that accords with the aspects of the complete purity of the 
enlightened activities which exist at the time of result, it is very clear in Yoga and Highest Yoga Tantra that there is the practice of the supreme victorious mandala in which there is a 
meditation on emanating various deities to work for the welfare of sentient beings and then 
gathering them back again.  
 
This is not so clear in Action and Performance Tantra. There, one visualises oneself as the deity and then places the mantra garland at one’s heart. One then visualises light rays radiating out, 
working for the benefit of sentient beings. I guess that meditation is the meditation that accords with the aspects of the complete purity of the activities which exist at the time of result.  
 So, the Tantric path is the path with the meditations that accord with the aspects of the four 
complete purities which exist at the time of result. The path that does not have these meditations that accord with the aspects of the four complete purities which e xist at the time 
of result is not the Tantric path. It is Sutra.  
 
Question: How does one make offerings to the self -generation deity?  
Answer : After one has generated oneself as a deity, one visualises from one’s heart, offering 
goddesses emanate. They hold various offering substances in their hands and they then make 
these offerings to oneself as a deity.  
 
Here is a qualm: When you visualise yourself as a deity, can you still do the practice of tonglen? Ordinary appearance and grasping are counteracted when you generate yourself as a deity. At that time, if one’s concentration is very strong, one may have a strong clarity of oneself as the deity with strong divine pride. I think at that time it may be difficult to do tonglen.  
 But if one’s ordinary appe arance and grasping are still there and the clarity of oneself as the 
deity and the divine pride are not that strong, at that time it may not be so difficult to do tonglen. A scholar, one Lama Kirti Lobsang Thinley,  mentioned in his text that one can prac tice 
tonglen by visualising oneself in the ordinary aspect at the heart of oneself as the deity. 
However he said this is  not definitive and only his personal view.  
 
In the Paramitayana, method and wisdom are two separate minds but there is a practice of conjoining them, either method conjoins with wisdom or vice versa. However, these two are 
separate entities although they assist each other.  
 In Tantra, however, method and wisdom are not distinct entities but both are found in one consciousness. In Tantra , method is referred to as the extensive deity yoga whereas wisdom 
refers to the profound wisdom realising emptiness.  What the consciousness is ascertaining is emptiness and that appears in the form of a deity. I am not saying that method is wisdom as method and wisdom are separate but they are found in one consciousness.  
 
Lesson  10           Page 63 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
Question: Why should it be more difficult to practice tonglen as a deity?  
Answer : The main reason for doing tonglen practice is to overcome self- cherishing and is a 
practice of volunta rily taking on the suffering of others.  
 Although this is the way that method and wisdom are united in the sutric system, uniting method and wisdom is not (achieved) by that alone in the mantric system. This is because both 
the extensive (method of) deity yoga and the profound wisdom comprehending the lack of 
inherent existence are inseparably united as the collection of method and wisdom, at one time and in one awareness. (Pg. 7 of the root text ) 
 The way method and wisdom are united in Sutra is mentioned in the Condensed Stages of the Path but t he way of uniting method and wisdom in Tantra is something different. As stated in 
the text “This is because both the extensive method of deity yoga and the profound wisdom 
comprehending the lack of inherent existence are inseparably united as the collecti on of 
method and wisdom, at one time and in one awareness. ” The reason for the inseparability of 
method and wisdom at one time and in one awareness in Tantra is then given.  
 The Stages of Mantra states:  
 
At the time of the result, both the deity adorned w ith the marks and signs and the mind 
of non- observation reliant on that abide indivisible in nature, at the same time. 
Similarly, at the time of the path,  it is necessary that both the method, which is one’s 
body appearing as the Tathagata’s body, and the wisdom focusing on the non -inherent 
existence of the suchness of one’s mind at that time, are indivisibly united as the collection of method and wisdom, at one time, in the indivisible nature of one awareness. ( Pg. 8 of the root text )  
 What this extract is saying is that the Buddha’s enlightenment that  we are trying to achieve is 
characterised by the Buddha’s body and mind. At the time of the result, these two (body and mind) are inseparable in entity. That being  the case, then at the time of the path, the causes for actualising such an inseparable body and mind must be a consciousness that unites both method and wisdom in one inseparable entity. ( Interpreter ’s note: “ At the time of the path”  is 
missing from the English translation of the original root t ext although it appears in the Tibetan 
text).  
 And:  
Although that wisdom comprehending the lack of inherent existence of the appearance of the deity, the aspect held, is one in nature with the mind of extensive deity yoga, positing them as distinct method  and wisdom is by the power of the convention of 
distinct isolates, which negates wrong (modes of) distinction. (Pg. 8 of the root text )   
 
Answer to question: The practice of method and wisdom is to achieve the two bodies - the 
truth body and the form body. This is the same in both Tantra and the Paramitayana. The method in Paramitayana is great compassion and bodhicitta which are the causes for achieving 
 
Lesson  10           Page 64 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
the fo rm body of the Buddha. The wisdom realising emptiness is the cause for achieving the 
Buddha’s mind, the dharmakaya.  
 
This is mentioned by Nagarjuna in the Precious Garland and also in the concluding prayers of 
the Eight Mahayana Precepts,  “May I and all s entient beings achieve the two enlightened holy 
bodies created by the two vast accumulations of merit and transcendental wisdom.” Why do we need method and wisdom? In order to achieve the two bodies - the form body and the 
truth body. Method and wisdom are  posited as the collection of merits and wisdom. The 
collection of merits is accumulated through the meditation on bodhicitta which results in the form body of the Buddha, i.e., that meditation is the cause for achieving the form body of the Buddha. Wisdom  is likewise the cause for the attainment of the truth body.  
 Question: In terms of method, how come bodhicitta is replaced by deity yoga in Tantra?  
Answer : The way to achieve these two bodies – the truth body and the form body - is the same 
in Tantra, i.e ., through method and wisdom. However in Tantra, the method is the practice of 
the extensive deity yoga which is the cause for the attainment of the form body. In Tantra, the way of achieving the form body is posited in an uncommon way. Therefore the metho d in 
Tantra is more profound than the method posited in the Paramitayana. This means that the method posited by the Paramitayana is not the ultimate method.  
 The reason for meditating on deity yoga to achieve the form body is because,  at the time of 
the cause, there must be a meditation that accords with the aspects which exist at the time of 
result, enlightenment. A specific cause leads to a specific result. Concordant causes lead to 
their respective results. A concordant result cannot arise from a disco rdant cause.  That is why 
the  method posited in Tantra is more profound and the method is changed from bodhicitta to deity yoga.  
 Student:  I can’t believe that bodhicitta is a discordant cause for achieving enlightenment.  
Response : Bodhicitta is a consciousness. A form body is form. If you assert  that bodhicitta is 
the substantial cause of the Buddha’s form body, this follows that a consciousness can act as the substantial cause for form. That is not possible. Can our present consciousness act as the substantial cause for the physical body of the next life?  
 A vase is a product and is also impermanent but these two, product and impermanence are not the same but are different. The vase is impermanent because the vase is the reverse of not 
undergoing change in each and every moment. Therefore it is posited as impermanent. The 
vase is a product because it is the reverse of that which is not produced. Product and 
impermanence are mutually inclusive but they are not the same because they have different names. One is the reverse of a specific thing and the other is the reverse of another specific 
thing. Therefore, they are not the same but they are mutually inclusive.  
 In Tantra, there is the inseparable yoga of method and wisdom which are found in one collection. It is method and also wisdom. The wisdom in this inseparable pair of method and 
wisdom is so called because it is the reverse of apprehending reality in the distorted way. The method is so called because it is the reverse of the lack of potential t o achieve the Buddha’s 
form body.  
 
Lesson  10           Page 65 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
Question: Please explain the meaning of “ the aspect held” in the above quotation. What is the 
referent object here?  
Answer : “Aspect held ” is sometimes translated as the aspect of the apprehender and the 
apprehended. In this context, the aspect of the apprehended refers to the mind realising emptiness which then arises as the deity. The aspect of the apprehender is in the aspect of the object that is appearing to that consciousness. For example, there is a consciousness  
apprehending vase. What appears to that consciousness is the object, vase. We can say that 
consciousness is in the “aspect” of vase.  
 
The two paragraphs from the root text are quotations from The Stages of Mantra. The first 
paragraph establishes the reason f or having method and wisdom in one inseparable entity. It 
shows that there is such a thing. A qualm may then arise. If method and wisdom are inseparable, are these two the same thing? The second paragraph then goes on to explain that although they are one inseparable entity, they are posited as “ distinct method and wisdom .”

LESSON NO: 11    DATE: 29TH MARCH 200 9  
 We are going to meditate for five minutes. You can reflect on any topics of your choice or you can reflect on what we have covered so far in class. You can also use this opportunity to set your motivation or do a simple deity yoga practice.  You can visualise Shakyamuni Buddha in the space in front and perform the seven limbs practice and generate the mind of 
enlightenment (bodhicitta). It is very beneficial to do this as generating a mere wish to achieve 
enlightenment purifies so many  obscurations and creates so much merit.  
 All of you have received many teachings so you should reflect for the next five minutes on whatever you think is most suitable and beneficial for your mind.  You should try to do this. Meditation is not easy and you will not succeed by just doing it once or twice. But for the next five minutes, you should try for one minute, then two minutes and then extend the duration of your meditation to  five minutes. It is not easy in the beginning as the mind gets distracted from the object of meditation. Sometimes, one gets bored after meditating for a while and the mind becomes very dull and one en ds up not thinking of anything at all. That is not meditation. 
The point is to hold the object of focus very clearly in the mind. It is important for the mind to remain alert whether one is doing analytical or placement meditation. If you allow yourself to  
space out and your mind to become dull, this will not benefit you. So let us try (to meditate) for the next five minutes.  
 
********************* 
We will continue with the verse from The Diamond- Cutter Sutra : 
 A star, a visual aberration, a flame of a lamp, An illusion, a drop of dew, or a bubble,  
A dream, a flash of lightning, a cloud -  
See conditioned things as such!  
 The analogy of “ a flame of a lamp” is used here to highlight the fact that all phenomena appear 
to the consciousness as inherently existent even though that is not how they exist in reality. Although phenomena do not inherently exist, nevertheless it is important to posit the existence of c onventional phenomena which appears to the conventional awareness (mind). 
How does the flame of a butter lamp come about? That depends on many conditions coming 
together, i.e., the butter and wick in the container.  The flame of the butter lamp exists in 
dependence on many conditions for its production. Therefore it is not inherently existent.  
 For the Autonomy Middle Way School and the tenets below it, a phenomenon, such as the flame of the butter lamp which came about through the coming together of many causes and conditions, is established by way of its own character.  
 According to the Consequence Middle Way School, however, because phenomena, such as the flame of the butter lamp, come into existence by depending on causes and conditions, therefore they  are not inherently existent.  
Neither samsara nor nirvana exists inherently. Samsara exists as the result of many causes and 
 
Lesson  11           Page 67 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
conditions. Likewise, nirvana (the attainment of liberation) occurs as the result of many causes 
and conditions. When we think of samsara, it appears to exist inherently from its own side. Not only does it appear  to be inherently existent from its own side to our minds, our minds believe 
that is how samsara exists. Due to this grasping at an inherently existent samsara, we experience  the problems of samsara.  
 Although samsara does not exist in the way it appears to us –  although it appears as inherently 
existent, it does not exist inherently – we believe that is how samsara exists and we grasp at 
samsara as inherently existent. That leads to all sorts of problems and sufferings. This is illustrated by the next analogy, “ an illusion .” An illusionist can produce many kinds of illusions 
such as an attractive person or a frightening image but, in reality, these are just  illusions. There is really nothing there but when we look at these images, we believe in those appearances, thinking that there is an attractive person or frightening image. That (belief) causes us suffering.  
 The analogy of the illusion shows that although things and eve nts do not exist by way of their 
own character (do not exist inherently), nevertheless we believe in and grasp at how they appear to us, which only results in problems and suffering.  
 
• The verse from The Diamond- Cutter Sutra  begins with “ a star ” which illu strates how the two 
truths (i.e., both appearance and emptiness) exist within any given phenomenon.  
• The analogy of “ a visual aberration” Illustrates that things do not necessarily exist in the way 
they appear to us. They appear to us as inherently existen t but they do not exist inherently. 
That appearance of the inherently existent  phenomenon is like a visual aberration.  
• The analogy of “ a flame of a butter lamp”:  If things do not exist inherently, does that mean 
they do not exist? No, that is not the ca se. Things do not exist inherently but nevertheless we 
need to posit their conventional existence. That phenomenon is mere appearance but it does  
exist.  
• The next analogy of “ an illusion ” illustrates how we are drawn into suffering because of our 
believing  in the appearance of an inherently existent phenomenon.  
 It is beneficial to reflect on the meaning of this verse as we recite it. Better still if we can 
memorise the verse and then we can reflect on each of those analogies as we read the words. 
If there is time, we can pause after each analogy that we recite, for example, when we say “ a 
star,” and give ourselves some time to reflect on its meaning. Likewise, we can do the same 
thing for “ a visual aberration”, “a flame of a lamp”  and so forth. When we can reflect on the 
meaning of the verse as we recite it, we fulfil the purpose of learning and  committing this verse to memory.   
 
***********************  
Thus, the Yoga without Signs in this context, is profound and clear non -dual yoga. It is not 
merely deity yoga qualified by discernment comprehending emptiness because without 
meditation on emptiness it does not become concentration without signs. ( Pg. 8 of the root 
text)  
 As explained here, the author is  saying that the non -dual yoga of profundity and clarity is the 
Yoga without Signs. If it is a non -dual yoga of profundity and clarity, is it necessarily a Yoga 
 
Lesson  11           Page 68 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
without Signs? Can there be a non -dual yoga of profundity and clarity which is a Yoga with 
Signs? Does the non-dual yoga of profundity and clar ity exist on the Tantric path of 
accumulation?  
 
It is difficult to say that the non- dual yoga of profundity and clarity, which is essentially the 
inseparable yoga of method and wisdom, does not exist on the Tantric path of accumulation. Will it be correct to say that such a yoga does not exist on the Tantric path of accumulation?  
 This is a qualm that you have to think about. There is no decisive conclusion here but according to this author, he is saying that the non- dual yoga of profundity and clarity is t he Yoga without 
Signs. He further substantiates his assertion by quoting Kedrup Je’s The General (Presentation 
of the) Classes of Tantra:  
 
Although they have consummated the absorption with signs, they do not have the direct antidote that severs the root o f samsara. Therefore to sever the root of samsara, 
Yoga without Signs is needed. To meditate on that one must meditate according to instruction through becoming expert in analytical and placement meditation on emptiness, that does not meditate on any relat ive aspect such as the body of the deity 
and so forth.  
And:  
Yoga without Signs familiarizes with the continuity of the determining awareness 
determining the emptiness of inherent existence of all dharmas through logic such as 
neither being one nor many and so forth. ( Pg. 8 of the root text )  
 
“Although they have consummated the absorption with signs, they do not have the direct 
antidote that severs the root of samsara. Therefore to sever the root of samsara, Yoga without Signs is needed” : Is this sentence then saying that the Yoga with Signs (the absorption with 
signs) is not the direct antidote that severs the root of samsara? If that is the case, then Yoga with Signs is not the realisation of emptiness.   
  
Here, it is clear that the Yo ga with Signs is not the direct antidote that severs the root of 
samsara, “ Therefore to sever the root of samsara, Yoga without Signs is needed.”  
 “To meditate on that one must meditate according to instruction through becoming expert in analytical and placement meditation on emptiness, that does not meditate on any relative 
aspect such as the body of the deity and so forth”  refers to the Yoga without Signs.  
A qualm is then presented:  
 
Regarding that someone says: Take as the subject Yoga without Signs, it follows that it is not deity yoga because the body of a deity with the aspects of face, arms and so forth, does not appear. This is because it is a mind directly comprehending emptiness.  
 Ngawang Palden: There is no pervasion because one must accept that t he subject, the basis 
which is empty, appears to the conceptual logical awareness comprehending emptiness. This is because the Great Commentary to the Madhyamikakarika and the Stages of the Path state just 
that. (Pg. 8 -9 of the root text ).  
 
Lesson  11           Page 69 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
The author replies to the qualm by saying that even if it is deity yoga, it does not necessarily mean that aspects of the deity, such as the arms, must appear. Even if it is a mind realising emptiness, it does not mean that the deity cannot appear.   
 This is a difficult point. The “ conceptual logical awareness comprehending emptiness ” refers to 
the inferential cogniser realising emptiness. The question is this: Does the basis of emptiness 
appear? According to Lama Tsongkhapa, the answer is “Yes”, the basis  of emptiness does 
appear. According to Khedrup Je however, the answer is “No,” the basis of the emptiness does 
not appear as stated in this quotation, “ To meditate on that one must meditate according to 
instruction through becoming expert in analytical and placement meditation on emptiness, that does not meditate on any relative aspect such as the body of the deity and so forth.”  
 This is a complicated issue as different masters hold different views. In accordance with the assertion of the author, even if it is a conceptual logical awareness comprehending emptiness , 
this does not necessarily mean that the deity’s body does not appear to that consciousness. It is possible for the deity’s body to appear.  
 Question: When there is the direct realisation of emp tiness, doesn’t only emptiness appear?  
Answer : This is not referring to the direct  realisation of emptiness but to the conceptual 
consciousness realising emptiness.   
 The author is basically saying that to the inferential cogniser apprehending emptiness, that consciousness realises emptiness and it is also possible for the deity’s body to appear to that 
consciousness. These two are not necessarily contradictory.  
 Although this is the way that method and wisdom are united in the sutric system, uniting method and wisdom is not (achieved) by that alone in the mantric system. This is because both the extensive (method of) deity yoga and the profound wisdom comprehending the lack of inherent existence are inseparably united as the collec tion of method and wisdom, at one time 
and in one awareness. ( Pg. 7 -8 of the root text ) 
 This goes back to our earlier discussion of what non-dual yoga of profundity and clarity is. In 
Tantra, both method and wisdom are found in one single consciousness. T his consciousness 
(non -dual yoga of profundity and clarity) realises emptiness and what appears to that 
consciousness is the deity’s holy body. This is the meaning of, “ the extensive (method of) deity 
yoga and the profound wisdom comprehending the lack of inherent existence are inseparably united as the collection of method and wisdom, at one time and in one awareness”  (Pg. 8 of the 
root text ). In this context, the Yoga without Signs is the non- dual yoga of profundity and clarity. 
It ascertains emptiness and the deity’s body appears to that consciousness.  
 To that someone says: It follows that when Yoga without Signs perceptually comprehends emptiness by the strength of meditation, the body of the deity possessing the aspect of a face and arms appears because your thesis is correct.  
 
 
Lesson  11           Page 70 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
Ngawang Palden: There is no pervasion because although the body of the relative deity does 
not appear, it is posited as deity yoga by means of continuum. (Pg. 9 of the root text ) 
 When we talk about Yoga without Signs, it c an refer to a Yoga without Signs that is conceptual 
consciousness or a Yoga without Signs that is a direct perceiver (direct perception consciousness). We said earlier that the Yoga without Signs that is the conceptual consciousness ascertaining emptiness,  it is possible for the deity’s body to appear to that 
consciousness.  
 What happens when the Yoga without Signs changes its entity from being a conceptual consciousness to a direct perceiver? When this direct perceiver  directly perceives emptiness, the de ity’s body does not appear to that direct perception of emptiness. The question then 
arises: is that Yoga without Signs deity yoga? According to the author, the answer is “Yes,” because “it is posited as deity yoga by means of continuum.”  
 In general, if it is either the Yoga with or without Signs, there is no pervasion that the body of the deity with the aspects of face and arms will appear, because in this context there are four yogas*: deity (yoga), empty (yoga), wind (yoga) and repetition yoga. Yoga wit h Signs and Yoga 
without Signs subsume these (four).
17 This is because the Stages of Mantra states:  
 
In Action and Performance Tantras there are four important yogas: deity and empty 
yoga, wind and repetition yoga. In that, the two yogas of the relative and the ultimate 
deity are the main establishing (factors) of the two bodies. Since repetition (yoga) is the branch for motivating the mind of whichever deity is meditated upon, it is subsumed as a branch of relative deity yoga. Since wind yoga is the branch stabilizing both deity yogas, it is subsumed by both (yogas). Through that (the four) are subsumed by two: the Yoga with Signs and the Yoga without Signs. (Pg. 9 of the root text ) 
 “The two yogas of the relative and the ultimate deity are the main establishing (factors) of the two bodies”:  The yoga of the relative deity establishes the form body and the yoga of the 
ultimate deity establishes the truth body. The wind yoga refers to the practice of keeping the wind (subtle energy) inside the body as we perform repetition yoga. Under these yogas, there is the concentration abiding in sound and concentration abiding in fire. In those concentrations, we do not focus on the deity’s body but rather focus on sound but these are deity yogas. Does the deity’s body appear in those deity yogas? No, it does not.  
 The main point is that if it is a deity yoga, it does no t necessarily mean that the deity’s body will 
appear to that consciousness.  
 Question:  What does “motivating” the mind mean?  
Answer : Kyabe Lama Zopa Rinpoche uses the word, “persuading.”  
17 The four yogas: in Action and Performance Tantras there are four yogas: 1) empty (ultimate) yoga is the main 
means for achieving the Truth Body, 2) deity (conventional) yoga is the main means for achieving the Form Body, 
3) wind yoga 4) repetition yoga. None of these include the Stage of Completion that is found only in the Highest 
Yoga Tantras.

FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
Question: Are we “persuading” the deity’s mind or our own mind?  
Answer:  When we are doing repetition, we are persuading the holy mind of the deity to grant 
us both the common and supreme attainments. That is why we do the repetition over and over again so that we can get closer to the deity with the hope that certain feats or attainments can be achieved. It is similar to a beggar harassing you to give until you succumb to his request.  
 
Question: Isn’t the action of a deity spontaneous?  
Answer:  Are you saying that if you don’t do anything, the Buddha will give you bless ings?  
 
At the very beginning, you should have a clear understanding of the two truths and how they are not mutually exclusive in the sense of both being present in any selected phenomenon.  
 In the Heart Sutra, there is the four -fold emptiness –  “Form is emptiness; emptiness is form;  
emptiness is not other than form; form is also not other than emptiness .” It will be beneficial to 
have a clear understanding of this in this context.  
 “Form is emptiness; emptiness is form ”: When one looks for form, in the e nd, what one finds is 
the emptiness of an inherently existent form. But form does exist. The form that one finds is 
the form that arises from within the state of emptiness –  while it is empty, there is form. One 
discovers a form that is the display of empt iness.   
 Understanding this, whatever phenomenon we select, be it form or the deity’s body, both are equal in their being empty and within the state of emptiness, both are equally pure. Within the 
state of emptiness, the emptiness of the I and the emptiness of the deity are the same and it is within this state of inseparability in emptiness, that one arises as the deity. That is how one arises as the deity.  
 1.1.2.4     THE WAY TO ATTAIN TH E SIDDHIS  
 Through relying on external substances such as the  sword and so forth, one attains knowledge 
bearing of the sword and so forth. Through meditation on the mandalas of earth, water, wind and fire in places inside (one’s) body, one attains the actions of pacification, increase and so forth. Through establishing Manjushri and so forth, if such a Bodhisattva were to stroke one’s head or congratulate one at the end of one’s recitation, it is stated in this Tantra to be the (sign 
of the) attainment of the concentration not forgetting bodhichitta. Many ways to att ain these 
and other siddhis are stated. ( Pg. 9 of the root text ) 
 
Question: How is the wind yoga subsumed under the ultimate deity?  
Answer : In the text, it says the “ wind yoga is the branch stabilizing both deity yogas, it is 
subsumed by both (yogas ).” The  wind yoga can be found in both the yogas of the relative and 
the ultimate deities and is essentially the practice of restraining the life -force exertion which 
stabilises the mind so that one can meditate on emptiness with a stable mind. Restraining the life-force exertion also helps in one’s concentration focussing on oneself as the deity and 
therefore helps one’s practice of deity yoga.  
 
 
Lesson  11           Page 72 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
Question:  In Performance Tantra, is there an order of progression in practice, starting with 
Yoga with Signs and then moving on to Yoga without Signs?  
Answer : Yes, one starts with training in Yoga with Signs and then progresses on to Yoga without 
Signs.  
 
Question: Should Yoga with Signs be seen as a preliminary to Yoga without Signs? What is the purpose of Yoga with Signs?  
Answer : At the end of Yoga with Signs, you generate calm abiding but the realisation of calm 
abiding alone is insufficient to sever the roots of samsara. Therefore, one needs to train in Yoga without Signs, the primary objective of which is to achieve the  union of calm abiding and 
special insight focussing on emptiness.  
 Question: Can we say that the Yoga with Signs is the path of accumulation and Yoga without Signs is the path of preparation?  
Answer:  Yes. The Yoga with Signs is practised on the path of a ccumulation and when one 
achieves the Yoga without Signs, one attains the tantric path of preparation.    
 Question: Can we assume that the Yoga with Signs is on the tantric path of accumulation and that the Yoga without Signs exists on the tantric path of preparation all the way up to the moment before enlightenment?  Answer: Before we answer this, we need to think ab out this: Is the Yoga with Signs necessarily 
found only  on the path of accumulation? Can Yoga with Signs be found on the higher paths –  
the path of preparation, the path of seeing and the path of meditation as well?   
    Interpreter: Ven. Tenzin Gyurme

LESSON NO: 12    DATE: 31ST MARCH 200 9  
 
YOGA TANTRA  
 1.1.3   THE WAY TO PROGRESS ON THE PATH OF YOGA TANTRA  
 
It has four divisions:  
1 Becoming a suitable vessel 
2 Keeping the vows and commitments pure  
3 The way to perform approximation  
4 The way to attain the siddhis  
 
1.1.3.1   BECOMING A SUITABLE VESSEL  
 One becomes a vessel suitable to meditate on the path through attaining the empowerments bestowed in such mandalas as the Vajra Sphere and so forth. There is a difference b etween 
those who merely have the trainee empowerment who hold only the bodhisattva vows, and those on whom the complete vajra master empowerment, who hold both (the bodhisattva) and the tantric vows. (Pg. 10 of the root text )  
 
In the empowerment of Yoga T antra, if the trainee (student) empowerment is bestowed, the 
vows to be protected are the bodhisattva vows.  
 
If the vajra master empowerment is also conferred, one will also have to keep the tantric vows. 
Whether one has to keep tantric vows or not depends on whether one has received the vajra master empowerment.  Since the vajra master initiation is usually conferred in the Yoga and Highest Yoga Tantra initiations,  the recipient will have to keep the tantric vows. Since the vajra master initiation is not  conferred in Action and Performance Tantra empowerments, one does 
not need to keep the tantric vows.   
 One becomes a suitable vessel to meditate on the path of Yoga Tantra by entering into the mandala of a Yoga Tantra deity. One receives the empowerment after entering the mandala.  
 
1.1.3.2   KEEPING THE VOWS AND  COMMITMENTS PURE  
If one is suitable vessel for (holding) the Emancipation (vows) one should practice through possessing the three (types of) vows. Therefore one should have all three vows and guar d the 
commitments
18 as stated in the Supreme Glory19 and in the Peak20. (Pg. 10 of the root text ) 
18 The fourteen commitments related to the Yoga Tantra: 1 -3) taking refuge in the Three Jewels as the three 
commitments related to the ground of the Tathagata family, 4 -6) commitment of holding vajra [mudra], bell 
mudra and master, as the three commitments related to the Vajra family, 7 -10) being generous with Dharma, 
material things, fearlessness and love, as the four commitments related to the Jewel family, 11 -13) holding the 
external action tantra, the inner yoga tantra and the Dharma of the three vehicles,  as the three commitments of 
the Lotus family, 14) striving in the offering activities, as the commitment of the Action family.  
19 Supreme Glory Tantra: Paramadya Tantra ( pal mchog dang po zhes bya ba theg pa chen po ’i nog pa’i  rgyal po)  
 
Lesson  12           Page 74 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment                                                                   
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
The emancipation vows are the vows of individual liberation or pratimoksha vows. A lay or 
ordained person can possess the three vows:   
 ● A lay person who possesses the three vows (holder of the three vows) is someone who has 
taken the lay precepts and the bodhisattva and tantric vows. ●  An ordained person is a holder 
of the three vows if that person has taken the ordination vows either as a novice or fully 
ordained monk or nun and, on that basis, taken the bodhisattva and tantric vows.  
 When one is a holder of the three vows, one has to protect and guard them as well as the uncommon commitments specified in some of these Yoga Tantras such as the Supreme Glory Tantra and the Vajra Peak Yoga Tantra .  
 1.1.3.3   THE WAY TO PERFORM A PPROXIMATION  
 There are two divisions:  
1 Yoga with Signs  
2 Yoga without Signs  
 1.1.3.3.1  YOGA WITH SIGNS  
 There are two divisions:  
1 Focusing on the whole deity.  
2 Focusing on specific hand implements.  
 1.1.3.3.1.1  Focusing on the whole deity  
 By obtaining merely the trainee empowerment, one has (the commitment for) the (single)  
yoga of the deity upon which the flower has landed. (Pgs. 10 –  11 of the root text )  
 The word “single” does not appear in the translation but it is in the Tibetan text. (In general, it 
is challenging to get an understanding of Yoga Tantra as it is not very clear in the various commentaries on Yoga Tantra). The yoga performed by those who rec eive only the trainee 
empowerment is called the single yoga. What single yoga actually refers to is not very clear. I haven’t seen any clear explanation of the practice of single yoga. Does it refer  merely to the practice of a self generation alone or the  practice of self generation together with the 
repetition of mantra or does it mean something else as well?  
 By obtaining the master empowerment, in order to empower students and establish oneself as the deity, one engages in either the extensive approxima tion which meditates without any 
decrease of the three concentrations along with the great yoga of self completion, the medium approximation condensing the rite for generating the supreme conqueror mandala
21, or the 
abbreviated approximation which includes the recitation of 100,000 (mantras) for each of the 
20 Vajra Peak Yoga Tantra: (Explanatory Tantra of the Yoga Tantras) ( sang ba rnal ’ byor chen po ’i rgyud rdo rje rtse 
mo) 
21 Supreme Victory Mandala ( dkyil ’khor rgyal mchog)  
 
Lesson  12           Page 75 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment                                                                                                                                                                                                                
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
great yoga deities . (Pgs. 10 – 11 of the root text )  
 
There are three ways that the approximation can be performed: extensive, middling and abbreviated.  The great yoga deities mentioned in the abbreviated approximation refer to the deities of Yoga Tantra.  
 The practice of Yoga with Signs in Yoga Tantra focusses on the coarse deity as one engages in 
the approximations. The extensive way of performing the approximation is done through the 
three concentrations which are found in the long sadhana and are:  
1. the initial preparation  
2. the supreme victorious (or royal) mandala   
3. the supreme victorious (or royal) activities  
 In the practice of the self- generation, one generates another mandala in front of oneself in the 
centre of which one visualises the Yoga Tantra deity in his full aspect. For example, if the principal deity is Kunrig, one visualises him in the centre of the mandala. The practice of the self generatio n up to visualising the principal deity in his full aspect in the centre of the 
mandala is the concentration of the initial preparation. If one decides at this point to focus on reciting the mantra, this is the shortest way of performing the approximation.   This is the 
abbreviated approximation.  
 
Having visualised the principal deity in the centre of the mandala in front of oneself, one then 
visualises emanating from the heart of the deity the other retinue deities and placing them in their respective posi tions in the mandala. This practice is the concentration of the supreme 
victorious mandala and doing this practice in addition to the concentration of the initial preparation is the medium approximation.  
 There is then the practice of persuading the holy m ind of the deity to work for the benefit of 
sentient beings. The principal deity in the mandala emanates limitless replicas of himself to do this. This practice is called the concentration of the supreme victorious activities. Adding this practice to the e arlier two practices is the way to perform the extensive approximation.  
 Question: What is the “ great yoga of self completion ”?  
Answer : It is not clear in the text as to whether this refers to the three concentrations or 
something else. The meditation on the generation stage of Highest Yoga Tantra also has similar 
terminologies and the structure of the three concentrations.  
 
In the practice of Yoga Tantra, after one generates oneself as the deity, one performs the impressions of the four seals which are:  
1. the great seal of the body  
2. the doctrine seal of the speech  
3. the pledge seal of the mind  
4. the action seal of the action

THE FOUR SEALS  
 The Basis  The Path  The Result  
The great seal of 
the body  One’s ordinary  
body  The meditation of oneself as the 
deity purifies the ordinary body  Achieving the  
holy body of the Buddha  
 The Basis  The Path  The Result  
The doctrine seal 
of the speech  One’s ordinary 
speech  The repetition of mantras purifies 
one’s ordinary speech  Achieving the  holy 
speech of the Buddha  
The pledge seal of 
the mind  One’s ordinary 
mind  The non -dual yoga of profundity 
and clarity purifies one’s ordinary 
mind  Achieving the  
holy mind of the Buddha  
The action seal of 
the action  One’s ordinary 
actions of body, 
speech and mind  The practice of emanating and 
gathering back the emanations who work for sentient beings is the supreme royal activit y that 
purifies one’s ordinary actions  Achieving the holy 
actions of the Buddha  
 
These four seals are related to purifying our ordinary body, speech, mind  and  action into the 
Buddha’s holy body, speech, mind and action.  
 
In the practice of Yoga Tantra, after one has generated oneself as the deity, one gathers the wisdom beings and causes them to dissolve into oneself. After that one performs the impression  of the four seals. This is extremely important in Yoga Tantra and is extensively 
explained in Lama Tsongkhapa’s Stages of Mantra.    
 The root tantra of Yoga Tantra is Compendium of Principles Tantra refers to the four lineages. 
Usually there are five line ages but it is mentioned very clearly in the Stages of Mantra that the 
Compendium of Principles Tantra specifically talks about the four lineages which are:  
1. the Tathagata lineage  
2. the Lotus lineage  
3. the Vajra lineage  
4. the Jewel lineage  
The fifth lineage, Action lineage is not explicitly mentioned but is subsumed under the Jewel lineage, i.e., this means that the agent and activities are combined into one. From the perspective of agent, we talk about the Jewel lineage. From the perspectiv e of activities, we 
talk about the Action lineage. We can employ the same logic with respect to the three lineages in Action Tantra which mentions only the Tathagata, Lotus and Vajra lineages. The Jewel and Action lineages are not explicitly mentioned and can be understood to be subsumed under the 
three lineages.  
 
So, to recap, Yoga with Signs has two divisions, the first of which is the focussing on the whole (or coarse/gross deity) as opposed to the second division which is focussing on the subtle (here translated as “specific”) hand implements.  
 1.1.3.3.1.2      Focusing on specific hand implements  
 
 
Lesson  12           Page 77 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
While clearly (visualizing) oneself as the deity, one holds one’s mind on the small vajra hand 
implement at the upper opening (of the central channel), simi lar in color to one’s special deity. 
One should perform (acts) such as sending out and gathering (the hand implements) and so forth, when the mind has stability on that. (Pg. 11 of the root text)  
 Having  visualised oneself as the deity, one then visualises a very small five -pointed vajra at the 
tip of the nose which, at its largest, should be no bigger than a mustard seed and, at its 
smallest, is the size of the tip of a single hair.  (The ‘tip of the nose’ accords with what appears 
in the  Tibetan text. The central channel is not referred to in the lower tantras. Only in Highest 
Yoga Tantra are the upper and lower openings of the central channel mentioned).  
 
Different masters have the different wa ys of explaining this practice:  
• According to the great master, Kunga Nyingpo (Anandagarbha), one first visualises this five -
pointed vajra at the navel and then draws it up to the tip of the nose to abide there. Then one concentrates on that vajra.  
• Accord ing to another master, Shakyamitra, one visualises the vajra at one’s heart and then 
draws it to the tip of the nose. One then concentrates on that object.  
• According to the master Buddhaguhya, one should stop breathing and hold the wind in the 
nose. While  doing that, one then focusses on the vajra at the tip of one’s nose.  
 By concentrating on the very small five -pointed vajra at the nose and keeping one’s mind on 
that object, one achieves calm abiding. To develop dexterity in calm abiding, one then 
visua lises that vajra emanating countless replicas of itself filling all of space. Then, one gathers 
back all those replicas into the vajra at the tip of one’s nose.  
 
In Yoga Tantra, through the practice of focussing on the coarse body of the deity and the sub tle 
hand implements, one brings those practices to culmination and one actualises calm abiding. These two practices are the Yoga with Signs.  
 “One should perform (acts) such as sending out and gathering (the hand implements) and so 
forth, when the mind has  stability on that.” : Here, “mind has stability on that,”  does that mean 
one only does the practice of emanating and gathering back the vajras after one has achieved calm abiding or is that done before one achieves calm abiding?  Reading this, I guess we c an 
say that this practice of emanating and gathering back the replicas of the vajra to the small five-pointed vajra at the tip of the nose is done after  one has achieved calm abiding.  
 
One text says, “ Within the deep concentration focusing on the vajra at the tip of the nose 
without being distracted from that, we then train by using another part of the mind to emanate 
and then gather back these vajras.”  This means that the yogi has already achieved the 
realisation of calm abiding and in order to improve the  dexterity of his concentration, while he 
is in a deep state of concentration focussing on the vajra, he uses another part of his mind to do the practice of emanating and gathering back the replicas.  This can be seen as the training to develop special ins ight which is cultivated within  calm abiding.  
 1.1.3.3.2  YOGA WITHOUT SIGNS  
 
 
Lesson  12           Page 78 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
One resolves that the deity in front of oneself together with the letters of the mantra do not 
inherently exist. When one attains certainty of that, and the continuum of that determining awareness combines analysis and placement, one establishes the union of calm- abiding and 
insight comprehending emptiness. When one obtains such insight, one transfers to the Path of Preparation and so forth, similar to before. ( Pg. 11 of the root text )  
 Let’s say one is doing the Yoga Tantra practice based on Vairochana:  
• One visualises Vairochana in the space in front.  
• In the heart of Vairochana is the mantra garland.  
• One then focusses on the deity together with the mantra garland at the hea rt, reflecting on 
how both do not exist inherently.  
 
(Interpreter ’s note: “awareness combines analysis and placement ” should be read as 
“awareness alternating between analysis and placement”.)  
 I myself am not knowledgeable on Yoga Tantra and it is quite challenging as the commentary in the text itself is not very clear. I guess what I have mentioned so far is correct. If there is anything that is incorrect, I will make the correction in the future.  
 Question: What is the difference between the dexterity o f concentration and pliancy?  
Answer : There are physical and mental pliancy and both are special forms of contact. Dexterity 
can also be translated as skill in training the mind to develop special insight.  
 Question: What is the process of generating onesel f as the deity in Yoga Tantra?  
Answer:  This is a complicated issue and I shall dispense with that. Basically one generates 
oneself as a deity through the five manifest enlightenments.
