LESSON NO: 13   DATE: 2ND APRIL 2009  
 
A star, a visual aberration, a flame of a lamp,  
An illusion, a drop of dew, or a bubble,  
A dream, a flash of lightning, a cloud-  
See conditioned things as such!  
 
The analogy of “ an illusion”  refers to the fact that although all pheno mena are not inherently 
existent yet we believe them to be so. This grasping at phenomena leads to all sorts of 
problems and sufferings. Phenomena are like illusions but be it a phenomenon, a self or an aggregate, we apprehend them to exist from their own side. This grasping at phenomena to be 
inherently existent acts as a basis for us to grasp at them to be permanent and unchanging.   
 
In reality, they are not permanent as shown in the next analogy, “ a drop of dew.”   The self, the 
‘I  and the aggregates are not permanent although we grasp at them to be so. They are like the 
drop of dew at the tip of a blade of grass. As soon as the sun rises, that dewdrop evaporates. 
Likewise our body, the self, the I are impermanent , undergoing change from moment to 
moment. From the moment they are produced, they are produced in the nature of undergoing 
momentary change.  
 
We are born to die as mentioned in Aryadeva’s Four Hundred Verses , “Production is for the 
purpose of destruction.” Once something is born, it undergoes momentary change and moves 
towards disintegration. In that sense, things are produced so that they can be destroyed.  
 The Lord of Death is one of the four maras. We usually think of him as  something external, a frightening creature waiting to take our lives. But we are the Lord of Death. We are moving towards death.   It is insufficient to simply realise that things can disintegrate. It is important to realise that they 
undergo momentary change. For example, when we  are born, in the next moment we have 
aged. Change is happening in each and every single moment. A person doesn’t start aging after 
a few years but aging begins  from the second moment of his existence. It is important we 
realise this.  
 
It is the same wit h our concept of time. When we talk about the past and the future, the past 
seems very short relative to the future. But in reality, whether it be the past or future, there is 
no difference in terms of the actual length of time but there is a difference in  how we feel 
about it. Let’s say we are in our 50’s or 60’s. Although 50 or 60 years had passed yet that time seems to be very short. When we think of the future, we feel we are going to live for many more years to come. But in reality it is not like that.   
 Let’s take the example of someone whose life span is 70 years. This person is now 50 years old. In his mind, he thinks that he still has many years to live. The past seems quite brief in comparison. But actually the reverse is true because he has alread y lived for 50 years and now 
he only has 20 years left. The future is much shorter. Having already lived for 50 years, he 
 
Lesson  13           Page 80 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
should be able to manage his life for the next 20 years without any difficulty. But because of  
thinking that he is going to live for another 20 years, he feels he  needs to work very hard to accomplish happiness.    
Such grasping at permanence leads to the arising of attachment, desire and anger. When we are able to remember the impermanent nature of things, it will benefit us in terms of reducing those afflictions. Therefore, it is important for us to reflect on impermanence. Using this 
analogy of the dewdrop, we can reflect on the impermanence of the self and I that is imputed 
on these aggregates. These aggregates can come to an end wh en conditions are present 
because these two, the I and the aggregates are in the nature of impermanence and momentary change, that is, they undergo change in each and every single moment.  
 It is important to reflect in this way as it becomes the reflectio n on subtle impermanence. The 
realisation of the momentary nature of impermanent phenomena helps us to develop renunciation, helps us to develop compassion and helps us to understand and realise emptiness. If we can understand the momentary nature of thing s and events, it will help us to 
reduce or even eradicate a lot of problems and sufferings in this life.  
 It is mentioned in the Lam Rim that amongst all the realisations, the best realisation is remembering impermanence. We understand this intellectually  but we have difficulty blending 
this understanding with our minds, which can only be achieved through continual reflection.  
 
******************************  
 
The way to perform the approximation in Yoga Tantra is through the Yoga with Signs and Yoga withou t Signs. Yoga with Signs is developed through focussing on the coarse body of the deity 
and the subtle hand implements. After that, one does the practice of Yoga without Signs.  
 1.1.3.4   THE WAY TO ATTAIN SI DDHIS  
 Many siddhis are said to be attained thro ugh the three: absorption, recitation and fire pujas.  
 The Stages of Mantra states that from among those, the two, coming to hold the knowledge mantra
22 of a Bodhisattva bearing the form of a complete Buddha and attaining the actual 
siddhi in the form of th e great seal of the body of one’s deity, are the purest siddhis initially 
established by the supreme disciples of Yoga Tantra.  
 In Action and Performance Tantras attaining the siddhi of knowledge -bearer (is stated) as the 
attainment of the great siddhi of the body. (Pg. 11 of the root text ) 
 Siddhis is a Sanskrit word and can also be translated as feats, many of which  are said to be attained through these three activities: - 
1. concentration (absorption)  
22  Knowledge mantra ( rig sngags) : (Vidya mantra) mantras primarily meant for establishing the vajra wisdom 
aspect of mind and the enlightened activities as a deity in a female form together with its sound, mudras etc.  
 
Lesson  13           Page 81 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment                                                                   
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
2. repetition  
3. burnt offerings (usually translated as fire pujas)  
 
Supreme attainment by (means of) the three lower tantras depends on the initial attainment of worldly siddhis. The Great Commentary of the Kalachakra states that in accordance with Highest (Yoga Tantra), it is suitable to establish supreme siddhi by uninterruptedly meditating on the generation and completion stages without relying on those.  
 Also the Stages of Mantra states:  
Initially one purely maintains the vows and commitments that are the sources of siddhis. One then attains potency by putting effort into four sessions (of) Yoga with and without Signs. Then, having attained the common and uncommon siddhis, one progresses through the path. This way (of making progress) should be known in the three lower tantras. Also, it is unsuitable to assert that one progresses on the path by fusing wind, repetition, empty and deity yogas. ( Pgs. 11 –  12 of the root text ) 
 This quotation is saying that in order to progress to the higher paths, one does so through achieving the common and uncommon siddhis and not merely by practising the wind, repetition, empty and deity yogas. In the three lower tantras, one progresses on the paths and grounds after having completed the attainment of, first, the common siddhis followed by the uncommon siddhis (feats).  
 
In the Yoga Tantra section of the Stages of Mantra by Lama Tsongkhapa, there is an extensive 
explanation of the various feats that are achievable. These feats are achieved after one has 
brought to culmination the practices of Yoga with Signs and Yoga without Signs. I will mention a few here:    
Feat of revealing treasure 1. Meditate on a treasure vase at one’s heart.  
2. There is a mo on inside the treasure vase.  
3. On the top of the moon, there is a five -pointed vajra.  
4. One meditates on these signs until one has a strong sense of being able to ‘see’ and touch the vajra in the treasure vase at one’s heart.  
5. Having achieved this, one then per forms repetition and meditation for the whole night. 
Then one goes to the place where one hopes to find the treasure and makes offerings to 
the deities and spirits.  
6. One should then protect the treasure and the place with the mantra and seal (or mudra) of 
Vajraspota.   
7. When all these have been done, the treasure will be found.  
 As mentioned earlier, to accomplish these feats, one has to bring to culmination the practices of Yoga with Signs and Yoga without Signs. Without the realisations of these yogas, it will be extremely difficult to achieve anything even though one tries to perform the visualisations.  
 Feat of not sinking into water  
1. One goes to the bank of a spri ng and fills a great clay vessel with water.  
 
Lesson  13           Page 82 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
2. One meditates for four sessions daily that the water (in the vessel) has transformed into 
vajras until one gets the sign of seeing the body of water as vajras and, when touched, is as hard as vajras.  
3. One then draws a mandala of the appropriate lineage, performs the preliminary rites and 
remains in that meditative stabilisation for the entire night.  
4. Then, when one goes into the water in that state of meditative stabilisation, one will be able to walk on the wat er. 
 These are common feats (or shared feats), so called because they are the common attainments of the four classes of Tantra. There are also specific or uncommon attainments in each class of Tantra, which are achievable only through that particular Tantra.  
 Question: What is the difference between worldly and common feats?  
Answer:  Worldly feats are equivalent to common feats. I am not sure whether common feats 
can be achieved by non -Buddhists. In reality, feats such as invisibility, flying through the sky  or 
pacification of illnesses can also be achieved by non -Buddhists. I am not 100% sure but I would 
equate worldly siddhis to common siddhis and transcendental siddhis to uncommon siddhis.  
There is a reason why one must establish the common siddhis before  establishing the supreme 
siddhi through the path of the three lower tantras because Engaging in the Bodhisattva’s Way 
of Life  states:  
 
All these branches
23 are proclaimed by the Muni for the purpose of wisdom.  
 The system of both Great Vehicles
24 is that the wisdom comprehending emptiness is 
enhanced by the factor of method. ( Pg. 12 of the root text ) 
 
The point of cultivating a method to enhance the factor of wisdom is to make that wisdom powerful. This is the same for both the Paramitayana and Vajrayana in that the wisdom comprehending emptiness is made powerful by the factor of method.  
 
According to the Perfection Vehicle, the wisdom comprehending emptiness is rendered 
capable to abandon the knowledge obscurations
25 through training for a limitless time 
in limitless clear divisions such as generosity and so forth.  
According to Highest (Yoga) Mantra, co mprehension of emptiness in reliance on a 
combination of the points of both (types of) bodhichitta is enhanced by taking desire 
from the union of the two (sexual) organs as the path.  
 
As for the three lower tantras, it is not mentioned that one should tra in for a limitless 
time in the limitless clear divisions such as generosity and so forth, as in the Perfection Vehicle. Nor is it mentioned that one should train in the method of generating a special object -possessor comprehending emptiness as mentioned in  Highest Yoga Mantra. 
Therefore, there is need to enhance the comprehension of emptiness through dexterity 
23 The first five perfections.  
24 The two Mahayana vehicles: (Perfection ) Sutric vehicle and Tantric vehicle.  
25 Knowledge obscuration s: obscuration to omniscience abandoned during the last three, pure grounds  
(according to the Middle Way  Consequence school).  
 
Lesson  13           Page 83 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment                                                                   
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
in methods such as the attainment of many common siddhis, being taken care of and 
blessed directly by Buddhas and higher Bodhisattvas. ( Pg. 12 of the  root text )  
 According to the Highest Yoga Tantra, from the union of the two sexual organs, the two bodhicittas – the white bodhicitta from the father and the red bodhicitta from the mother –  
come together. One then generates the mind of great bliss which  is used to ascertain 
emptiness. The purpose is to generate that special object- possessor comprehending emptiness 
in order to abandon the knowledge obstructions  
 
It is not mentioned in the three lower Tantras that:  
• one has to train for a long time or train  in so many things such as generosity and so forth as 
in the Perfection Vehicle nor is  
• there a special method for generating the special object -possessor comprehending 
emptiness that is produced by depending on the gathering of the two bodhicittas as 
desc ribed in the Highest Yoga Tantra.  
 What happens then in the three lower Tantras? As stated in the above extract, “ Therefore, 
there is need to enhance the comprehension of emptiness through dexterity in methods such as the attainment of many common siddhis , being taken care of and blessed directly by Buddhas 
and higher Bodhisattvas.”  We are examining here what makes the wisdom realising emptiness 
powerful in the three lower Tantras, i.e.:  
• the attainment of many common siddhis and  
• being taken care of and ble ssed directly by Buddhas and higher Bodhisattvas .  
These are the two things that make the wisdom realising emptiness powerful in the three 
lower Tantras, which is different from that which makes the wisdom comprehending emptiness powerful in the Paramitayana.  
 As stated earlier, it is the factor of method that makes the wisdom comprehending emptiness powerful in Paramitayana. It is also stated that it is the factor of method that makes the wisdom comprehending emptiness powerful in Tantra. It is specifical ly mentioned in the three 
lower Tantras that  it is the factor of method that makes the wisdom comprehending 
emptiness powerful. Therefore all of them assert that it is the factor of method that makes the 
wisdom comprehending emptiness powerful but the met hods posited are different.  
 With regard to that, someone says: It is unacceptable to explain that the disciples of Mantra do not need to train in the various limitless, clear divisions of generosity and so forth, because from the awakening mind up to trai ning in the conduct of the Six Perfections is the common 
path of both Great Vehicles.  
 Ngawang Palden: There is no pervasion because only the gross body of practice which is the path for training in the awakening mind, the basis of conduct, and the conduct of the Six Perfections is common. Training in the various, limitless and specific divisions such as generosity and so forth, is different from the path of Mantra because this is stated in the Abbreviated Stages of the Path and in the Illuminating Lamp . (Pgs. 12 –  13 of the root text ) 
 
 
Lesson  13           Page 84 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
The author said that there is no pervasion because from the awakening mind up to training in 
the conduct of the six perfections is the common path of both Great Vehicles but the need to 
train in the limitless divisions such as generosity and so forth, is not common to Mantra but only applicable to the  Paramitayana (Sutra).  
 There is the practice of generosity in both Sutra and Tantra but it is only in Sutra that one has to train:  
• in the limitless divisions of generosity  
• for a limitless time.  
In Sutra, there is an extensive discussion of the practice of generosity such as offering one’s body and so forth. This is not taught in Tantra.  
 
In both Sutra and Tantra, one must cultivate the awakening mind and the conduct of the six 
perfections but in Tantra, one does not have to train in   the limitless division s of the six 
perfections for a limitless amount of time.  
 Answer to question:  The awakening mind and the bodhisattva’s conduct of the six perfections 
are two things. The awakening mind (bodhicitta) is the basis for the bodhisattva conduct.  
 Question: We ha ve established that the bodhisattva’s conduct of generosity that is mentioned 
in Tantra is different from Sutra as in Sutra, there are the limitless divisions of generosity and the need to practise them for a limitless amount of time. How do we delineate t hese two 
different practices of generosity?  
Answer : There is some debate regarding this. That comes later in the text. In Sutra, the practise 
of generosity is not done in relation to deity yoga whereas in Tantra, it is done in relation to deity yoga.

As before, we will spend five minutes in silence to do some meditation. You can reflect on the 
two verses that we have just recited:  
 
Do not commit any non- virtuous actions,  
Perform only perfect virtuous actions,  
Subdue your mind thoroughly  
This is the teaching of the Buddha  
 A star, a visual aberration, a flame of a lamp,  
An illusion, a drop of dew, or a bubble,  
A dream, a flash of lightning, a cloud-  
See conditioned things as such!  
 
We have covered the explanations for the second verse up to a “ a drop of dew.” If it is not 
possible for you to reflect on these two verses, then you can meditate on whatever you wish 
but the point is to keep your mind alert and to try to transform the mi nd into a virtuous state. 
It is your responsibility to do this and is not something that is impossible for you to do. It is my responsibility as a teacher to tell you this and it is your responsibility as a student to do it.  
 Since you are here to listen to the teachings, you have to do this. You should not just sit there, doing nothing. You have to try. If you really cannot meditate for even five minutes, then in future you should not come to class in the beginning but you can come in at 8.00 pm.  
 
*************  
We will continue with the verse from The Diamond- Cutter Sutra . The analogy of “ a drop of dew”  
illustrates impermanence. The moment that things and events are produced or born, they are produced in the nature of impermanence and undergo change. This understanding is primarily used as a basis to examine the self and the aggregates and how they are impermanent by nature.  
 
The cause that produces an impermanent phenomenon is the same as that which produces a 
phenomenon having a momentary nature. Our attachment to our appropriated/contaminated 
body arises due to the grasping at that body as being perma nent. We have to stop that 
attachment and the way to do this is to realise that our body and aggregates are impermanent.  
 
The analogy of “ a bubble”  illustrates that the nature of cyclic existence is suffering. We are so 
attached to our contaminated physic al and mental aggregates which are actually in the nature 
of suffering. A water bubble arises from a body of water. When the bubble bursts, it dissolves back into the water. When we are born, our physical and mental aggregates are produced in the nature of suffering. When these aggregates disintegrate, they do so in the nature of 
suffering.  
 There are three types of sufferings:  
 
Lesson  14           Page 86 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
1. Suffering of pain  
2. Suffering of change  
3. Suffering of conditionality  
 
We all understand the suffering of pain. From the moment we are  born, we know what 
suffering is. We are born in the nature of suffering and we  disintegrate in the nature of suffering as well.  
 
We all look for pleasure and happiness but they are actually the suffering of change. But we do not know this. When the feel ing of pleasure arises, we do not know that it is in the nature of 
suffering. It is only when that pleasure ends that we realise this is so. But we are not aware that it is in the nature of suffering when it is first produced. We do not know instinctively that happiness and pleasure are in the nature of suffering but we can establish this through logic and reasoning. If the pleasure that is produced has the nature of happiness from its first moment, then it would be impossible for that experience to become suffering in the end. But, in reality, the very moment that pleasure is produced, it is produced in the nature of suffering. It is not produced in the nature of happiness and then, somehow along the way, that  nature changes to that of suffering. It is not like that.  
 The third type of suffering is the suffering of conditionality which primarily refers to our appropriated physical and mental aggregates which are the bases for us to experience all 
suffering. When we look at the nature of these aggregates, they are produced in the nature of 
suffering. Although we do not like suffering, problems and suffering arise naturally. Why is this so? This is because we have a contaminated body and mind.  
 Although we want happiness, we have to put in so much effort and  hard work to achieve it 
because of our contaminated bodies and minds. We have to experience aging, sickness and death on the basis of these contaminated bodies which we will not experience otherwise. We are so attached to our bodies, cherishing them and s eeing them as so precious. But if we ask 
ourselves, “What is the basic nature of this body?” our answer will be that it is in the nature of suffering. By understanding that the real nature of our aggregates is in the nature of suffering, we will then be able to overcome our attachment to them.  
 We may understand that our contaminated bodies are the source of all our sufferings and difficulties but we do not really recognise this. Instead we  do the opposite like someone paying 
the utmost respect and revere nce to an enemy. Our bodies are our enemies as they harm us 
and cause us to suffer.  
 
The superior beings, unlike us, perceive the suffering of change and suffering of conditionality to be in the nature of suffering, i.e., they perceive that the aggregates  and samsara are in the 
nature of suffering. The aggregates and samsara do exist in the way they are perceived by the superior beings. It is the truth as it appears to the superior beings. Therefore, it is called the noble truth because this is reality and this is how the superior beings perceive it. Whatever the 
superior beings perceive accords with reality.  
 This analogy of “ a bubble”  illustrates that the nature of samsara and aggregates is suffering and 
 
Lesson  14           Page 87 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
by our understanding this, it will help us to over come our attachment to them. It is extremely 
important for us to understand the suffering of change and suffering of conditionality because 
they form the foundation for our spiritual transformation and realisations. Without this understanding, nothing will  happen for us no matter what kind of profound practices (such as 
Tantra) we may do. We will go nowhere. Most of you may already be aware of this but there may be some people who are not.   I thought it would be beneficial for me to mention this again.  
 
************************  
In order to achieve enlightenment in the three lower classes of Tantra, does one need to accumulate merit for three great countless eons?  
 Again someone says: It follows that through the path of the three lower classes of tantra there is enlightenment without having to rely on three countless great aeons because in those three classes, there is a difference in the speed of the path in comparison with the Perfection Vehicle.  
 Ngawang Palden: There is no pervasion because although in the three lower classes of tantra there is a mere difference in the speed of the path in comparison with the Perfection Vehicle, it is stated that there is no difference in the speed of attaining enlightenment in one lifetime or attaining enlightenment without having to rely on the countless aeons. (Pg. 14 of the root text)  
 Is the author saying that one has to go through three countless eons in order to achieve enlightenment in the three lower clas ses of Tantra?  
 Again, someone says: In the three lower classes of tantra, it is unacceptable to state that there is no method for producing the special object -possessor comprehending emptiness by means 
of taking desire as the path. This is because in the  three lower classes of tantra, (1) taking as 
the path, the bliss derived from oneself and the primordial awareness knowledge woman one meditates on, looking at each other, smiling, holding hands or embracing exists, and (2) that this is for the purpose of developing the special object possessor comprehending emptiness. 
(Pg. 13 of the root text)  
 This extract discusses whether in the lower classes of Tantra, the practice of taking desire into the path is for the purpose of developing the special object - possessor comprehending 
emptiness or not.  
 Ngawang Palden: The latter part of the reason (2) is not established because taking desire as the path for the purpose of generating the special object- possessor comprehending emptiness 
is an attribute of Highest (Yo ga Tantra) alone. This is because the Illuminating Lamp states:  
 Taking desire to pleasurable objects as the path for the purpose of generating a special object -possessor comprehending emptiness does not exist for anything other than the 
Highest (Yoga Tant ra). ( Pg. 13 of the root text)

As discussed in the previous class, in order to make the wisdom realising emptiness powerful in 
the Paramitayana, the bodhisattva has to train in the limitless divisions of the six perfections for  a  limitless amount of time.  
 We have also examined the two things that make the wisdom realising emptiness powerful in the three lower classes of Tantra, which are:  
• the attainment of many common siddhis and  
• being taken care of and blessed directly by the buddhas and higher bodhisattvas .  
 The method that makes the wisdom comprehending emptiness powerful in Highest Yoga Tantra is the practice of taking into the path the desire that arises from the union of the two sexual organs.  
 When the author says, “ The latter part of the reas on (2) is not established,”  his position is that 
in the three lower classes of Tantra, the practice of taking the desire as the path is not for the purpose of developing the special object-  possessor comprehending emptiness.  
 His reason is “ because taking desire as the path for the purpose of generating the special 
object -possessor comprehending emptiness is an attribute of Highest (Yoga Tantra) alone. This 
is because the Illuminating Lamp states: Taking desire to pleasurable objects as the path for the purpose of generating a special object -possessor comprehending emptiness does not exist for 
anything other than the Highest (Yoga Tantra) .” 
 Again someone says: It follows that there is no comprehension of emptiness by the three types 
of bliss derived from looking, smiling and so forth, because taking those as the path is not for the purpose of developing the special object- possessor comprehending emptiness.  
 Ngawang Palden: This should be examined because although most followers explain those t o 
exist, one needs to research whether such is clearly stated in the explanations of the great Je Rinpoche. ( Pg. 13 of the root text ) 
 The author says that we should examine whether the practices of taking the bliss derived from looking, smiling and holding hands  into the path is for the purpose of developing the special 
object -possessor comprehending emptiness. “ Although most followers explain those to exist” 
means that most scholars assert that the bliss derived from such practices are used to realise emp tiness.  
 We have to analyse whether it is all right to say that the bliss derived from looking, smiling and 
holding hands with the visualised goddess is used to realise emptiness. I guess it is OK to say that as we are following what most masters say. But  is there any fault with saying that?  
 The practice of taking desire as the path does exist in the three lower classes of Tantra. If this is so, then there is no reason why that bliss derived from looking, smiling and holding hands (with the visualised goddess) cannot realise emptiness. We can say that this bliss can lead to the realisation of emptiness.  
 
Lesson  14           Page 89 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
But the author states very clearly here that that bliss, in the three lower classes of Tantra, 
cannot realise emptiness. It is only in Highest Yoga Tant ra that taking desire into the path can 
realise emptiness.  
 I think it is better to say that the bliss derived from taking desire into the path in the three classes of lower Tantra can realise emptiness as I cannot find any good reason to say otherwise. Although we say this, the author states very clearly that taking the desire as the path in the 
three classes of lower Tantra is not for the purpose of developing the special object- possessor 
comprehending emptiness. The author substantiates his reason by quo ting from the 
Illuminating Lamp which states : “Taking desire to pleasurable objects as the path for the 
purpose of generating a special object -possessor comprehending emptiness does not exist for 
anything other than the Highest (Yoga Tantra).”  
 
I think it is also all right to follow the author’s assertion that taking desire to pleasurable 
objects into the path in the three classes of lower Tantra is not for the purpose of generating a special object -possessor comprehending emptiness. This is because genera ting a special 
object -possessor comprehending emptiness, which refers to the fundamental innate mind of 
clear light, only exists in Highest Yoga Tantra.  
 This is my opinion. We can discuss further whether this is correct or not.  
 
Question by student : As stated by Ngawang Palden: “ This should be examined because 
although most followers explain those to exist, one needs to research whether such is clearly 
stated in the explanations of the great Je Rinpoche.”  Are the “ those”  and “ such”  referring to the 
speci al object -possessor?  
Answer : Here “those”  and “ such”  are referring to the same thing, i.e., the practice of taking 
desire as the path in the three classes of lower Tantra. If we interpret “ those ”  and “ such” to 
mean the special object -possessor, does that make sense?  
 Answer to question: Terminologies such as the subtle wind and subtle mind are only used in 
Highest Yoga Tantra and is not used in the lower classes of Tantra.  
 So go and read the text again – even it is one or two pages –  and bring the answer  to the next 
class. The question is: In order to achieve enlightenment in the three lower classes of Tantra, does one needs to accumulate merit for a period of three great countless eons? If one says one 
needs three great countless eons, it looks difficult. If you say you don’t need three great 
countless eons, then it is complicated. You need to find out the author’s position by reading pages 13 –  14 of the text. I will randomly pick one of you to give me the answer in the next 
class, so everyone must do th eir readings.  
 Other questions for further discussion:  
1. What makes the realisation of emptiness powerful in the Paramitayana, three lower classes of Tantra and Highest Yoga Tantra?  
 2. What is it that makes Tantra more profound than Sutra? What is it that makes Tantra a 
quicker path than Sutra?  
 
Lesson  14           Page 90 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
For this question, we need to understand what makes Tantra more profound than Sutra and what makes Tantra a quicker path than Sutra. We need to analyse this until we gain the ascertainment, “Yes, this is true.”  That will then benefit our minds for we will naturally 
generate faith that the Tantric path is indispensable for achieving enlightenment  
 3. If it is a path of any of the three lower classes of Tantra, is it necessarily one of the two yogas 
- Yoga with Si gns and Yoga without Signs?  
 
4. Is there a Yoga without Signs that is a Yoga of repetition?  
 We have already seen how the presence or absence of the meditation that accords with the aspects of the four complete purities of the resultant stage determines whether it is Tantra or Sutra. As Action Tantra is Tantra, it necessarily has the meditation that accords with the aspects of the four complete purities of the resultant stage. Since that is the case, by using the Nyung Na sadhana, try to determine which par t of the sadhana accords with the four complete 
purities of the resultant stage.   Knowing Action T antra, you perform  the self basis by generating yourself as the deity through 
the process of the six deities. When you know what are the meditations that ac cord with the 
aspects of the four complete purities of the resultant stage, then whatever Action Tantra 
sadhana you pick up, you will be be able to identify, “This is this” and “That is that.” The whole 
practice becomes richer in taste because of your unde rstanding and you know what to think 
about. You can see the benefits that can be derived from the practice when you know what 
you are practising. This shows that studying is not an obstacle to nor will it harm your practice. It assists your practice. It is  like singing a song. When you know the lyrics, singing it is more 
enjoyable. Without such understanding, when you read the sadhana, it is merely that, reading.

LESSON NO: 15   DATE: 9TH APRIL 2009  
 
All of us have enough material to meditate on. We already know so many topics. The centre 
has been around for 20 years and we have been receiving so many teachings all this time. It is 
just that we are not used to meditating  regularly so we find it diffic ult to meditate. From our 
own side, we have to make the effort to start meditating.  
 
Meditation is simply mixing what we have learnt and what we know with our  mind. Meditation 
is like a mental exercise, thinking about and arriving at an ascertainment of what is mentioned 
in the text.  Meditation is experiencing the teachings and recognising that, “Yes, that is really true. It is like that.” It is difficult to see any positive change in our minds until we have this kind 
of experience.  But once we have exp erienced that kind of feeling in our hearts, change will 
definitely come.  
 
How do you bring about that feeling of, “Yes, the teachings of the Buddha are really great. 
Things are exactly like that (what is described in the teachings)?” That can only come through 
meditation. So, meditation is extremely important as it is only through meditation that we can 
achieve a positive transformation of our minds. When we first begin to meditate, we don’t have to do it for a very long time. We should start with small steps. We will meditate in silence for five minutes and try to place our minds in a virtuous state.  
 We should always remember and understand that we now have a human rebirth and we have met the teachings of the Buddha. On top of that, from our own side, we  have the interest to 
study the deeper teachings of the Buddha, specifically his tantric teachings. We have to understand that we are experiencing the fruits of the powerful virtues or merits that we had 
created in our past lives. Remembering this, we shou ld rejoice and be happy.  
 We are born in a developed country, living in a society where the lifestyle is very busy. Because of our financial situation, most of us have to work to earn a living. Despite all this, we actually put aside time at the end of the  day to come to class. I always have this thought, “How amazing 
it is that people come to class after a day of work.” I always rejoice that this is so.  
 
From your own side, you should also feel very fortunate to be able to do this.  You don’t learn if 
you don’t come to class.  Then whatever you do will only be for the happiness of this life and 
there will be nothing to hope for in your future lives. You need to work to earn a living but you 
are also able to do something for your future lives through studyi ng and practising. You should 
rejoice that you are able to do this.   The fact that you have the interest to study and practise in this life in spite of having so many obligations and responsibilities is the result of the many virtuous imprints planted in your previous lives. So, if you want your future lives to be better than this life, then you should try to place as many positive imprints as possible in this life. If you do that, then there is a real hope that your future lives will be better.  
  
The poi nt I am trying to make here is that we should be delighted and always keep a positive

and happy mind when we are studying the teachings rather than treating this as a burden.  
 
***************************************  
 
According to the author of this text, in the three lower classes of tantra, is it possible to achieve enlightenment without taking three countless great eons?  
 
What is it that enables the person to achieve enlightenment quickly in the   three lower classes 
of tantra?   The two things that make  the wisdom realising emptiness powerful in the three lower classes 
of tantra are: (1) the attainment of many common siddhis and (2) being taken care of and blessed directly by the buddhas and higher bodhisattvas .  
 As for the three lower tantras, it is no t mentioned that one should train for a limitless time in 
the limitless clear divisions such as generosity and so forth, as in the Perfection Vehicle. Nor is it mentioned that one should train in the method of generating a special object- possessor 
comprehe nding emptiness as mentioned in Highest Yoga Mantra. Therefore, there is need to 
enhance the comprehension of emptiness through dexterity in methods such as the attainment of many common siddhis, being taken care of and blessed directly by Buddhas and higher Bodhisattvas. ( Pg. 12 of the root text )  
 In the three lower classes of tantra, does the bliss that is derived from taking  desire into the path realise emptiness? We should analyse this but it seems that it is better to say that this 
bliss does  realise emptiness.  
 It is also clearly stated in the text that the bliss that is derived from taking the desire into the path  in the three lower classes of tantra is not  for the purpose of generating the special object 
possessor realising emptiness.  
 “…because taking desire as the path for the purpose of generating the special object- possessor 
comprehending emptiness is an attribute of Highest (Yoga Tantra) alone. This is because the Illuminating Lamp states:  
 
Taking desire to pleasurable objects as the path for the purpose of generating a special 
object -possessor comprehending emptiness does not exist for anything other than the 
Highest (Yoga Tantra).( Pg. 13 of the root text ) 
 
According to Highest Yoga Tantra, this special object -possessor comprehending e mptiness 
refers to “the fundamental innate mind of clear light,” a term which is not even mentioned in 
the three lower classes of tantra, where there is no explanation of the realisation of emptiness by such a mind. From this, we can  decisively say that t aking desire into the path in the three 
lower classes of tantra is not  for the purpose of generating the special object possessor 
comprehending emptiness. That should be the conclusion.  
 What is this special object possessor comprehending emptiness? Does i t refer to the

FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
fundamental innate mind of clear light which can realize emptiness? When we analyze further, 
it is possible for more questions to arise.  
 Question: What is the difference between the mind of non- dual profundity and clarity realising 
emptiness and the fundamental innate mind of clear light realising emptiness?  Answer : One is a coarse mind, the other a subtle mind. The mind that is used to realise 
emptiness in the lower classes of tantra is necessarily a coarse mind.  
 Question:  What is then the purpose of generating bliss in the three lower classes of tantra?  
Answer : We can answer that question in many ways. We can say it is:  
• for the purpose of realising emptiness,  
• for the purpose of generating the Yoga with Signs, and  
• for the purpose of generating both the Yogas with Signs and without Signs.  
 If we say that in the three lower classes of tantra, through the practice of taking  desire into the 
path we generate bliss and that bliss realising  emptiness is posited to be the non -dual yoga of 
profundity and clarity, what are you going to say?  
 We must ascertain the difference between sutra and tantra. In sutra, there is no practice of taking desire into the path, but in tantra, there is such a practice even in the lower classes of tantra. When you take desire into the path, you generate bliss and then that bliss realises emptiness. In terms of subtlety, is there any difference betwee n that blissful mind realising 
emptiness and the mind that realises emptiness in the Paramitayana? It is not clear in the text but this is something that we can think about.  
 The blissful mind that realises emptiness in the lower classes of tantra is a co arse mind. The 
mind that realises emptiness in the Paramitayana is also a coarse mind. When we compare these two coarse minds, is one less coarse than the other?  
 It is clear that there is a difference in the power of the mind realising emptiness in the Paramitayana and the mind realising emptiness in the lower classes of tantra. We have to say 
that the mind that realises emptiness in the lower classes of tantra is more powerful than the mind that realises emptiness in the Paramitayana. But why is this so?  
  
Geshe -la: You say that it is due to deity yoga but what makes deity yoga more powerful?  
 
Again someone says: It follows that in the three lower classes of tantra, the path is no faster 
than in the Perfection Vehicle because with regard to their discernm ent comprehending 
emptiness, a difference in the strength by which one takes or does not take desire as the path is not explained.  
 Ngawang Palden: There is no pervasion because the Stages of Mantra states:  
 
The difference in speed between the three lower classes of tantra and the Perfection Vehicle is (the fact that in the three lower tantras) one completes the enlightened conduct through dexterity in many methods such as attaining many common siddhis 
 
Lesson  15           Page 94 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
through relying on the might of deity yoga and repetiti on and from being directly taken 
care of by Buddhas and great Bodhisattvas and by being blessed.  
 
This states that there are quicker paths by means of the common siddhis. It does not state that they are quicker paths by merely the component of having a gre ater strength of discernment 
comprehending emptiness by means of taking desire as the path. (Pg. 13 of the root text )  
 
The author says that there is no pervasion and that the path of the lower classes of tantra is 
faster than the Paramitayana path. Howeve r, the reason for this is not  due to the practice of 
taking desire into as the path but rather it is due to:  
• the attainment of many common siddhis and  
• being taken care of and blessed directly by the buddhas and higher bodhisattvas .  
 
“…the enlightened conduct through dexterity in many methods,”:  The “ enlightened conduct ” 
refers to the bodhisattva’s deeds, i.e., primarily the practice of the six perfections. In the previous lesson, we have seen how bodhicitta is the basis of the conduct and the conduct refers to the six perfections.  
 Again someone says: It follows that through the path of the three lower classes of tantra there is enlightenment without having to rely on three countless great aeons because in those three classes, there is a difference in t he speed of the path in comparison with the Perfection 
Vehicle.  
 Ngawang Palden: There is no pervasion because although in the three lower classes of tantra 
there is a mere difference in the speed of the path in comparison with the Perfection Vehicle, it is stated that there is no difference in the speed of attaining enlightenment in one lifetime or 
attaining enlightenment without having to rely on the countless aeons. The Stages of Mantra states:  
 Since the difference in the speed of attaining enlightenmen t not reliant on practice 
exceeding countless aeons is an attribute of Highest Yoga Tantra, a disciple of the lower classes of tantra relies on entering the two stages of Highest Yoga Tantra to attain that. It is not possible that they attain that merely by their path.  
 
Therefore, when debating the differences in speed of Mantra, one should not hold just (to the attribute of) attaining enlightenment in one life or attaining enlightenment not reliant on practice for countless aeons.  
 And:  
 
Attaining enlighte nment in this life is not established by merely practicing the paths of 
three lower classes of tantra because they do not excel the generation stage of the highest uncommon path of Mantra.  
 The author’s position is that in the three lower classes of tantra , one has to rely on three 
countless great aeons to achieve enlightenment. But earlier on, he had asserted that the path 
 
Lesson  15           Page 95 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
of the three lower classes of Tantra is a quicker path than the Paramitayana path.  There seems 
to be an internal contradiction.  How then is that path a quicker path than the Paramitayana (Perfection Vehicle) path?  
 The author is saying that in the three lower classes of tantra, one needs to rely on the three great countless eons to achieve enlightenment “ because although in the three l ower classes of 
tantra there is a mere difference in the speed of the path in comparison with the Perfection 
Vehicle, it is stated that there is no difference in the speed of attaining enlightenment in one 
lifetime or attaining enlightenment without having to rely on the countless aeons.”  
 He substantiates his reasoning by quoting from the Stages of Mantra i.e., by following the path 
of the three lower classes of tantra, one has to rely on three great countless eons to achieve enlightenment. That means one will not achieve enlightenment by simply relying on the path of the three lower classes of tantra as one must enter the path of the Highest Yoga Tantra.  
 One will not achieve enlightenment by depending on the Paramitayana path alone. One has to enter the tantric path. But one definitely cannot achieve enlightenment by depending merely 
on the path of the three lower classes of tantra. One must definitely enter the path of Highest Yoga Tantra.  
 This is similar to the assertions of selflessness in the four Buddhist tenet systems. One will not 
achieve enlightenment by realising the selflessness by the Vaibashikas (Great Exposition 
School). Likewise, one will not achieve enlightenment by realising t he selflessness taught by the 
Sutra, the Mind Only School and even the Autonomy Middle Way Schools. One can only 
achieve enlightenment by realising the selflessness that is asserted by the Consequence Middle Way School.  
  
So just as we must  realise the v iew of selflessness of the Consequence Middle Way School, in 
order to achieve enlightenment, we must enter into the practice of Highest Yoga Tantra.  
 Answer to student’s query : The meditation on the path that accords with the aspects of the 
four complete purities of the resultant stage is definitely taught in the three lower classes of tantra. But one cannot achieve enlightenment by depending on that meditation alone.  We need to understand:  
• Why one cannot achieve enlightenment by depending on Sutra alone.  
• Why it is necessary to enter the tantric path?  
• Why, to achieve enlightenment, one must definitely practice Highest Yoga Tantra and not 
tantra alone  
 When we know the answer to the first question, then we can say that we really know why tantra is more prof ound than Sutra.  
 When we know the answer to the second question, we will know why of the four classes of tantra, Highest Yoga Tantra is the most profound.  
 1. By merely depending on the three lower classes of tantra, can we achieve enlightenment?  
 
Lesson  15           Page 96 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
2. The Buddh a that we become by practising the three lower classes of tantra, is that a 
Buddha or not?  
3. The five paths and ten grounds taught in the three lower classes of tantra, are they all false?  
4. There is a state of enlightenment that is explained in the three lowe r classes of tantra, does 
that state of enlightenment exist?  
 
It is very clear that there is no way one can achieve enlightenment without entering into 
Highest Yoga Tantra. It is mentioned in the text. The state of enlightenment is characterised by 
the bo dy and mind of the Buddha which are  one entity at the time of the result. In order to 
achieve such a result, at the time of the path, the cause to achieve the result must be an inseparable union of the two causes that will lead to the attainment of such a  state, i.e.,  to 
achieve the truth body and the form body as one entity at the time of the result.  
 The three lower classes of tantra posit the cause for the attainment of that  union of method and wisdom as the yoga of non -dual profundity and clarity. T his is the cause to achieve 
enlightenment. This does not exist in the Paramitayana path. When the three lower classes of tantra look at the Paramitayana path, they said that that path does not have a real cause for achieving this state of enlightenment whi ch is the union of the two bodies. Therefore the 
Paramitayana path is less powerful and why the tantric path is superior to Paramitayana path. We have to analyse however whether the yoga of non -dual profundity and clarity is the 
substantial cause for the a chievement of those two bodies.  
 According to the view of Highest Yoga Tantra, the yoga of non- dual profundity and clarity 
explained in the three lower classes of tantra is not the cause for achieving the Buddha’s body 
and mind because this is a coarse mi nd and therefore it can never act as the cause for the 
Buddha’s body and mind.   We have to relate this to the death process. When we die, it is the subtle mind that moves to the next life because the coarse mind will already be dissolved during the death process. At the 
end it is only the subtle mind that moves from life to life and it is this mind that moves toward enlightenment. The coarse mind can never move to enlightenment.  
 This subtlest mind of clear light is only taught in Highest Yoga Tantra. It is not taught in the Paramitayana and the three lower classes of tantra. This is one of the reasons why one must enter into the path of Highest Yoga Tantra to achieve enlightenment.

LESSON NO: 16   DATE: 14TH APRIL 2009  
 
We have been using the term, “no pervasion” very often, with the assumption that we 
understand its meaning. But apparently this is not the case.  
 
Let us say someone makes a statement: “That person over there has a lot of attachment 
because he talks a lot,” i.e., the reason for believing that person has a lot of attachment is 
because he is very talkative. When we say, “There is no pervasion,” to that  statement, we are 
saying that the reason given –  his talkativeness is proof of his having a lot of attachment -  does 
not stand. “No pervasion” means “It is not necessarily so.” This is because being talkative is not 
necessarily the correct reason or proof  to show that a person has a lot of attachment.  
 
For example, we cannot say, “That person is a woman because that person walks like one.” 
This is not necessarily so because a person who walks like a woman is not necessarily a woman. Therefore we say “Ther e is no pervasion that person is a woman because that person walks like 
a woman”. Likewise, we cannot say, “That person is a man because that person walks like a 
man”.  
 
When we use the words “no pervasion,” we should understand that to mean that the reaso n 
being stated does not necessarily imply that such and such is true. If we say, “That person is a 
sentient being because that person has afflictions,” the reason given is that if you have 
afflictions, you are definitely a sentient being. This is necessarily so. In that case we can say, “There is  pervasion.”  
 This is to give you an idea of what “no pervasion”  and “there is pervasion” means.  
 
********************* 
In the previous lesson, we looked at whether in the three lower tantras, one needs to rely on three countless great eons to achieve enlightenment. The author, Ngawang Palden, says “Yes,” but he also says that the Mantric path is quicker than the Paramitaya na path.  
 
In texts composed by other masters on the tantric stages of the path, it is mentioned that in 
the three lower tantras, one does  not need to rely on three great countless eons to achieve 
enlightenment although the manner of progressing along the paths and grounds is similar to 
the Perfection Vehicle.  
  
These are the two different positions. To support his position, Ngawang Palden quotes from 
the Stages of Mantra.  
 
Since the difference in the speed of attaining enlightenment not reliant on practic e 
exceeding countless aeons is an attribute of Highest Yoga Tantra, a disciple of the lower classes of tantra relies on entering the two stages of Highest Yoga Tantra to attain that. It is not possible that they attain that merely by their path.  
 
 
Lesson  16          Page 98 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
Therefore , when debating the differences in speed of Mantra, one should not hold just 
(to the attribute of) attaining enlightenment in one life or attaining enlightenment not 
reliant on practice for countless aeons.  
 And:  
 
Attaining enlightenment in this life is no t established by merely practicing the paths of 
three lower classes of tantra because they do not excel the generation stage of the highest uncommon path of Mantra. (Pg. 14 of the root text )  
 My personal opinion is that in the three lower classes of tantr a, one does  not need to rely on 
three great countless eons to achieve enlightenment. I think it is better to take this position because there is a difference in the speed of progressing on the path between the Paramitayana and the Mantric paths. The Mantric path is said to be quicker.  
  
This is my personal position but there are many scholars or masters who take different positions and they also have reasons for their stand. This is something we have to think about and analyse for ourselves.   1.2  THE WAY TO POSIT THE FIVE PA THS AND THE TEN GROU NDS FOR THEM  
 
The way to posit those (lower tantras) as five paths and ten grounds exists because there are 
three ways of positing the paths of Action, Performance and Yoga Tantras in five paths and ten grounds.  
 1.2.1  THE WAY OF POSITING THE PATHS OF ACTION TANTRA IN FIVE PATHS  AND TEN 
GROUNDS  
 The Action Tantra Path of Accumulation is the path (ranging) from producing the uncontrived experience of discernment pledging to attain enlightenment through the path of the Action Tantra, having obtained pure empowerment, up to (but not including) attaining insight comprehending emptiness through the strength of meditating on the absorption bestowing emancipation at the limit  of sound. (Pg. 14 of the root text ) 
 Here are  the paths of Action, Performance and Yoga Tantras. How does one initially enter 
those paths? If we look at Action Tantra, how does one initially enter the path of Action Tantra?  
 In the beginning, one needs to receive the pure empowerment in Action Tantr a. When one 
develops the uncontrived thought, “I will achieve enlightenment by depending on the path of Action Tantra,” one enters the path of Action Tantra. You have to know the boundary of when you enter the path of Action Tantra.  When you have the unco ntrived mind generation 
(bodhicitta) specific to Action Tantra that is when you enter the path of Action Tantra. From the time you enter the path of Action Tantra up to just prior to the attainment of the concentration bestowing liberation at the end of so und, that is the Action Tantra Path of 
Accumulation .  
 
Lesson  16          Page 99 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
The Action Tantra Path of Preparation is the path (ranging) from that insight, up to (but not including) perceptual comprehension (direct realization) of emptiness through the strength of having medita ted on that absorption. (Pg. 14 of the root text ) 
 The boundary of the Action Tantra Path of Preparation  starts from the attainment of the 
concentration bestowing liberation at the end of sound up to just before the attainment of the 
direct realisation of emptiness.  
 
Question: By depending on the concentration bestowing liberation at the end of sound, one achieves the union of calm abiding and special insight. If that is the case, then the way you posit the Path of Accumulation should be different.  
 Answer :  The concentration bestowing liberation at the end of sound is the union of calm 
abiding and special insight which is when you achieve the Path of Preparation. Prior to actualising the concentration bestowing liberation at the end of sound, the union of calm abiding and special insight has not  yet been achieved.  
 The preparation starts when you are meditating on calm abiding, that is,  before achieving calm abiding. The purpose of achieving calm abiding is to actualise special insight. You seem to be asking whether there is a gap between calm abiding and special insight. If there is a gap 
between these two, then we have to say that there is a gap between the Path of Accumulation and the Path of Preparation.  
 
When you achieve the concentration bestowing l iberation at the end of sound which is the 
union of calm abiding and special insight, you have already achieved calm abiding. But you need some time to achieve special insight. So that is the gap. That whole period of time, which is the concentration abidi ng in sound, is the Path of Accumulation.  
 The Action Tantra Path of Seeing is the path (ranging) from the perceptual comprehension of emptiness through that absorption, up to (but not including) the generation of the direct antidote to the great of the great afflictive meditation abandonment.  
 The Action Tantra Path of Meditation is the path (ranging) from the absorption at the limit of sound, which is the direct antidote to the great of the great afflictive meditation abandonment, 
up to the point preceding the enlightenment attained through the path of Action Tantra.  
 
The Action Tantra Path of No -More Learning is the enlightened path attained through the path 
of this tantra. ( Pgs. 14 -15 of the root text )  
 The Action Tantra Path of Preparation is a conce ptual realisation of emptiness,  not a direct 
realisation.  
 When the practitioner realises emptiness directly, that is when he achieves the Action Tantra 
Path of Seeing which is the path ranging from the direct realisation of emptiness through that absorption, up to but not including the generation of the direct antidote to the great of the

FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
great afflictive Path of Meditation abandonment.  
 
From those, the first ground is  posited as the Path of Seeing. The second ground is posited 
from the attainment of the direct antidote to the great of the great afflictive meditation -
abandonment. The third ground is posited from the attainment of the direct antidote to the medium of the  great. The fourth ground is posited from the attainment of the direct antidote 
to the small of the great.  
 The fifth, sixth and seventh grounds are posited from the attainment of the direct antidotes to the three cycles of medium affliction
26. The eighth g round is posited from the simultaneous 
attainment of the direct antidote of the three cycles of small affliction.27 Again from the eighth 
ground, great knowledge obscurations are abandoned.  
 
The ninth ground is posited from the attainment of the direct anti dote of the medium 
knowledge obscurations. The tenth ground is posited from the attainment of the direct 
antidote of the small knowledge obscuration. The direct antidote to the subtle small (knowledge obscurations) is posited as the primordial awareness of  the final continuum and so 
forth.  
 
It is necessary to accept these assertions in accordance with the Sutra Consequence system 
because the method of abandoning both obscurations in the three lower classes of tantra is 
not explained in any source to be different from the Perfection Vehicle. Nor is that established 
by logic. (Pg. 15 of the root text ) 
 We had previously covered the sutric paths and grounds in accordance with the Consequence 
Middle Way School in the last module. Before that, I had also taught a class on the paths and 
grounds.  
 With regards to the abandonment of the various types of afflictions and obscurations on the 
paths, what is mentioned in this text is different from what I had covered in those earlier 
lessons on the paths and grounds.  
 
This text explains that at the beginning of the eighth ground, the afflictive obstructions are not 
abandoned. This is different from the sutric paths and grounds where the afflictive obstructions are abandoned upon attainment of the eighth ground. This is an example of the 
difference in the assertions which happens because different scholars have different views regarding how one progresses along the paths and grounds.  
 
The reason for the different explanations is because it is unclear in the texts of the g reat Indian 
masters. There are no explicit explanations in the texts. For example, in Engaging in the Middle 
Way  by Chandrakirti, it states that the afflictive obstructions are abandoned “ from the eighth  
ground” but how are we to interpret this? We can interpret this to mean that the afflictive 
26 The three rounds of the medium: the great of the medium, the middle of the medium and the small of the 
medium.  
27 The three roun ds of the small: the great of the small, the middle of the small and the small of the small

FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
obstructions are abandoned upon attainment  of the eighth ground but it can also mean that 
the afflictive obstructions are abandoned after  one has achieved the eighth  gro und.  
  
Lama TsongKhapa also states in his Illuminating the Intent , which is a commentary on 
Chandrakirti’s text, that the big and medium of the path of meditation abandonments are 
abandoned from the second to the seventh grounds. Again, this statement doe s not explicitly 
mention when the afflictive obstructions are abandoned –  are they abandoned at the 
beginning of the eighth ground or on the eighth ground?  
We should understand that there are different ways of explaining this topic. It is not that this scholar is right and that scholar is wrong. These scholars are all experts but this happens due to 
the lack of explicit explanations in the texts of the great Indian masters.  
 According to this text, the grounds are attained by the uninterrupted paths.  Thi s is another 
point of difference from the sutric paths and grounds which states that only when one achieves the path of thorough release does one achieve that particular ground. In the sutric path, the second ground is achieved after one has achieved the path of thorough release of the second ground but here in the text, one achieves the second ground when one achieves the uninterrupted path of the second ground:  “The second ground is posited from the attainment 
of the direct antidote to the great of the gr eat afflictive meditation -abandonment.”  
 We should compare some of these differences from what we have studied earlier in the sutric 
path. Whether or not you understand what is being said in this particular text depends on 
whether you have a good understan ding of what we have covered earlier. You can then 
compare the differences between the two systems.  
 “It is necessary to accept these assertions in accordance with the Sutra Consequence system because the method of abandoning both obscurations in the thre e lower classes of tantra is not 
explained in any source to be different from the Perfection Vehicle. Nor is that established by logic ” means that the way of abandoning the obstructions to liberation and the obstructions to 
omniscience is the same in both the Perfection Vehicle and in the three lower classes of tantra.  
 1.2.2&3  THE WAY OF POSITING THE PATHS OF PERFORM ANCE AND YOGA TANTRA IN 
FIVE PATHS AND TEN G ROUNDS  
 The Paths of Accumulation of each of these two tantras are the paths ranging from producing 
the uncontrived experience of discernment pledging to attain enlightenment through the paths 
of each of these two, up to (but not including) attaining insight comprehending emptiness through the strength of having meditated on the Yoga without Si gns of each Tantra.  
 The Paths of Preparation of each of these two tantras are the paths ranging from the insight comprehending emptiness, which is the Yoga without Signs of each of these two, up to (but not including) the perceptual comprehension of emptiness. All the rest should be known by applying the former instances of a similar type. (Pg. 15- 16 of the root text ) 
 Entry into the path is similar for both Performance and Yoga Tantras:  
• We enter into the path of Performance Tantra:

FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
o when we have received pure initiation and  
o when we develop the uncontrived bodhicitta with the thought, “I will achieve 
enlightenment by depending on the path of Performance Tantra.”  
• We enter into the path of Yoga Tantra:  
o when we have received pure initiation and  
o when we develop the uncontrived bo dhicitta with the thought, “I will achieve 
enlightenment by depending on  the path of Yoga Tantra.”  
This is when one enters the Path of Accumulation . 
 To that someone says: Take as the subject the paths explained in the Perfection Vehicle such as love, compassion, bodhichitta and so forth, in the continuum of a practitioner on the Path of Accumulation of the lower tantras. It follows that they are paths of lower tantra because one accepts that method of positing the five paths and the ten grounds of these. That is unacceptable because these are not paths of Mantra because they are not uncommon paths of Mantra.  
 Ngawang Palden: There is no pervasion. ( Pg. 16 of the root text )  
 We need to analyse whether the love, compassion, bodhicitta and so forth in the co ntinuum of 
a practitioner on the Path of Accumulation of the lower tantras is a sutric or tantric path or whether it is both, i.e., both a sutric and tantric paths.  
 Again he says: Take that subject, it follows that they are Perfection Vehicle paths becau se they 
are common Perfection Vehicle paths.  
 Ngawang Palden: There is no pervasion because it is similar to the case where though the pristine awareness comprehending the sixteen aspects such as impermanence and so forth, in the continuum of a Bodhisattva is a common intermediate scope path, it is not an intermediate scope path. ( Pg. 16 of the root text)  
 Basically what the text is saying is that the love, compassion, bodhicitta and so forth in the 
continuum of a practitioner on the Path of Accumulation of the lower tantras is a tantric path 
but is not  an uncommon tantric path. It is not a sutric (Paramitay ana) path.  
 The love, compassion, bodhicitta and so forth in the continuum of a practitioner on the Path of Accumulation of the lower tantras is not the Perfection Vehicle path but are paths shared with the Perfection Vehicle.   If it is a path that is sha red with the Perfection Vehicle, is it necessarily a Perfection Vehicle 
path? No, “There is no pervasion because it is similar to the case where though the pristine awareness comprehending the sixteen aspects such as impermanence and so forth, in the conti nuum of a Bodhisattva is a common intermediate scope path, it is not an intermediate 
scope path”.  
 Therefore we can see that the tantric and sutric paths are different.  
• Does the text show that these two paths are mutually exclusive?  
 
Lesson  16          Page 103 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
• Does the text show tha t these two paths have a common locus?  
The text is saying that these two paths are mutually exclusive.  
 
Question: Why is the bodhicitta in the continuum of a practitioner on the Path of Accumulation of the lower tantras a tantric path?  
Answer : Bodhicitta e xists in both the sutric and tantric paths. Here, the text says the bodhicitta 
in the continuum of a practitioner on the Path of Accumulation of the lower tantras is the path that is shared with both sutric and tantric paths. But this bodhicitta is not a s utric path.  
 Is the shared Paramitayana path and the Paramitayana path the same? Is the direct realisation of the 16 aspects of the Four Noble Truths in the continuum of a bodhisattva superior a path of the person of intermediate capacity?  
  
Is there a co mmon locus between the paths of the three lower classes of tantra and the path of 
Highest Yoga Tantra?  
 Question: What is the uninterrupted path? What helps the practitioner to accumulate merit 
that enables him to move from one ground to the next?  
Answer :  In the Paramitayana path, one accumulates the collection of wisdom during 
meditative equipoise. One then arises from that and enters the post -meditative session to 
collect merit . One then alternates between meditative equipoise and the post meditative 
(equipoise) sessions.  
 
During meditative equipoise, the bodhisattva superior focuses on emptiness and can only accumulate the collection of wisdom. He cannot accumulate the collection of merit. He has to arise from that meditative equipoise and enter the post -meditative equipoise session to 
accumulate the collection of merit.  
 We need to analyse whether we need to do that in the tantric path. In the tantric path, the tantric practitioner on the grounds accumulates the collection of wisdom and the collection of 
merit simultaneously . That being the case, there is no reason for that person to arise from 
meditative equipoise.  
 In the sutric path, method and wisdom are conjoined but are not conjoined inseparably. In the 
tantric path, method and wisdom are conjoin ed in one inseparable entity. I have not seen any 
explicit mention in the text of  the need for the tantric practitioner on the grounds to arise from meditative equipoise. My personal opinion is there is no need to do so because both the collection of wisd om and the collection of merit are accumulated together at the same time.  
    Interpreter: Ven. Tenzin Gyurme

LESSON NO: 18   DATE: 19TH APRIL 2009  
 
As before, we will spend five minutes in silent meditation. You can reflect on the meaning on 
some of the verses that we usually recite at the beginning of the class, such as:  
 
Do not commit any non- virtuous actions,  
Perform only perfect virtuous actions,  
Subdue your mind thoroughly  
This is the teaching of the Buddha  
 
Or you can meditate on the verse from the Diamond Cutter Sutra:  “A star, a visual aberration, a 
flame of a lamp…”  and so forth.   
 
There are many things you can reflect on. The point is to tr y to develop a virtuous state of mind 
with a positive motivation for listening to the teachings.  
 
Meditation is extremely important. But if you try to meditate when there is strong attachment 
(desire) or anger manifesting, it will not work because the min d is preoccupied with those 
manifest afflictions. That is why there is the  practice of meditating on the breath, concentrating on the inhalation and exhalation, which helps to bring the mind to a state of 
equilibrium. This is something you have to understand and keep in mind.  
 
At the beginning of your meditation, if you discover that attachment is manifesting strongly, you can get rid of it by reflecting on how the object of attachment is, by nature, unpleasant. If the mind is distracted by many wild disc ursive thoughts, then you have to meditate on the 
breath to stop them.  
 When the mind is in equilibrium and not distracted by the manifest afflictions, then it is much easier to meditate and whatever meditation you do will be successful.  
 It is good to r eflect on the meaning of the verse:  
Do not commit any non- virtuous actions,  
Perform only perfect virtuous actions,  
Subdue your mind thoroughly  
This is the teaching of the Buddha  
 This verse encapsulates all the teachings of the Buddha. The entire Lam Rim is in this verse. If we know how to reflect, it is all in this verse. In order to meditate, we have to know the subject of the meditation well. For example, in order to meditate on Lam Rim, we have to know the stages of the path to enlightenment well. When we know the Lam Rim well, we will be able to 
condense the entire Lam Rim into this verse and meditate on the entire Lam Rim.  
 When we do not know the entire Lam Rim well, it will be difficult to see how this verse encapsulates the entire Lam Rim. We will only be able to generate the thought, “I am not supposed to do anything bad and I am supposed to do only good.” That’s about it. There will be 
 
Lesson  18          Page 106 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
nothing much for us to think about no matter how hard we may try. From this, you can see the 
big difference betwe en knowing and not knowing the subject well.  
 This is something we have to understand as many people think that studying is an obstacle that interferes with our practice. That is wrong. If we really want to meditate, we have to know the subject well. If w e don’t want to meditate, then that is fine but if we really want to practise and 
meditate, acquiring knowledge through studying is the favourable condition that helps us in 
our meditation. We have to understand the importance of studying and then act on i t and put 
effort into our studies.  
 
I mentioned at the last class that at the end of every session, everyone should come up with a question. Since we are meeting for a discussion at 1.30pm today, perhaps everyone can think about how this idea should be implemented. Basically, we are looking at ways to improve the quality of our studies.  
 If people were to submit questions, how should those questions be addressed and handled? We can’t answer every question submitted. How should we answer the simpler queri es?  
 When I say, come up with a question, you shouldn’t submit a question simply for the sake of fulfilling that requirement. It should be a question that you are really interested to know the answer to but you have not been able to find the answer for yo urself. For example, someone 
may approach you with a question you cannot answer. You may submit such a question but it must be a question where you are interested to find out the answer. Otherwise, if there is no sincere interest from your side to ask a go od question and there is no interest from your side in 
the answer, there will be no benefit to this whole exercise. Because without genuine interest from your side, I can give you the answer but we will be back at square one. It is not going to work.  
 So keep this in mind. Squeeze your mind a little and formulate your question carefully and with 
some thought on something you really want to understand. If you can do this, I think it will be helpful.  
 The only reason I am bringing this up is to see how we ca n improve things. There is no other 
reason. And it is something that all of us have to think about –  how we can make things better.
