To that someone says: Take as the subject the paths explained in the Perfection Vehicle such as love,  compassion, bodhichitta and so forth, in the continuum of a practitioner on the Path of 
Accumulation of the lower tantras. It follows that they are paths of lower tantra because one accepts that method of positing the five paths and the ten grounds of the se. That is

The love, compassion, bodhicitta and so forth in the continuum of a practition er on the Path of 
Accumulation of the lower tantras -  are those minds a Paramitayana path or a Tantric path? 
Ngawang Palden asserts those minds are Tantric and not Paramitayana paths.  
  
The text is saying that these two paths –  the Tantric path and the Pa ramitayana path -  are 
mutually exclusive.  
 
If we were to say that those minds of love, compassion, bodhicitta and so forth in the 
continuum of a practitioner on the Path of Accumulation of the lower tantras are both Tantric 
and Paramitayana paths, what will be the consequence?  
 In the Chapter 4 of the Ornament for Clear Realisations , there is a section on knower of paths 
in which there are knower of paths that knows the hearers’ paths, the knower of paths that knows the solitary realisers’ paths and so forth.  Are these two Hinayana or Mahayana paths?  
 From this we can see how important it is, especially when we are studying these more advanced topics that, as we move from one subject to another, we do not forget what  we had 
covered earlier. The teachings are all interrelated. I am not saying you are not putting in the effort to study but this is a source of difficulty. When you can remember what you have studied earlier and as you learn more, you discuss the material further, then that discussion will be very fruitful. I am not saying you are not studying but I am highlighting to you one of the challenges 
due to the busy lifestyle here to the study of these deeper teachings.  
 
The way of attaining enlightenment reliant on those paths:  
If one is a basis for newly attaining enlightenment through the paths of the Perfection Vehicle 
and the paths of the three lower tantras, one must be a basis in the Akanishta Field because it is well known to be like that in the Perfection Vehicle. (Pg. 16 of the root text )  
 Question: Is the basis of Ngawang Palden’s assertion related to the motivation, i.e., the 
strength of the bodhicitta generated?  
Answer : This author is saying that the Tantric path and Paramitayana path are mutually 
exclusive. He also mentions that the path of a person of intermediate capacity and path of a person of great capacity are different and not the same.  
 Is there any difference in the bodhicitta of the Paramitayana path and the bodhicitta of the 
Action Tantric path?  
 
Is there any difference in the mind realising emptiness of the Paramitayana path and Tantric path?  
 Using the Sutric path as the foundation, is there any difference in the hearer’s direct perception of impermanence and the bodhisattva superior’s  direct perception of impermanence?  
 
 
Lesson  18          Page 108 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
When we look at this from one perspective, there may not be any difference. But when we 
discuss this from another perspective, then, there is a difference.  
 The bodhicitta in Tantra is the bodhicitta that is taught in Sutra. This bodhici tta is the main 
mind that is endowed with the two aspirations focusing on the welfare of others and one’s own enlightenment.  
 
It is the same with emptiness because the emptiness that is realised in Tantra is the emptiness 
that is taught in Sutra.  
 According to the Paramitayana, the basis for achieving enlightenment cannot be one in the desire realm but in the Akanishta Field. It is the same with the three lower classes of Tantra.  
 The way of attaining enlightenment in the Action and Performance Tantras:  
 It has already been explained as being similar to the Perfection Vehicle. According to Yoga Tantra, both the masters Shakya Shenyen and Sangye Sangwa explain that when Prince Siddhartha was in equipoise at the supreme limit of the fourth absorption by the bank of the river Neranjana, all the Buddhas of the ten directions awoke him by the sound of snapping their fingers and having inspired him, they took him to the Akanishta Field. There, after bestowing the crown empowerment, they made him meditate se rially on the five -fold mode of 
enlightenment
. At the completion of that process, he attained enlightenment as the Enjoyment 
Body. ( Pg. 16 of the root text)  
 Note: Shakya Shenyen is Shakyamitra in Sanskrit. Sangye Sangwa is Buddhaguhya in Sanskrit.  
 Ther e are three masters – Shakyamitra, Buddhaguhya and Anandagarbha –  who are experts in 
Yoga Tantra. They are well known in both India and Nepal.  
 
According to Shakyamitra and Buddhaguhya, Prince Siddhartha undertook  the practice of 
austerity for six years. At the end of that period, he attained the tenth bodhisattva ground, i.e., 
he was a fully qualified tenth ground bodhisattva. Sitting on the bank of the Neranjana River, 
he entered the meditative absorption called the supreme limit of the four con centrations. At 
that time all the buddhas of the ten direc tions gathered and roused him from that meditative 
stabilisation with the sound of their snapping fingers.  
 
They told him, "You cannot become com pletely and perfectly purified simply  through this 
meditative stabilisation alone.”  
 
He asked, "How then should it be done?"  
 
 
Thereupon, they brought him to Akanishta, leaving behind his coarse body on the bank of the 
Neranjana  River. He went to Akanishta with his mental body . After all the buddhas of the ten 
directions conferred on him the crown initiation, they caused him to cultivate serially the five 
manifest enlightenm ents. At the end of completing the five manifest enlightenments, he 
 
Lesson  18          Page 109 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
achieved enlightenment in the nature of Vairochana. This means that he newly achieved 
enlightenment in Akanishta.  
 
After achieving enlightenment, he taught the Yoga Tantras at the peak of  Mount Meru.  
 
Then, as a buddha, he returned to the bank of the Neranjana and re -entered his coarse body. 
He then performed the deeds of overcoming the maras and the deed of manifesting 
enlightenment.  
 
These are the assertions of these two great masters of Yoga Tantra, Shakyamitra and 
Buddhaguhya, but their assertions are incorrect:  
 
However since that is incompatible with the general system of the Great Vehicle, it is not 
accepted in our system. The St ages of Mantra states:  
 
The Stainless Light and the uncommon Secret Tantra state that our Teacher had 
attained enlightenment before this, and here he demonstrated the way of attaining 
enlightenment. Also the assertion by many other mantric explanations tha t that being is 
an emanation not newly enlightened is similar in both the Mantra and Perfection Vehicles. (Pgs. 16 –  17 of the root text )  
 
We do not accept their assertions and our position can be substantiated with the quotation from 
Lama Tsongkhapa’s St ages of Mantra.  According to our system, Prince Siddhartha was already 
enlightened even before he performed the twelve deeds, i.e., even before he was born. The 
common Mahayana explanation of Shakyamuni Buddha was that he was already a buddha even 
before c oming to this world and performing the twelve deeds.  
 
The Hinayanists assert that Prince Siddhartha was an ordinary being, not a buddha, before his 
birth. He achieved enlightenment only after meditating on and progressing along the paths. We 
tend to accept the Hinayanist assertion because this is what many people have seen. But this is 
incorrect.  
 The twelve deeds are:  
1. Abiding in the abode of Tushita  
2. Transferring from Tushita 
3. Entering the womb of the mother  
4. Being born  
5. The sports of a youth  
6. The deeds of a prince, enjoying the company of a princess  
7. Renunciation  
8. Undergoing austerities  
9. Conquering the maras  
10. Becoming enlightened  
11. Turning the wheel of Dharma  
12. Entering parinirvana

The first six are the deeds of a layperson. The last six are the deeds of a re nunciate, or ordained 
person.  
 
The reason why we do not accept the assertion of Shakyamitra and Buddhaguhya  is because 
their assertions do not accord with the general Mahayana presentation. The correct position is 
as follows:  
 
Thus the master Kunga Nyingpo states that one must accept the explanation that this Teacher 
in his final existence, having accumulated merit for three countless aeons, entered equipoise at the supreme limit of the fourth absorption in the Akanishta Field, and all the Buddhas of the ten directions woke him through the sound of snapping their fingers and so forth, because the Illuminating Lamp states:  
 In the root tantra ( Compendium of Principles ), it only states “When the Bodhisattva 
Siddhartha was seated ne ar the bodhi tree, the Tathagatas inspire him...” without 
clearly mentioning either the Akanishta Field or the Desire Realm. Also in the Vajra Peak, it is explained as being placed in the Dense Array Akanishta Field.(Pg. 17 of the root text)  
 Note: Kunga N yinpo is Anandagarbha in Sanskrit.  
We can understand why different masters will have different interpretations  due to the 
ambiguity in the Compendium of Principles .  
 
Someone says: This method of explanation teaches the method of attaining enlightenment through adding the path of Yoga Tantra at the end of the Perfection Vehicle path.  
 Ngawang Palden: This is unacceptable because in Highest Yoga Tantra it is clearly explained that there are two different ways to attain enlightenment through differentiating one’s own path and the path of others. In Yoga Tantra that is not clearly explained. The Illuminating Lamp states:  
 
As it is explained, one attains enlightenment in two ways. Through entering one’s own path in the system of Highest Yoga Tantra from the beg inning and attaining 
enlightenment in one life like a jewel -like disciple. Alternatively, one adds the mantric 
path at the end of the Perfection Vehicle.  
 In the system of Yoga Tantra applying that previously explained to the Perfection Vehicle through dif ferentiating one’s path and other’s paths does not appear and 
supplementing the way of attaining enlightenment at the end of one’s path does not clearly appear.  
 Therefore I wonder whether those explanations of one’s path such as attaining enlightenment through the Buddhas inspiring one, having completed the ground 
previously explained and so forth, are the system of Yoga Tantra. This should be well examined.  
 
 
Lesson  18          Page 111 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
Therefore I wonder whether one should not assert that in the system of Yoga Tantra, our 
Teacher attained enlightenment through entering that path of Yoga Tantra from the beginning 
and it is not necessary to add the path of Yoga Tantra in attaining enlightenment in the Perfection Vehicle. ( Pg. 17 of the root text)  
 Summary of the correct explanation g iven by master Anandagarbha  
At the end of accumulating the collections for three countless great eons, Shakyamuni Buddha was 
already at the tenth bodhisattva ground. In Akanishta, he entered into the meditative 
absorption called the supreme limit of the fo ur con centrations.  
 
At that time, the buddhas of the ten directions gathered and roused him from that meditative 
stabilisation through the sound of snapping their fingers. They told him, "You cannot become 
completely and perfectly purified through this meditative stabilisation."  
He asked, "Then, how is it done?"  
 
Thereupon, all the buddhas of the ten directions conferred upon him the crown initiation, 
after which they caused him to cultivate serially the five manifest enlightenments.  
 
Upon completing them, he achieved enlightenment in the nature of Vairochana. He then 
proceeded to the peak of Mount Meru, where he taught the Yoga Tantras. He then descended 
into this world and was born as Prince Siddhartha and subsequently performed the twelve 
deeds.  
 
Here you can see that there is a difference between the assertion of Anandagarbha  and those of 
the previous two masters Shakyamitra and Buddhaguhya.  
 
There are five manifest enlightenments which can be considered:  
• from the perspective of the manner in which t he Buddha achieved enlightenment by 
looking at his life story and  
• from the perspective of what the trainees should practise.  
 
1. Prince Siddhartha was conferred the crown initiation by all the buddhas of the ten 
directions who imparted to him the mantra,  citta -prativedham karomi and made him meditate 
on its meaning.  By doing so, he directly realised the suchness (or the factor of natural purity) of 
his own mind in meditative equipoise.  
 
Arising from that meditative equipoise, in the post meditative equipoise, his realisation of the 
suchness of his own mind took the aspect of a moon disc at his heart and he gained the mirror-
like exalted wisdom , the entity of Akshobhya.  
 
Of the five enlig htenments, the name of this first manifest enlightenment is called the manifest 
enlightenment from the individual clear realisation of the mind.  
 
Trainees should also reflect on the meaning of the mantra, citta -prativedham karomi and through 
such reflection, they will come to realise the factor of natural purity of their minds. That 
realisation will likewise transform into the aspect of moon discs (at their hearts).  
 
Lesson  18          Page 112 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
2. Next, Prince Siddhartha was told to meditate on the meaning of the mantra om bodhicittam 
utpadayami. By doing so, in medita tive equipoise, he directly realised the suchness of his mind 
as devoid of adventitious defilements.  Arising from that meditative equipoise, in the post 
meditative equipoise, his realisation of the suchness of his mind took the aspect of a second 
moon disc at his heart and he gained the exalted wisdom of equality , the entity of 
Ratnasambhava. This second  manifest enlightenment is called  the manifest enlightenment 
from generating the mind of supreme enlightenment.  
 
 
Likewise, trainees utter the mantra, om bodhicittam utpadayami  and meditate to realise the 
emptiness of their minds as devoid of adventitious defilements. That realisation transforms 
into the aspect of a second moon discs at their hearts which rest on top of the first moon discs.  
  
3. The buddhas then made Prince Siddhartha meditate on the meaning of the mantra, tistha -
vajra.  By doing so, Prince  Siddhartha  vividly saw the previous all -good mind of enlightenment 
in the aspect of a white five -pointed vajra standing upright on the moon disc at his heart and 
he attained the exalted wisdom of individual analysis , the entity of Amitabha. This manifest 
enlightenment is called the enlightenment from the stable vajra.  
 
When applied to the practice to be done by trainees, the trainees utter tistha -vajra and meditate 
on the white five -pointed vajra at their hearts.  
 
I am not sure whether this white five -pointed vajra is on the second moon disc or the second 
moon disc transforms into the white five -pointed vajra. It is not clear here. My guess is that by 
meditating on the second moon disc, that  completely transforms into the white five -pointed 
vajra.  
 
4. All the buddhas of the ten directions then conferred the name ini tiation on Prince 
Siddhartha after which he was designated as Bodhisattva Vajradhatu. T hey then caused him 
to meditate on the meaning of the mantra, vajra -atmako ham . By meditating on that, the 
vajra -elements of the exalted body,  exalted speech and the exalted mind of all the buddhas 
entirely and manifestly entered into the white five -pointed vajra at his heart, and he di rectly 
saw that vajra as established from the minute vajra particles of all the Ones -Gone -Thus. He 
gained the exalted wisdom of achieving activities , the entity of Amoghasiddhi. This manifest 
enlight enment is called the manifest enlightenment from the vajra essence.  
 
When this is app lied to the practice of the trainees, they utter vajra -atmako ham  and meditate 
on the white five -pointed vajra at their hearts emitting rays of light in the ten directions, 
through which the vajra- elements of exalted body, the vajra -elements of exalted speech, and the 
vajra- elements of exalted mind of all the Ones -Gone -Thus entirely enter into the white five -
pointed vajra at their  hearts.  
 
5. All the buddhas of the ten directions caused Bodhi sattva Vajradhatu to meditate on the 
meaning of the mantra om yatha sarva- tathagatas tatha 'ham.  Through that meditation, the 
vajra and moons at his heart were thor oughly transformed and manifest as the Complete 
 
Lesson  18          Page 113 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
Enjoyment Body, Ma havairochana, adorned with the thirty -two major marks and eighty signs, 
becoming completely and perfectly buddhafied. He attains the exalted wisdom of the sphere 
of reality , the entity of Vairochana. This manifest enlightenment is called the manifest 
enlig htenment from the equality of all the Ones -Gone -Thus.  
 
With regards to the trainees, they utter  om yatha sarva- tathagatas tatha 'ham   and the vajras as 
well as the moons at their hearts transform into the exalted body of Vairo chana. The trainees 
meditate  on this.  
 
According to the system of Yoga Tantra, this is how the Buddha achieved enlightenment. Within 
Yoga Tantra, we follow the assertion of Anandagarbha. The Buddha achieved enlightenment in 
Akanishta where he meditated and serially cultivated  the fi ve manifest enlightenments in the 
manner I have just explained.  
 
Looking at the five manifest enlightenments in a simplified manner, the process for achieving 
enlightenment in Yoga Tantra is:  
• One meditates on the emptiness of the mind  
• The emptiness of the mind then transforms into the aspect of the moon disc.  
• One then meditates on the emptiness of the mind as  devoid of the adventitious defilements.  
• That realisation transforms into the aspect of a second moon disc.  
• On top of that, one meditates on the wh ite five -pointed vajra at the heart  
• The white five -pointed vajra at the heart emits rays of light in the ten directions which then 
come back to the heart  
• Then the moon discs and the white five -pointed vajra transform into  the body of the deity.

Answer to student’s query :  According to the Autonomy Middle Way School and the schools 
below it, the emptiness of the mind is not a true cessation and is not an ultimate truth. It is a 
conventional truth.  
 
According to the Consequence Middle Way School, however, true cessation is none other than 
the emptiness of the m ind. When the emptiness (suchness) of the mind is purified of the 
adventitious defilements, that mind is true cessation.  
 
I am telling you this based on my personal observation. This is not based on any text.  Looking at 
the above process, the emptiness o f the mind and the emptiness of the mind devoid of the 
adventitious defilements are strongly connected. The first and second emptinesses of the mind 
are closely related and both become moon discs. If the first is a moon disc and the second a sun 
disc, ther e seem to be no connection between the two. True cessation is not something that is 
separated from the mind.  
 
The five manifest enlightenments also occur in Highest Yoga Tantra but their meaning is totally 
different from what we have discussed here. For example, restraining life -force exertion occurs 
in both lower Tantra and Highest Yoga Tantra but their meanings are totally different.  
 
Question: If Prince Siddhartha was already a Buddha and his spiritual journey was a display for 
our benefit, then how about the story of how the Buddha first generated compassion as a hell 
being? Was that also a display?  
 
Lesson  18          Page 115 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
Answer : At that time, he was a truly qualified hell being and wasn’t a buddha then. This account 
shows how much hardship was endured and how hard he practised to become a buddha, the 
effort put into generating bodhicitta.  
 
If we were to assert that everything is a display, then that means that right from the very 
beginning, he was a buddha already. What is the problem with such an assertion? If that was so, 
then there is no need for practice because he is already a buddha.

LESSON NO: 19   DATE: 21ST APRIL 2009  
 
(Geshe -la taught this class in English in the absence of the interpreter )  
 
My main purpose for having this class is to give some kind of understanding to those of you who have not grasped the basic points (of what we  have covered so far). Maybe this class will 
be helpful for you, (using) my simple English to give you some simple understanding.   
 
After having been here for almost ten years, this is the first time in the Basic Program that I am 
trying to talk to you di rectly (in class). Due to my pronunciation or grammer, some of you may 
have difficulty in understanding me. In case you don’t understand what I am trying to say, you 
can ask. You are my guinea pig and I am your guinea pig!  
 
I am not going to cover anythin g new today so don’t expect any new information. I will be 
repeating some of the old material. But when I say that and if you don’t pay attention to what I 
am going to say, then there is no point to this. You must pay attention.  
 
You may close your eyes, thinking that you know all this. You shouldn’t do that as sometimes 
you can get some meaning even from hearing the same words. It is just like reading a book. When you read the Lam Rim  for the first time, you will get some meaning from it but when you 
read  the same page the second time, you will get a different meaning. The same applies to 
listening to the teachings.  
 If you really pay attention, you will get something from this session. Don’t think, “I know this stuff” and then shut off your mind. Such an attitude is  not so helpful. It is good to listen.  
 If I run out of things to say, I will see what we can do. If I don’t run out of things to say, I will continue. It is very difficult for me to plan what I am going to say. I cannot plan for an hour talk . 
I am very bad in that. I can think of what (I am going) to talk about for only ten minutes. After ten minutes, I don’t know what I am going to say. Some people are very good in their planning such as ten minutes on this (topic), ten minutes on that but f or me, I don’t have that kind of 
experience.  
 
In the sutra, Dependent Arising, Buddha Shakyamuni mentions that:  
Whoever sees dependent arising,  
That person sees the Dharma.  
Whoever sees the Dharma,  
That person sees the Buddha.  
 The dependent arising that Buddha Shakyamuni talks about here can be understood in many 
different ways, at the gross level and at the subtle level. Dependent arising is the very basis for Buddhism. For example, when the Buddha taught the Four Noble Truths -  the truth of suffering 
and the truth of cessation –  that is dependent arising at the very gross level.  All sufferings 
come from and depend on a cause. Cause and effect are related to each other. Because there is 
 
Lesson  19          Page 117 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
a cause, one gets the result (effect). You suffer because there is a cause. Dependent on that 
cause, you experience suffering.  
 When you understand the relationship between cause and effect, that they depend on each other, you will understand karma. The main thing is to understand karma. Through that understanding, you will try to engage in virtue and avoid negative actions. When you do that, you are purifying negative karma and creating merit. All virtuous things come from merit. We 
experience negative karma from the bad things. When we understand this, then we will put  
effort into practising virtues and avoiding negativities. And once we do that, we accumulate 
merit.  
 Here is one of the meanings of the line, “ Whoever sees dependent arising,/That person sees the 
Dharma.”  Dharma means understanding the law of karma. This line can be understood in that 
way. When you understand the Dharma, you understand how karma works and you will engage in the accumulation of merit. Through that, you achieve higher rebirth and the Buddha (buddhahood).  
 When we say, “Buddha,” that can als o be understood in different ways. The Buddha can be 
divided into the form body and the truth body. Here, when you relate it to merit, we can understand Buddha refers to the form body.  
 
When you understand dependent arising, then you see the Dharma. When you understand the 
Dharma, you achieve the Buddha. What that means is that when you understand dependent arising, you understand cause and effect and through that, you will understand karma. That 
means you understand the Dharma and through that, you achiev e the Buddha. Through the 
accumulation of merit, you achieve the form body  of the Buddha. That is one way to 
understand (dependent arising).  
 Another way you can understand dependent arising, at a deeper level, is that it is not only cause and effect. It is that all existing things are dependent on something else. They cannot exist by themselves. They must depend on something else to exist.  
 Even the result depends on the cause and the cause depends on the result. Why is there a cause? It is because of the result. Why is there a result? It is because of the cause. When you think about this, (you will see that) all existing things depend on something else other than 
themselves to exist. They cannot  exist by themselves.  
Whatever exists depends on imputation, how the mind labels it. For example, the mind gives 
the label, the name “Geshe Chonyi.” “Geshe Chonyi” doesn’t exist from its own side. If “Geshe Chonyi” exists from its own side, then when  I go to Orchard Road, everybody should recognise me as Geshe Ch onyi.  
 When you talk about dependent arising, that is the subtle dependent arising. When you understand that, then you understand the Dharma. That means you really understand the reality of existence and how things exist. That is the real wisdom and when y ou have that 
wisdom, then ignorance will definitely be removed.  
 
 
Lesson  19          Page 118 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
Here, you see the Dharma means you see the reality of things, i.e., how they  exist. Once you 
understand that, you are accumulating wisdom which means that you are practising the Dharma and you will achieve the Buddha at the end of the day. Through that, you will achieve the truth body  (or the wisdom body) of the Buddha.  
 So here are two different ways to understand the verse:  
Whoever sees dependent arising,  
That person sees the Dharma.  
Whoever sees the Dharma,  That person sees the Buddha.  
Method and wisdom can be understood in that way.   We can also relate it to the two truths. Whatever exists is either a conventional existence (conventional truth) or ultimate existence (ultimate truth). They exist because the two truths are always there.  
 Because of conventional existence, therefore, there is good karma, bad karma, suffering and happiness. When you understand conventional existence, you can relate to it and accumulate merit.  
 When you look at conventional existence, at happiness or suffering and if you really search for 
what is happiness and what is suffering, you will not be able to find them. You can look deeply 
into them but you will not be able to find suffering or happines s and you cannot find the 
person. What you do find, in the end, is emptiness. That is the ultimate truth.  
 When you realise that suffering or happiness is merely imputed by  the mind (merely labelled by the mind), you will not find true suffering when you  search for it. That means you 
understand the reality of suffering and the reality of happiness. That is the wisdom that understands the reality of suffering and  happiness. Because of ultimate truth, you gain the realisation of wisdom and through that, yo u achieve the Buddha’s truth body.  
 When I explained the first meaning for “ Whoever sees dependent arising,/That person sees the 
Dharma,” I related that to karma. Understanding how karma works, we know we suffer 
because of bad karma. In exactly the same w ay,   other people suffer due to their negative 
karma. Through our understanding of karma, this helps us to generate some kind of 
compassion for others. Otherwise, it is very difficult for you to generate compassion for others 
when  you yourself do not understand how you suffer because of karma. When you really understand (the workings of) karma, then that really helps you to develop compassion for others, how they are the same as us, how they are suffering because of their karma.   
 When you relate to depe ndent arising in a subtle way, (you see how) all things exist not by 
depending on how you see them but they exist in dependence on how you label them. When you understand reality, you can see why other people have so much suffering. When you understand rea lity - how things exist -  then you can understand how all sentient beings are so 
ignorant, not being able to see the reality of the object but just believing in whatever they see and thinking that that is true.  When you understand subtle dependent arising , then you 
 
Lesson  19          Page 119 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
understand the reality of existence and from there, you can see how sentient beings suffer. 
Through that, you develop great compassion towards others Through the wisdom that realises reality, it helps you to generate compassion and make that com passion stronger.  
 In this way, wisdom and method support each other. Wisdom makes bodhicitta stronger. When your bodhicitta is stronger, your understanding of reality is also stronger.  
 
That is the most difficult thing to understand –  how things exist. When you understand the 
reality of the object, then there would be fewer problems and less suffering. We all suffer and 
are unhappy because we believe in what appears to us, in what we see. We don’t really understand reality. We don’t really understand the  person. We look at things in a (certain) way, 
believe in that and then decide that is the way (things are). All the problems start from there. When we understand the reality of all things including the person, it will make our life easier with less proble ms and suffering and more happiness.    
 Therefore, it is important to understand the two truths. Our understanding of reality is almost next to nothing. We don’t understand anything about the object at all. That’s why we suffer continually in samsara again and again and we create so much negative karma. Whatever we see, we believe it to be 100% true but we are, in fact, a 100% wrong. We suffer because we are following the wrong mind.  
 
Based on the two truths, there are two methods. Because of the two methods, we achieve the 
two Buddha’s bodies, the truth body and the form body.  
 
When the Buddha gave Hinayana teachings, he did not really mention buddhahood but he  talked only about nirvana. After nirvana, then that is the end of suffering. He did not explain that after the Hinayana Path, one has to enter the Mahayana path because the Buddha taught  according to the level of the students.   The Hinayanists want to be free of suffering and (to achieve that), they want  nirvana. They have this kind of attitude . The Buddha never mentioned to the Hinayanists that after nirvana, 
they have to enter the Mahayana path because he knows that they are not suitable for such Mahayana teachings. That is why, in the Hinayana teachings, the Buddha does not mention that after  one becomes an arhat, one has to enter the Mahayana path.  
 
But, in reality, whether one has to enter the Mahayana path or not, that is another question. 
This does not mean that one does not need to enter the Mahayana path just because the Buddha did not mention it in the sutras. In reality, an arhat has not yet achieved enlightenment 
and he has to enter the Mahayana path.   In the same way, with regards to the Sutra vehicle, the Buddha didn’t mention to the Sutra disciples that after one has achieved a ce rtain level, one has to enter the Tantric path. In reality, 
one has to enter the Tantric path after one has completed the Sutric path. Basically, what the Buddha teaches depends on the students’ level. He guides them on the path according to the kind of be liefs they have.  
 
 
Lesson  19          Page 120 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
It is important that everyone should know why you need to enter the Tantric path and why the 
Sutric path alone cannot lead to enlightenment. It is very important to understand this. We are so used to the Sutric path and it is difficult for us to accept that through the Sutric path, we cannot get enlightened. We must enter the Tantric path.  
 My point is that, here, today, one thing you must understand, with logic, is this very simple thing: Why you must enter  the Tantric path. Also, what  are the differences between Sutra 
and Tantra ? Today, I think my main message is these two (points).  
 As I have also mentioned before, (from your side) you must have some kind of interest to know, from your heart, so when someone explains this, it will re main in your mind for a long time. If 
you have studied here for 10 or 20 years and after having studied Tantra for six months, imagine someone asks you, “What is Tantra?” If you are unable to explain this in a simple way, how will you feel -  happy or unhappy? If you are happy with saying, “I don’t know,” then that is 
fine. But we should not be happy with that. That is my point. We should understand this simple thing. This is my purpose of deciding to teach these two classes without a interpreter  as I 
though t it may be helpful for those who want to learn this simple thing.  
 We think that by practising the Sutric path, we can get enlightened. We can get the two bodies through the collection of merit and wisdom. Why do we need Tantra?  
 
● When we talk about emptiness, we talk about the understanding of the reality of things. That 
understanding and explanation of reality, as explained by Nagarjuna in the Sutric vehicle, is 
exactly the same in both Sutra and Tantra. There is no difference.  
 ● Bodhicitta is also exactly the same in both the Sutric and Tantric vehicles: it is to achieve 
enlightenment for the benefit of all sentient beings.  The entity of bodhicitta and emptiness is the same in both the Sutric and Tantric vehicles. It is the co mmon practices for both the Mahayana Sutra and the Mahayana Tantra vehicles to 
practise bodhicitta and to realise emptiness.  
 
• In both the Sutric and Tantric paths, in order to achieve the form body and truth body, we 
must have the two collections of method and  wisdom.  
 
• Generating bodhicitta and the practice of the six perfections are also the same in both Sutra 
and Tantra.  
 In the Sutric path, it is mentioned that the collection of merit is achieved through the generation of bodhicitta and the practice o f the six perfections accompanied by strong prayers. 
Through this accumulation of merit, we achieve the Buddha’s form body.  
  
How does one achieve the truth body? Through meditating on emptiness and realising emptiness directly.  Through that, the mind ca n transform into the truth body. When the 
Buddha meditates on emptiness, he sees reality without any dualistic appearance. In order to achieve that kind of buddha mind, you must have the cause. In Sutra, there is a wisdom directly realising emptiness that doesn’t have dualistic appearance, i.e., there is no differentiation 
 
Lesson  19          Page 121 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
between the subject and object. They become one. The Sutra explains it in that way. That kind 
of mind can be the cause to achieve the Buddha’s truth body.   The wisdom realising emptiness  can be a cause for the Buddha’s truth body. But in Sutra, they 
cannot clearly explain the real cause of the form body. The question is whether the generation of bodhicitta and the practice of the six perfections coupled with strong prayers can become the cause of the form body?  
 Tantra offers a deeper and better explanation of the cause of the form body, which Sutra is unable to do. The practice of deity yoga (in Tantra) where we visualise ourselves as the deity, this meditation is a similar  cause for achi eving the Buddha’s form body. You see the method is 
different – practising deity yoga is a similar cause to get the Buddha’s form body but Sutra does 
not have that, the same kind of cause to achieve the form body.  
 All bodhisattvas wish to reach enlighten ment for the sake of others. When we talk about the 
truth body and the form body, the bodhisattvas’ main aim is to achieve the form body so that he can help numberless sentient beings. The truth body cannot do that as sentient beings are unable to see it.  
 So the main goal in both Sutra and Tantra is to achieve the form body but Tantra offers a better cause and explanation on how to do this, i.e., the practice of generating oneself as the deity. 
The method of achieving the form body is different in Sutra a nd Tantra. Tantra is more 
profound. The details are much clearer in Highest Yoga Tantra.  
 
When we talk about the form body, it is also not so clear in the lower classes of Tantra even 
though there is the practice of deity yoga. Even in Highest Yoga Tantra , there is the appearance 
of the deity during the generation stage but it is not clear whether this deity is the cause of the form body or not.    
The real cause of the form body can be explained only  by the completion stage when you 
achieve the illusory body (in Highest Yoga Tantra). I am not going to talk about this today but in Highest Yoga Tantra, the explanation about this is very clear. That is the reason why, finally, you must  practise Highest Y oga Tantra to achieve enlightenment.  
 What is the difference between Mahayana Sutra and Mahayana Tantra? Both have the same 
goals for achieving the form body but the method  for achieving  the form body is different. 
Why is there deity yoga in Tantra? Beca use that is the cause for achieving the form body. It is 
very important you understand this and keep this in mind.  
 
Also, in Sutra, when we talk about wisdom and method, it cannot be explained how they can exist within one mind. (In Sutra), wisdom and met hod are separate minds and cannot be 
combined within one mind although they do support one another.  
 Tantra explains how wisdom and method can be combined in one mind. Why is this so important - to have wisdom and method in one mind?  If you separate them , what will be the 
problem? Because when you achieve buddhahood, the Buddha’s form body and truth body are 
 
Lesson  19          Page 122 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
one entity. Therefore when one gets enlightened, the body and mind become one entity. In 
order to achieve that, you must have a cause (similar to the  result).   
 So we need to understand this:  
• That Tantra has a better method that provides a cause (similar to the result) for achieving 
the form body; and  
• Tantra shows how method and wisdom can be combined within one mind.  
These two points are a simple w ay of understanding the difference between Sutra and Tantra, 
why we need deity yoga, why the Sutra Path alone cannot achieve enlightenment and why we must practise Tantra. So my answer is there. Now you should have a clear idea of the necessity of entering  the Tantric path and why you must practise Highest Yoga Tantra to achieve 
enlightenment.   
 What is Tantra (or mantra)? “Mantra,” in Sanskrit means the mind. “Tra” means “protection.” Tantra or mantra literally means protection of the mind. What are we protecting the mind from? We are protecting the mind from ordinary appearance and grasping.  
 There is the explanation of deity yoga in Tantra, the generation of yourself as a deity. The question may arise: Can you transform yourself into a deity? When we th ink about the reality 
of the person and based on that understanding, actually there is nothing there – the person is 
empty. From  my side, when I look at Geshe Chonyi, I cannot find it. It is empty. But within emptiness, there are different manifestations at different levels – a monk, a geshe and so forth. 
Therefore, within emptiness, one can appear as a deity.  
 
Tantra explains that to clear your ordinary appearance is the method to generate yourself and other beings as deities. It is only through the stro ng  appearance of oneself and others as 
deities that we can stop our ordinary appearance and grasping.  
 It is helpful for us to develop that kind of attitude and that kind of mind. All problems arise because we see everyone as ordinary, as worse than us. We don’t see other people as perfect. When we see others as perfect, we will not get angry or upset. Once we generate ourselves and others as deities, we develop clear appearance of all sentient beings and that can stop suffering.  
 For the same reason, Tantra explains meditating on our environment as a pure land or Buddha’s palace and seeing the reality of desire objects as good objects. When we meditate in this way, our environment will not give us any problems, our sense enjoyments will not be sources of  problems but instead, transformed into something that can give us some real 
pleasure and happiness. It is also explained that whatever things we use, we should think of them as how Buddha would experience these things.  
 
There is another practice related to how the Buddha helps sentient beings. When we meditate and recite the mantra, we can visualise light rays radiating from ourselves and touching all sentient beings and purifying their negative karma. Then we absorb the light rays back into ourselves. Th at practice also helps to create the cause for enlightenment and to be able to help 
all sentient beings.  
 
Lesson  19          Page 123 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
Everything is very detailed in Tantra. Everything comes from the mind. Nothing comes from outside. When we see an object and we label it with our ordi nary mind, when we label it as bad, 
that is how we will experience it and we suffer. When we label it as good, we have some kind of happiness. But that is how we label things, not using our wisdom but with our deluded minds. We only see part of the object and not the whole picture. We may see only 1% of that 
object but we believe in  what we see with our (deluded) minds. We do not understand reality 
and we really suffer due to this lack of understanding.  
  
In Tantra, the method is to understand the reality of the object and its ultimate truth. When we 
understand the ultimate truth, our minds can manifest in different ways. When the mind is good, then everything becomes good. What we see depends on what kind of mind we have. It 
is difficult to see all objects as blissful objects because at our level, we do not have that kind of mind to see them as such. For us, it is only at the level of imagination. If we really practise Tantra, particularly Highest Yoga Tant ra that (has the practice of) generating great bliss and so 
forth, etc, when we have this kind of wisdom, we will see things in a different way. It will be  possible to see all objects as blissful. For us, at this moment, we do not see them like that becau se we do not have that kind of mind.  
That is why Tantra explains how to meditate on oneself as a deity, how to meditate on one’s environment as a pure land, how to think that all objects are blissful and how to help others before one gets enlightened. Tantra teaches the four complete purities  so that one can 
meditate on them before one gets enlightened. That is Tantra. But you won’t find this of method of meditating on yourself as a deity in Sutra. That is the difference between Sutra and Tantra.  
 What I w ant you to know today is:  
• Why you need to enter Tantra to get enlightened  
• Why Tantra is better than Sutra  
 When we get to the next section on Highest Yoga Tantra, we will explain all this in greater detail. We will then have a better understanding of how  deity yoga leads to the form body in 
Highest Yoga Tantra in its discussion of the illusory body -  where it comes from, what it looks 
like and so forth. Now, it is difficult to understand how deity yoga can lead to the form body. Even the three lower class es of Tantra cannot really explain how deity yoga is the cause of the 
form body as they cannot explain this in terms of the subtle winds and subtle mind.  
 Sutra never talks about using the attachment (desire) and transforming it into the path. In Hinayana, the three poisons are to be totally abandoned. In the Mahayana Sutra vehicle, anger and ignorance are seen as not being beneficial but although attachment is to be abandoned, it is not totally bad as it can be helpful in some ways when put into the practice.  
 In Tantra, attachment is not only helpful but can be transformed into the path. Buddha does not teach Tantra to everyone but only to those who are capable of managing it. He does not teach Tantra to (practitioners of) the Hinayana or Mahayana Sutra but only to the special 
disciples who can transform desire into the path. So, for the Buddha to teach Tantra, there must be special students who are capable of transforming desire into the path. Perhaps, we are 
 
Lesson  19          Page 124 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
not such suitable students.  
 
Historically, King Bimbisara requested the Buddha, “I cannot abandon my entourage. I have to look after them but I also want to get enlightened. Is there any method for me to do so?”  Buddha gave him the method, Tantra. Buddha manifested the form of a Highest Yoga Tantra deity with  consort and taught the king how to practise Tantra. From the king’s side, he was a suitable disciple. With this tantric practice, the king can still keep his wives and children, take 
care of the kingdom and also get enlightened.  
 
It is importa nt to study Highest Yoga Tantra to get some understanding of it. Unless we do this, 
it will be difficult for us to explain how desire can be transformed into the path.

LESSON NO: 20   DATE: 23RD APRIL 2009  
(Geshe -la taught this class in English in the absence of the interpreter )  
 
As usual, we will do five minutes of silent meditation. If you think you have nothing to meditate 
on, it is good to review what was covered in the last class. This is just a sugges tion.  
 
• In the last class, I had mentioned that:   
Whoever sees dependent arising,  
That person sees the Dharma.  
Whoever sees the Dharma,  That person sees the Buddha.  
It is important to understand the meaning of this verse. When you think about it, it can be 
explained in many different ways.  
• In the last class, I also talked about the differences between Sutra and Tantra which you 
should remember.  
• You also need to know why, in order to get enlightened, you need to practise Highest Yoga 
Tantra.  
You should un derstand these three very basic things.  If someone were to ask you about them, 
you should be able to answer that person.  
 
An easy answer for the difference between the Sutra and Tantra is that in order to get 
enlightened, there are different methods and Tantra and Sutra are differentiated by method, 
not by wisdom. Tantra has a better method than Sutra.  
 
All sentient beings have the potential to get enlightened. In the Hinayana tradition, it does not 
mention that everyone has the potential to be liberated - some can and some can’t. However, 
when we look at the Mahayana vehicle, it is clearly stated that every  sentient being has the 
potential to get enlightened. In Tantra, this is definite.  
 So it is important for us to understand the potential we have and how all sentient beings 
possess this potential for enlightenment. What is that potential and when did it begin? As soon 
as sentient beings exist, the potential was there.  
 In the previous m odule on the Tathagata Essence , we discussed buddhanature. In Sutra, it is 
mentioned that buddhanature is the potential to get enlightened. Every sentient being 
possesses this buddhanature and there is no sentient being who cannot get enlightened. Sutra 
explains that the clear mind is the potential to get enlightened.  
What does the clarity of mind means? We can understand this in different ways. The clear mind has a kind of energy (that is) clear and knowing. One way to understand that is that the mind its elf is empty and not inherently existent. The mind itself is clear. Its nature is emptiness. 
We should understand two things here:  
• every sentient being has this clear mind and  
• the nature of that clear mind is emptiness.  
 
 
Lesson  20          Page 126 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
Based on the two truths, the mind is the conventional truth and its emptiness is the ultimate 
truth. These two exist whenever sentient beings exist. That is the basis or the seed for one to 
attain enlightenment.  
 The clear mind then slowly transforms into the wisdom truth body –  the Buddha’s mind. When 
we talk about the Buddha, there are many different things -  the Buddha’s mind, the Buddha’s 
body, the Buddha’s speech and so forth. When we talk about true cessation, the Buddha 
possesses true cessation that is free of negative karma. What is the cause for that? It is the 
emptiness of the mind that transforms into the Buddha’s true cessation.  
 Mahayana Sutra explains the buddha potential in each sentient being in that everyone possesses the clear mind and the emptiness of that clear mind. Th ese two are the causes to 
achieve enlightenment as one transforms into the Buddha’s mind and the other transforms into the Buddha’s true cessation.  
 From the Tantric viewpoint, the question arises as to what does the Sutric vehicle posit to be the cause o f the Buddha’s body? The Sutric vehicle has no clear explanation for that as it can 
only explain the clear mind and its emptiness transforming into the Buddha’s (mind).   But the Buddha has two bodies: the truth body and the form body. Sutra can only expla in the 
cause of the truth body but not the cause of the form body. Do all sentient beings possess the 
cause for the form body? It is difficult to say this is so from the Sutric point of view. In Tantra, 
however, especially Highest Yoga Tantra, each and eve ry sentient being has the causes for the 
Buddha’s form body and truth body.  
 What is the cause for the form body from the Tantric viewpoint? Amongst the (classes of) Tantra, only the Highest Yoga Tantra gives the final view that explains that all  sentient beings 
have the subtle wind and subtle mind. The subtle wind is the cause for the form body. The Sutric (view) cannot explain clearly how all sentient beings possess the cause for the form body. That is the reason why Tantra is more profound as it can exp lain how each and every sentient 
being possesses the cause for the form body and truth body. Only the Highest Yoga Tantra can explain this very clearly. Even the three lower classes of Tantra cannot explain so clearly the cause for the form body. That is w hy Highest Yoga Tantra is the best.  
 What does receiving an initiation really mean and how do we do so? What is the point of 
receiving initiations? What difference does it make whether one receives an initiation or not?  
As explained earlier, we all have the potential to get enlightened. An initiation is like planting a 
seed in the field for it to grow. The field alone is not enough. We already have the potential but 
in order for that to grow, we need to put in the seed by leaving an imprint in the mental continuum. That is the meaning of initiation, i.e., leaving an imprint on the mental continuum and also to awaken the clear mind.  
 When we talk about the lower classes of Tantra, such as Action Tantra, we have two initiations: the water initiation and the  crown initiation. Sometimes, five initiations are given: the water, 
crown, vajra, bell and name initiations but normally only two initiations are given.  
 
 
Lesson  20          Page 127 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
When the vajra master gives the water initiation, the vase is placed on your head and you drink 
the water from the vase. At that time, when you are drinking the water, you need to think, “By 
drinking this water, I am receiving the blessing,” and generate great bliss and a happy mind. This is one type of initiation which transforms your mind into a happy one (through the action of drinking the water and having the vase placed on your head). If you experience that, that means you have received some kind of initiation.   
At the same time, the happy mind leaves an imprint in the mental continuum. That is another 
initiation.  
 As mentioned in the text, when the master gives initiation to 100 people, those 100 people will receive 100 different kinds of initiations. Not everyo ne receives the same initiation. From the 
master’s side, he is giving one initiation but what kind of initiation the disciples receive depends totally on the mind of the disciple, i.e., if one does not generate any bliss or pay attention (during the initia tion), one does not receive any initiation (at all).  
 For one to receive an initiation, one must have some kind of a joyful mind. Very good students or disciples can generate real bliss in their minds during an initiation. That is the real initiation, the real path to enlightenment.  
 For us, we just imagine by thinking, “I have received (the initiation)” and have a happy mind. By 
transforming your mind into a happy state at that moment –  that is the initiation and at the 
same time, it leaves an imprint in  the mental continuum. If you don’t think of anything and 
from your heart, there is no devotion, no faith, then you do not receive the initiation simply by 
drinking the water or having the vase touch your head.  
 
So in order for you to receive the initiation or leave some imprint in your mind, from your side, you must have full devotion. In order to practise Tantra, you need to leave some imprints in your mind. That is one of the reasons you need to receive an initiation. It is not enough that you have the potential (clear mind) to get enlightened. You need to have some imprints in that 
clear mind. In Tantra, you need to have an initiation to attain enlightenment. `  
  As explained in Action Tantra, when the water initiation is given, it purifies your negativ e 
emotions and leaves an imprint in your mental continuum to achieve the Buddha’s truth body 
and also gives you the permission to practise the Tantric path.  
 
When we talk about the lower classes of Tantra, we have two paths:  
• the Yoga with Signs and  
• the Yoga without Signs.  
By receiving the water initiation, it allows you to practise the path of Yoga without Signs.  
 After that, the crown initiation will be given. When the vase touches your head, you generate a happy mind. This is how you receive the initiation. The crown initiation leaves the imprint in your mental continuum to achieve the Buddha’s form body and it also gives you the permission to meditate on the path of Yoga with Signs.  
 
Lesson  20          Page 128 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
Sometimes the vajra, bell and name initiations are given in the lo wer classes of Tantra. You 
then receive a total of five initiations.  
• When the vajra initiation is given, it leaves an imprint in the mental continuum to achieve 
the Buddha’s wisdom body.  
• When the bell initiation is given, it leaves an imprint in the mental continuum to achieve the 
Buddha’s perfect speech.  
• When the name initiation is given, it leaves an imprint in the mental continuum to achieve 
the Buddha’s perfect name. (The Buddha’s perfect name means that by reciting that Buddha’s name, it can purify eons of negative karma).  
 The purpose of receiving an initiation is to leave an imprint in our mental continua. We have the clear mind but we do not have the imprints.  It is just like having a nice field without any seeds in it. We need to have the seeds in order to have crops. But just leaving imprints in our 
mental continua without doing anything else is also not enough. We will need to wait for eons and eons to get enlightened.  
 What should we do after having received the initiations? We need to make the imprint function just like we need to cultivate the crops. That is the reason for the paths of Yoga with Signs and Yoga without Signs. In order for the seeds to grow to give you nice fruits for you to enjoy, you need the field, water, manure, the sun a nd other conditions. It is the same thing 
here. You need to practise with many conditions, such as Yoga with Signs and Yoga without 
Signs, after having the imprint in your mental continuum.  
 
How do you practise Tantra after having received the initiation? By generating yourself as a 
deity. How you do that? Whenever you look at a Tantra text such as the Tara sadhana, it mentions that from the letter TAM, it manifests into Tara or in the Chenrezig sadhana, it mentions the letter HRI manifesting into Chenrezig . When we make offering, by reciting OM, 
we manifest this and that. There are many syllables in the practices. What is the reason for this? Tantra always emphasises emptiness in the beginning. So here whatever letter or syllable is mentioned, be it TAM, HR I or so forth, we need to understand that the syllable is the word and 
(its function) is for us to understand that everything -  whatever things we enjoy, whatever 
offerings we make, even we ourselves - are all labelled by name, labelled by the mind. That is 
the meaning behind having the syllables (or letters).  
 In order to practise Tantra well, you must understand emptiness.  Whenever we see the 
mantra, “ Om svabhava -shuddhah sarva- dharmah svabhava- shuddho ham, ” that means to 
understand emptiness, that every thing is empty. Only through understanding emptiness will 
your Tantric practice become powerful. Otherwise your Tantric practice is nothing when you 
don’t understand emptiness.  
 If you generate yourself as the deity without understanding emptiness, the question arises as to whether that can be the antidote to your self -grasping? It is almost impossible for that 
(practice) to be an antidote to the self- grasping. It may even increase it. It definitely will not 
help. But if you understand emptiness and then y ou generate yourself as the deity within 
emptiness, then that can be the antidote to the self- grasping (the delusions and negative 
 
Lesson  20          Page 129 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
emotions).  
 
As mentioned earlier, the quality of your practice in Tantrayana depends on how good you are in Sutrayana. Witho ut understanding the Sutrayana, your Tantric practice will not become 
powerful. So whenever you generate yourself as a deity, at the beginning, think about the emptiness of yourself and the deity that you are going to meditate on. Analyse how you and the deity exist. Do this until you have reached the conclusion that your existence depends on 
others and you cannot exist without depending on others. Similarly, the deity also exists by 
depending on others.   Ultimately, you and the deity are equal and of the same nature as both of you do not exist inherently. Within emptiness, you and the deity are not different but the same. Conventionally, you and the deity are different but ultimately you are both the same. Amongst the six deities, the mind that understands  this reality is called empty deity (ultimate deity). This mind with its 
understanding of reality transforms into a moon disc (which, in reality, is your mind).  
 We can then visualise the sound  deity  on top of the moon disc by thinking of the sound of the  
mantra of the deity in space. In reality, this is the deity in the aspect of sound.  
 The sound then comes down to the moon disc and sits on the moon disc in the aspect of letters. That becomes the letter deity . From those letters, light rays radiate out into different 
directions and touch each and every sentient being,   purifying their negative karma. The light rays then absorb back into the letters. Again the light rays radiate out into the different buddhas’  pure lands and makes extensive offerings to the buddhas and bodhisattvas. The light 
rays then absorb back into the mantra. The letters and the moon disc then transform into the shape of the Buddha. Initially, the shape is not clear but it becomes clearer later, with more details. It is like when a human being is conceived in the mother’s womb, the shape of the foetus during the early months of the pregnancy is not so clear but the shape becomes clearer when it nears the ninth month of pregnancy. The shape of the deity is the form deity . We 
meditate on that.  
 In the lower classes of Tantra, we then bless the five different points of our bodies -  at the 
crown, throat, heart, right and left shoulders. This is the seal deity (mudra deity) .  
 After having a cle ar vision of the deity, we meditate on that as long as we can and try to have a 
very clear appearance of the deity in our minds with a strong concept, “This is the deity”. That is the sign deity .   
 After that, we recite the mantra by thinking of the moon disc and the mantra garland at the 
deity’s heart. We recite as many mantras as we wish to accomplish the required number of mantras.  This is how we do the retreat in Action Tantra based on the sequence of the six 
deities.  
 In the beginning, it is important to understand the reality of the object – yourself and the deity. 
Within that, then you generate yourself as a deity. That is the way to do it. You cannot immediately meditate on the deity’s body.   
 
Lesson  20          Page 130 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
If you want to meditate on the front generation, you ca n look at sadhanas such as the Nyung -
na sadhana. From your heart the white syllable BHRUM radiates out five -coloured light rays in 
front of you, where you then visualise the mandala. The mandala is not outside but comes from your heart. The mandala has fou r doors. Inside the mandala there is a cushion. Visualise 
the deity inside the mandala. Then from the sadhana, we do the practice which includes the seven -limb practice.  
 In fact, the front generation comes from your heart and is not separate from you. That is why the Buddha’s mandala is Buddha, the Buddha’s body is Buddha, the Buddha’s eyes are Buddha and the Buddha’s ears are Buddha. In order to have (that unity as) one, you need to meditate that the mandala comes from your heart. That is the practice. I n fact when one gets 
enlightened, it all becomes one. We can say the Buddha’s pure land is Buddha, the Buddha’s palace is Buddha. In the Hinayana tradition, they do not say the Buddha’s body is the Buddha. In Mahayana, they say the Buddha’s body is the Buddha. In Tantra, it is definitely so.   
 The main point of practising Tantra is to achieve enlightenment. In order to get enlightened, you need to achieve calm abiding and special insight. This is the same in Sutrayana.  
 How does Action Tantra explain the development of calm abiding? It is through generating yourself as a deity (self -generation) and the front generation. From the front generation deity, 
you meditate on the deity, the moon disc and mantra garland without me ntal distraction for as 
long as possible. You can do the mantra recitation at the same time but your mind should focus on the three objects (the deity, the moon disc and mantra garland). This helps you to achieve 
concentration.   
 When you are good at this  concentration, you then move to the second level which is based on 
yourself as the deity and switch the objects (of focus) to the moon disc and mantra garland at your heart. You only focus on the moon disc and mantra garland, not the (self- generated) deit y. 
Then, you recite the mantra without any mental distraction.  
 When you are good at this, then you move to the subtler level of just focussing on the sound of the mantra, not even thinking of the letters or anything else. Up to this point, this is the practice of the recitation developing calm abiding.  
 
When you are good at this meditation, you move up to the next level of concentration without 
recitation. Based on the self generation, think of a flame of a butter lamp at your heart. Inside this flame is  the sound of the mantra. In this meditation, you focus on that flame of the butter 
lamp and the mantra garland without recitation for as long as possible.  
 When you are good at this, then you move up to a different level. Visualising yourself as the deity, at your heart, there is a moon disc within which there is another small deity. At the heart 
of this small deity,   there is a tongue of flame, inside of which there is the sound of the mantra. Here, y ou only concentrate on the sound, i.e., you only hear  the sound and nothing else. If you 
are able to meditate on that for three to four hours without any distractions, that means your mind is stable and you have achieved calm abiding. So up to here is the practice of Yoga with 
 
Lesson  20          Page 131 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
Signs. All of this practice is combined with emptiness but most of it entails focussing on an 
object. That is why it is called Yoga with Signs.  
 Up to here when you have achieved calm abiding, you have completed Yoga with Signs.   
 We then move on to  Yoga without Signs. Within that sta te of concentration, you again 
meditate on emptiness and understand  emptiness. At the time of realising emptiness within 
that meditation, you achieve special insight and it means you have achieved Yoga without Signs. Why is it called Yoga without Signs? Th is is because the main object of this practice, although it 
is combined with deity yoga, is emptiness.  
 
• By practising Yoga with Signs, we create the cause to achieve the Buddha’s form body and 
the Buddha’s speech.  
• By practising Yoga without Signs, we crea te the cause to achieve the Buddha’s truth body. 
Yoga without Signs is the non -dual yoga of profundity and clarity. That is the main cause for 
achieving the Buddha’s truth body.  
 As mentioned earlier, even the three lower classes of Tantra cannot explain c learly the cause 
for the Buddha’s form body. But in Highest Yoga Tantra, there is a very clear explanation of the causes of the form body and truth body.  In the three lower classes of Tantra, although it mentions deity yoga but, in reality, whether that i s the cause of the form body or not is not so 
clear.  
 To summarise:  
• We all have buddha potential but that alone is not enough.  
• We need the seed and that is the reason why we need to receive initiations in order to leave 
imprints in our mental continua.  
• By just leaving imprints in our mental continua is also not enough.  
• We need to develop that by engaging in the path through deity yoga and by cultivating calm 
abiding and special insight. That can help us achieve enlightenment.  
• How do we perform deity yo ga? Through the six deities.  
 
Having gone through the above process, how do we dissolve ourselves to become ordinary 
beings again? In the front generation, the deity disappears (absorbs into) and becomes the letters at the heart. The letters then dissolve  into the moon disc which then dissolves into the 
self-generation.  
 The self -generation then dissolves into the letters at one’s heart. The letters then disappear 
into the sound of the mantra. The last remaining object is the sound. Then again you meditat e 
on emptiness and think about how that sound exists. When we meditate on sound, it is easy to understand that there is nothing concrete there. The sound is empty from its own side and is merely labelled. Other than that, there is no sound. So the last par t is to  meditate on the 
sound and meditate on emptiness.  
 Again, within the state of emptiness, you arise as an ordinary being, your conventional self. If 
 
Lesson  20          Page 132 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
you (wish to) continue with your deity yoga, within emptiness, you can arise as a deity. For 
example, when you visualise thousand -armed Chenrezig (within session), during the break 
times, within emptiness, you can visualise yourself as two -armed Chenrezig. You can do 
whatever you want in your daily lives. Here you see yourself as illusory and not real, that there 
is no concrete ‘I’. It helps if you understand that in your daily life.

LESSON NO: 21   DATE: 23RD JUNE 2009  
 
It is important to set a pure motivation for listening to the teachings. We have had a long break 
and it is quite normal that when we come back (to class), we may experience some difficulty in 
the beginning. It is the same for me. When classes have stopped for a while, I find it difficult to 
teach again in the beginning.  
 
Geshe -la: For me, when there is a break, at the first teaching (after that), there is almost no 
mood to teach. Once the classes continue, then I don’t feel that kind of difficulty. But once there 
is a break, the first two to three classes, it will be like that.  
 
But this should not be the case. We should feel energised to study or teach again after such a 
long break. But somehow this is not the case for us.  
 There is a Tibetan proverb that says: “You offer a kilo of butter in order to take (your) leave.” 
When is this proverb used? Usually, at the beginning of a project, one is excited and makes 
(many) promises. But somehow, in the middle of the project, one changes one’s mind and 
(begins t o) make excuses. That is when this proverb is used.   
 In our case, everyone here wants to study. That is the reason why you are here. Therefore, it is 
important to complete one’s studies by putting up with any difficulties that may come along.   
 
There is  a story behind this proverb. In Tibet, there was this notorious person called Aku 
Tongpa. One day he lost his cow. He went around looking for his cow but could not find it. He lost his way and became hungry and thirsty. He didn’t know what to do.  
 
Suddenly, at a distance, he saw a big house. He decided to go to that house, thinking he could get some food there. When he arrived at the house, he made some noise to make his presence felt. The lady owner heard him shouting and took a peek through the window.   Aku Tongpa saw that this lady had a goitre and out of shock, he said, “I guess you don’t need to 
cut away your goitre.”  
 
This lady who was quite wealthy had wanted to remove the goitre but when she heard what 
Aku Tongpa said, she thought, “He must be a doctor.”  
She invited him into the house and offered him a feast. As Aku Tongpa ate,  he wondered, 
“What should I do to remove the goitre?”  
 After Aku Tongpa had finished eating, the woman asked him, “What tools do you need to remove this goitre?”  
 Aku To ngpa replied, “Just give me a cup of water and a very rough towel.” He then placed the 
woman’s head on his lap, soaked the rough towel in the water and then started to rub the goitre. The more he rubbed, the goitre got redder and more sore.  
 
Lesson  21          Page 134 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
   
In pain, the lady asked Aku Tongpa, “How many goitres have you removed so far?”  
 
He said, “Not many. Just two. One died and the other asked to be excused.”  
 Upon hearing this, the lady decided to excuse herself. She asked, “What should I do to excuse myself from this operation?”  
 (Aku Tongpa’s) answer was, “You can offer (me) a kilo of butter in order to be excused.”  
 So the next time when you cannot come to  class and want to be excused,  you should know 
what to do! There are many stories about Aku Tongpa. He is  said to be a very intelligent person. Here, he used his intelligence to get some food. This proverb is usually used when one wants to be excused fro m a project that one has already undertaken.
