It is analogous to having high regard for the medicine that helps you to recover from a serious 
illness. Having recovered, you will really appreciate the value of that medicine. Likewise, when 
you put effort into your studies and doubt arises and someone comes along who clarifies those doubts, that makes it very meaningful, isn’t it? Only then does one fully appreciate the value of the teacher or the Dharma friend who helps you (in this way).   
 This is how real faith in your guru or teacher arises, when he has really helped you to learn and 
understand (the teachings). Then a special kind of faith arises from the heart. That is real faith. 
It is the same as a patient’s respect for the doctor, the patient having complete confidence in the doctor’s ability and know ledge to cure his illness.  
 
****************  
We had been looking at the definition of the generation stage. You must understand what exactly the generation stage is. On that basis, if you were  asked, “What is the main practice that is done during the gene ration stage?” the answer would be, “The practice of taking the 
three bodies into the path.”  
 “Why do you meditate on taking the three bodies into the path?” The answer:  “To purify ordinary death, intermediate state and birth.” How do we do this?  We hav e talked about this before.  
The first (part of the reason) is established because rnam gzhag rim pa’i  rnam bshad 
extensively states:  
Those (yogas) prior to union with a knowledge consort (rig ma ‘du bya ba) are applied (sbyar) in the context of attaining Buddhahood, but are not applied on the ordinary (srid) (generation) 
stage..etc. ( Pg. 19 of the root text )  
 (Interpreter ’s corrections:  
• “Those yogas prior to …” should be amended to “Those yogas up to and including….”  
• “….in the context of attaining Buddha hood” should be amended to “…in the context of the 
buddha activities.”)  
The second (part of the reason) is established because that (text) states:  
The protection wheel is meditated on during (Itar) the ordinary (srid) generation stage and 
since it is just a requisite or preliminary (state) to the actual basis of the generation stage, it is not necessary that dharmas compatible with the ordinary (srid) (generation) stage are applied 
to them.  
Regarding that one says: The protection wheel and yogas prior to union with a knowledge consort (rig ma ‘du bya ba) are not the actual generation stage because it is pervasive that the 
generation stage meditates on taking any of the three bodies as the pat h. That is because it is 
not pervasive that the path in the continuum of one on the stage of generation is the stage of generation.  
Ngawang Palden: There is no pervasion (to your assertion), however (your) reason is 
 
Lesson  25          Page 164 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
established because there are many commo n paths of the Perfection Vehicle and the lower 
Tantras in their continuum. (Your initial assertion) is unacceptable because it would contradict 
the explanation of the bonds (ching ba) of the four requisites of sadhanas (bsnyen sgrub), and 
the four yogas, and the six requisites, and the three concentrations in the classification of the 
general bonds subsumed in the generation stage. These should be extensively known through the Stages of Mantra. (Pg. 19 –  20 of the root text)  
With relation to the sadhana ( the self- generation rite), the generation stage can be divided into 
three meditative stabilisations (or the three concentrations):  
1. The meditative stabilisation of the initial preparation (or initial union).  
2. The meditative stabilisation of the supreme victory mandala (or supreme royal mandala)  
3. The meditative stabilisation of the supreme victorious activity (or supreme royal activity)  
 There are four yogas:  
1. Yoga  
2. Subsequent yoga  
3. Sublime yoga  
4. Great yoga  
There are different ways of dividing these four yogas i n relation to the three meditative 
stabilisations. According to the Arya tradition of Guhyasamaja (or the practice of Guhyasamaja according to Nagarjuna), these four yogas are included in the first meditative stabilisation of initial preparation. When we add the meditative stabilisations of the supreme royal mandala 
and supreme royal activity to these four yogas, that makes up the six branches (or six 
requisites).  
 (Interpreter ’s correction: “ Bsnyen sgrub” is translated here as “ the four requisites of sadhanas.”   
This is usually translated as the four branches of approximation and attainment.)  
 The four branches of approximation and attainment are:  
1. The branch of approximation  
2. The branch of proximate attainment  
3. The branch of attainment  
4. The branch of great attainment  
 At the moment, it is sufficient for us to know the classifications, to understand how the self -
generation rite of the generation stage is broken up into different parts. In order to understand 
this well, we need to know the self -generation rite sadhana very well. We are not going into 
that here.  
 
To summarise the debate discussed in the text, basically there is a position that states there is the practice of visualising the protection wheel and the practice of entering into the union with a knowledge conso rt during the self -generation rite. According to some scholars, these are not  
included in the generation stage. According to Ngawang Palden, however, these are part of the generation stage. It is just that they are not the practice of taking the three bodi es into the 
path.  
(Interpreter ’s correction: “ The protection wheel and yogas prior to union with a knowledge

Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
consort…”  should be “The protection wheel and yogas up to and including union with a 
knowledge consort……”)  
 
(Geshe -la checked with the class how m any of us had read the text as he had instructed and 
also the number of times we had done so.)  
 Geshe -la: His Holiness has mentioned that he has read the Lam Rim (Chen Mo) over a hundred 
times. There is something there. So when you tell me you have read that page three times, it is not enough.   Texts are interesting, you know. When you read a second time, you get one understanding. When you read a third time, you have another understanding. When you read ten times, then again different understanding. So it  almost seems like never ending.  
 (Lama Zopa) Rinpoche mentioned before that the blessing of the text that enters your mind depends on how often you read (the text). That’s why Rinpoche says the text is not ordinary. It is the manifestation of your own ro ot guru. He always describes it like that. When you read the 
text, there is some kind of understanding. Each time (you read), you will have a different understanding and get more knowledge. Therefore, Rinpoche described all the thangkas and texts as the ma nifestations of the root guru.  
If the Generation Stage is classified there are two:  
1 The yoga of coarse single recall ( rags pa dran pa gcig pa’i rnal ‘byor ) 
2 The yoga of subtle investigation ( phra ba rtog pa’i rnal ‘byor ) 
 2.1.3.1.1.1  The yoga of coarse  single recall  
is equivalent to the coarse generation stage (bsked rim rags pa) because the Bud of Precious 
Certainty (mtha’ gcod rin po che’i myu gu) states:  
 It is not called ‘single recall’ because it recalls a deity on a single occasion (thebs gcig) or recalls 
just a single deity but (because) it recalls the deity singly, or recalls oneself and the deity as one. Though in general it engages in meditation on both the subtle and coarse, here it is the deity yoga of the coarse supporting (mandala) and supp orted (deities). ( Pg. 20 of the root text)  
 The Bud of Precious Certainty  is a text composed by Lama Tsong Khapa. Single recall does not 
mean recalling the deity on a single occasion nor does it mean recalling just a single deity. 
These two are not the me aning of single recall. “I t recalls the deity singly ” means one does not 
recall anything else but focuses (only) on the deity. Another meaning of a single recall is 
recalling  “oneself and the deity as one,”  i.e., oneself and the deity are one entity, not separate. 
These two (single recalls) appear in both the subtle and coarse generation stages.  
 In this context of the yoga of coarse single recall, it refers to the deity yoga of the coarse supporting and su pported mandala. This extract is used to prove that the yoga of single recall 
and the coarse generation stage is (one and) the same.  
 
 
Lesson  25          Page 166 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
And:  
When one trains in the generation stage, concentration to which the coarse clearly appears is generated because it is prior (man chad) to the supreme victory mandala (dkyil ‘khor rgyal mchog).(Pg. 20 of the root text)  
(Interpreter ’s correction: Again here, “prior” should be corrected to “ up to..”  
 The generation stage is divided into two:  (1)  the coarse generation stage (also known as the yoga of coarse single recall) and  
(2) the subtle generation stage (also known as the yoga of subtle investigati on).   
 Single recall means:  
• recalling  the deity singly, i.e., remembering the deity single -pointedly without distraction, 
or  
• recalling  oneself and deity as one  
 The coarse generation stage occurs in the first and second meditative stabilisations. The s ubtle 
generation stage occurs in the third meditative stabilisation:  
 
Coarse generation stage  • The meditative stabilisation of initial preparation  
• The meditative stabilisation of the supreme royal mandala  
Subtle generation stage  The meditative stabilisation of the supreme royal activity  
 
2.1.3.1.12  The yoga of subtle investigation  
is equivalent to the subtle generation stage (bskyed rim phra ba) because that (text) states:  
 
In order to train in that, one trains in conceptual yogas such as meditating on the mandala inside a subtle drop (thig le phra mo) and so on.  
 The yoga of subtle investigation is mutually inclusive with the subtle generation stage.  
 “In order to train in  that, .. ”: The meaning of “ that” is that after one has trained well in the  
coarse generation stage, then one proceeds to train in “ conceptual yogas such as meditating 
on the mandala inside a subtle drop (thig le phra mo) and so on.”   
 Although conceptual  or investigative yoga is the general name of the generation stage, in this 
context, it is given the general name of ‘subtle yoga’. ( Pg. 20 of the root text ). 
 The generation stage is generally known as investigative or conceptual yoga. But here we have said that the subtle generation stage is the same as the yoga of subtle investigation. In this 
context, it is given the name ‘subtle yoga.’  
 The practice of taking the three bodies into the path falls under the first  meditative 
stabilisation of initial preparation.  Usually when one is doing the long sadhana of a Highest 
 
Lesson  25          Page 167 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
Yoga Tantric deity, such as Guhyasamaja, the minimum that one must do is the practice that 
includes the first meditative stabilisation because all the  essential points of the generation 
stage are included there. It will be good if one can (also) do the practices of the second and the third meditative stabilisations.  
 Question: Does that mean the second and third meditative stabilisations are not part o f the 
main practice of the generation stage?  
Answer : The generation stage covers three meditative stabilisations but the second and third 
of these are not the main generation stage because the generation stage is defined as the yoga which meditates on aspe cts compatible with any of the three: death, intermediate state and 
birth.  
 Question: When is calm abiding achieved on the generation stage?  
Answer: Fully qualified calm abiding is achieved when one obtains a little control over 
primordial awareness.  
 (From this class), one  must know:  
• The divisions of the self generation rite into the three meditative stabilisations  
• That the generation stage is divided into two: coarse and subtle  
• That the meditation on the coarse generation stage occurs at the first two me ditative 
stabilisations and the subtle generation stage occurs at the third meditative stabilisation.  
• What do the coarse generation stage and subtle generation stage mean?

(As usual), we will start with five minutes in silence to do some practice meditation to make the 
mind alert and generate the mind into a virtuous state. There are many ways to do that. Even if 
you are not familiar with these methods, at the very least, you ca n visualise clearly Shakyamuni 
Buddha and then do the seven- limb practice.  
  
Close your books and get ready with a sheet of paper and your pen. For those of you who don’t 
have a strong desire to study but is content with whatever comes along the way, then you 
don’t have to do anything. You can just sit there and relax.  
 
Geshe -la: If you are those type of people, then no stress. You can relax.  
 
But for those of you who really want to study and learn, then we have to do something. There is no choice. There a re those who tell me they want to study, they want to learn but they are 
stuck. So I have been thinking for a while what we can do. I know you are all very busy and 
have your own difficulties, but I still have to think of some way to improve things. So wha tever 
exercise we are going to do is not intended to be negative (in any way).  
 All of us are the same isn’t it? When we don’t meet with difficulties or suffering, somehow we 
don’t do anything. Whatever practices need to be done are not done. But when pra ctitioners 
meet with difficulties or come down with some serious illness, only then will they start to recall 
and remember the Dharma and practise the Dharma. When things are going well and we are healthy, somehow we don’t remember the Dharma.  
 
It is the same with our studies. When we don’t meet with problems or difficulties or there is no one to push us a bit, then nothing happens.   Geshe -la: When there is no one there to pinch you, then nothing improves. You need someone 
there to pinch you.   
This is really very difficult as all of us are like that because we are all so habituated to thinking 
in such a way. What can we do? What should we do?  
When I say read the text before coming to class, even one or two pages, it should be 
understood that I a m (addressing) those people who really want to study. This is not for those 
who don’t want to study or who do not have any interest in studying. This must be made very 
clear.  So if you want to study, you must follow (the instructions, i.e., to read the te xt). 
So I am going to ask a few questions. If you don’t know the answer, it’s fine. If you know the answer, that is good.

2. What is the main practice done during the generation stage?  
3. Why is that practice d one?  
4. It is said that all sentient beings possess in their mental continua the substantial 
causes for actualising the form and truth bodies. If so, what are they?

Question: Now, do those training in the Generation Stage move to the latter (focal circle) when they obtain stability through meditating on the former focal circle (dmigs skor) as in the Completion Stage, or is it through meditating on the entire sadhana from beginning to end, in a 
single session? ( Pg. 21 of the root text)  
The question is asking the way of training in the generation stage similar to the completion stage where we only move on to that practice when we have achieved stability in the generation stage. In other words, how do we train in the generation stage?  
(In answer) to that, there are four (types of practitioners, differentiated) by means of the time that the path is generated in their continuum (lam rgyud la skye ba):  
1. Beginners (las dang po ba)  
2. Those attaining a little primordial awareness (ye shes cung zad ‘bab ba)  
3. Those obtaining a little control over primordial awareness (ye shes la cung zad dbang thob pa) 
4. Those obtaining perfect control over primordial awareness (ye shes la yang dag par dbang 
thob pa’i gang zag).  
One should know what is stated (about) differentiating how they meditate and on what they meditate. (Pg. 21 of the root text)  
(Interpreter ’s comment: “ Primordial awareness ” is usually translated as “wisdom.”)  
 You need  to memorise (the four types of practitioners). When you don’t even know the words, 
then there is no basis for any further analysis or discussion.  
 1. In the context of the first, (a beginner) must fully meditate on all the rites of the 
generation stage occur ring in the sadhana. Further, all (practitioners) from those first 
meditating on the generation stage, up to but not (including) those capable of (generating) in an instant (yud tsam) the clear appearance of all (the elements) of the coarse mandala, are called ‘beginners’. (Pg. 21 of the root text ) 
The beginner must meditate on the entire rite of the self generation in accordance with the sadhana. The entire sadhana is divided into the three meditative stabilisations:  
4. The meditative stabilisation of initial  preparation (or initial union)  
 
Lesson  26          Page 170 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
5. The meditative stabilisation of the supreme victory mandala (or supreme royal 
mandala)  
6. The meditative stabilisation of the supreme victorious activity ( or supreme royal 
activity)  
 
“Mandala” here refers to both the supporte d and supporting mandala.  
 
When the practitioner starts to meditate on the generation stage in accordance with the rite of 
the sadhana, he gradually meditates on the coarse supported and supporting mandala (i.e., the 
coarse deity and the coarse inestimabl e mansion) and is able to induce a clear appearance of 
them. But he is still unable to produce that clear appearance in an instant, like switching on a 
light switch. That practitioner is known as a beginner.  
2. Those attaining a little primordial awareness are capable of (generating) clear and 
stable appearance of all the main (yan lag) and secondary (aspects) all at once (dus cig) when visualising (gsal btab) the inestimable mansion (gzhal yas khang) and all the  coarse deities 
arranged within it, but are not yet capable of doing that for the deities of the bases (skye mched kyi lha)
30 such as Kshitagarbha and so on. ( Pg. 21 of the root text )  
(Interpreter ’s correction: “ Main and secondary aspects ” should be amended to “main and 
secondary limbs.” I prefer to translate “ deities of the bases ” as the “deities of the sources ”).  
 Those attaining a little primordial awareness are able to have a clear and stable appearance in 
an instant of the coarse supported and supporting mandala and are able to see all the major 
and minor limbs clearly. But this practitioner is still unable to do this with regards to the deities of the sources such as Kshitagarbha and so on.  
 In the case of the Guhyasamaja practice, one visualises oneself as a deity with 32 different deities at the various parts of the body. When this practitioner focuses his mind on these 32 deities on his body, he is able to gradually gain a clear appearance (of them) but he is unable to have a clear appearance of them in an instant.  
There is a difference in the method of realizational (mngon rtogs) meditation between beginners and those attaining a little primordial awareness. Beginners must meditate by visualising (gsal btab pa) the entire supporti ng (mandala) and supported (deities) slowly (dal 
bus) and in stages. Those attaining a little primordial awareness recall the coarse (aspects) all at 
once (dus cig du) and after that they must meditate by visualizing the subtle (aspects) (24) such 
as the deities of the bases in stages.  
30 The deities of the bases: visualising the eight bodhisattvas and the five goddesses on the internal and external 
bases. The bodhisattva Kshitigarbha (Heart of the Earth) is visualised in the eyes, in union with the goddess 
Rupavajra (Vajra Form); the bodhisatt va Vajrapani is visualised in the ears, in union with the goddess Shabdavajra 
(Vajra Sound); the bodhisattva Akshagarbha (Heart of Space) is visualised in the nose, in union with the goddess 
Gandhavajra (Vajra Smell); the bodhisattva Lokeshvara is visualis ed in  the tongue, in union with the goddess Vajra 
Ishvari (Vajra Sphere of Dharma); the bodhisattva Sarvanirvaranaviskambini (Eliminator of Obscurations) is 
visualised in the secret place, in union with the goddess Sparshavajra (Vajra Tactile Object); the  bodhisattva 
Samantabhadra is visualised in the joints and the bodhisattva Maitreya is visualised in the crown.  
 
Lesson  26          Page 171 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment                                                                   
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
3. Those obtaining a little control over primordial awareness are all those capable (of 
generating) clear and stable appearance of the coarse (aspects) all at once (dus cig) as well as 
all the main and secondary (aspects) of the  subtle deities of the bases, up to but not (including) 
those capable of obtaining perfect control. ( Pg. 21 –  22 of the root text)  
(Interpreter:  “mngon rtogs ” means sadhana or method of achievement.)  
 Those obtaining a little control over primordial awareness are not only able to generate a clear 
and stable appearance in an instant of the entire coarse supported and supporting mandala 
but they are also able to do this for the subtle deities of the sources.  
 The boundary of such a practitioner is up to but not including those capable of obtaining perfect control. However, the qualification of those capable of obtaining perfect control is not defined clearly here.  
It is said that for those obtaining a little control over primordial awareness both the genera tion 
and completion stage exist.  
When one reaches this (level), since it is said that it is not necessary to differentiate (so gcod) the session and post session (thun mtshams), it is permissible to do a single (self) generation each day without engaging multiple meditation (sessions) newly activating the realization (mngon rtogs) (of oneself as the deity etc). ( Pg. 21 of the root text )  
 Those obtaining a little control over primordial awareness are those able to generate clear and 
stable appearance in an instant of both the coarse supported and supporting mandala and the subtle deities of the sources. As the practitioner is already very stable, he does not have to sustain this pr actice over four sessions as is normally done in a retreat. He only needs to   do 
his practice once a day to maintain his realisation.  
Just by reaching this stage, the stage of generation is not consummated (mthar phyin pa) 
because the Mantric Stages stat es: 
Still one must consummate the stage of generation and then meditate on the stage of completion.  
4. It is said that one part (phyogs gcig) of obtaining a little control over primordial 
awareness is the completion stage and obtaining perfect control over primordial awareness is reaching the higher levels of the completion stage. ( Pg. 21 of the root text)  
(Although) it says here, “ obtaining perfect control over primordial awareness is reaching the 
higher levels of the completion stage ,” that boundary is not clearly defined. My personal 
opinion is that those obtaining perfect control over primordial awareness are those who have 
reached the completion stage level known as the isolation of mind.  
 
Question: Aren’t these supposed to be the four types of practitio ners of the generation stage? 
How come the third type of practitioner also practises the completion stage ? 
Answer : It is clearly (stated) in the text that these four types of practitioners are not necessarily 
 
Lesson  26          Page 172 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
practitioners of the generation stage because those obtaining perfect control over primordial 
awareness belong to the completion stage and for those obtaining a little control over 
primordial awareness, some belong to the generation stage and some belong t o the 
completion stage.  
 I am not sure as to why the fourth type of practitioner (i.e., those obtaining perfect control 
over primordial awareness) is included here but this may be for the purpose of positing the 
boundary of the third type of practitioner,  which is up to but not including those capable of 
obtaining perfect control.  
 As for the fourth type of practitioner, there isn’t any explanation on its boundary.  
 Question: How do the four types of practitioners correlate to the four yogas we learnt in the 
last class?  
Answer : The four yogas and the four branches of approximation and attainment are just ways 
of dividing the rite of self- generation into various parts. Here we are not discussing the 
divisions of the sadhana but rather the types of practitio ners that practise the sadhana and 
how they meditate on the supported and supporting mandala, the level of realisations achieved and so forth.   What we have been discussing so far is the coarse generation stage. We have not yet discussed 
the subtle genera tion stage.  
 
Of the four (types of) practitioners, the first three are related to the coarse generation stage. 
When does one meditate on the subtle generation stage? When one has obtained a little control over primordial awareness.  
 There are those have obtained stability  with respect to the coarse generation stage and there 
are also those have obtained control  with respect to the coarse generation stage. These (two) 
are different.  
 In some texts, it is said that those have obtained control with respect to the coarse generation stage are those who have consummated the coarse generation stage. However in other texts, this is expressed differently: those have obtained stability with respect to the coarse generation stage and those who have consummated the c oarse generation stage. (But in 
essence) these two manner of expressions are referring to the same thing (i.e., control and consummation mean the same thing.)  
 Those have obtained stability with respect to the coarse generation stage are  able to have a clear and stable appearance in an instant of the coarse supported and supporting mandala 
together with all the subtle deities of the sources without excitement and laxity. Those (practitioners) can maintain that entire visualisation clearly for four hours. That is the measure (criteria)   of having obtained stability with respect to the coarse generation stage.  
 If the practitioner can maintain that entire visualisation clearly for a month, a year, then this practitioner has consummated (or have control over) the generation stage.

This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
In summary, one must know (the following):  
• that the generation stage is divided into the coarse and subtle  
• that there are four different ways of meditating on the generation stage.  
• what are they and what they mean: beginner, t hose attaining a little primordial awareness, 
those obtaining a little control over primordial awareness and those obtaining perfect 
control over primordial awareness

Lesson  26          Page 174

Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
LESSON NO: 27   DATE: 14TH JULY 2009  
 
In Highest Yoga Tantra, who are the four types of practitioners  differentiated by means of the 
time that the path is generated in their continua?  
 
****************  
1. Beginners are those who when fully meditating on the self -generation (rite) are able to gain 
a clear appearance of the coarse mandala.  
2. Those attaining a little primordial awareness  are those practitioners who have a clear 
appearance of the coarse mandala and the principal deity and their retinue deities but are 
unable to gain (a clear) appearance of t he subtle deities of the sources.   
3. Those obtaining a little control over primordial awareness are those practitioners who have 
clear appearance of not only the coarse mandala and the principal deity and their retinue deities but are also able to gain (a clear) appearance of the subtle deities of the sources.   
4. Those obtaining perfect control over primordial awareness are those who have reached the 
“higher levels of the completion stage. ” Whether it is correct to posit that this can be taken to 
refer to  attaining the level of the completion known as the isolation of mind is something that 
we can investigate further.  
 
As to what constitutes “ an instant ( yud tsam )”, it may be the same as the measure of time 
stated in the Abhidharmakosha:  
• There, one finger snap is made of 65 parts.  
• 1/65 is the shortest moment of time.  
• 120 x 1/65 parts of the finger snaps make up the measure of time known as “ teh ge she 
ma ” which literally means “instant of that.”  
• 60 x “ teh ge she ma” make up one “ thang chig”  
• 30 x “ thang chig ” make up one “ yud tsam ” (translated here as “as instant”). Therefore 
one “yud tsam ” = 120 x 1/65 x 60 x 30  
• 30 x “ yud tsam ” make up one full day = 24 hours  
• Therefore a “ yud tsam ” = 0.8 hours = 48 minutes  
 
In terms of the measure of time, it is mentioned  that the beginners are not capable of 
generating in an instant (yud tsam) the clear appearance of all the elements of the coarse 
mandala. A “ yud tsam ” is not the shortest moment of time but in accordance with 
Abhidharmakosha, it is 48 minutes.  
 
The uncommon objects to be abandoned (thun mong ma yin pa’i spang bya) on the generation 
stage are (1) (generating) the appearance of oneself as ordinary and (2) conceiving oneself as ordinary (tha rnal pa’i snang zhen).  
(1) Ordinary appearance is not the appearance of the environment and its inhabitants (snod 
bcud) to sense awareness (dbang shes) but the ordinary appearance of the environment and its 
 
Lesson  27          Page 175 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
inhabitants to mental awareness (yid shes). The antidote to that appearance is the appearance 
dawning as th e pure supporting (mandala) and supported 31 (deities). (Pg. 22 of the root text)  
 Ordinary appearance and ordinary concept happen to both the sense and mental 
consciousnesses but what is being discussed here is ordinary appearance and ordinary concept 
in relation to the mental consciousness. The uncommon objects of abandonment on the 
generation stage are the ordinary appearance of the environment and its inhabitants to the 
mental consciousness together with conceiving them as ordinary in the mental conscio usness.  
 
(2) The antidote to conceiving oneself as ordinary is meditating on the pride of being the pure 
supported (deity) in the supporting (mandala). (Pg. 22 of the root text)  
 We live on planet Earth which is the world supporting us. So the world is “supporting” and we, 
its inhabitants, are “supported” by the supporting. Likewise, in terms of the inestimable 
mansion, it is supporting the deities inside the inestimable mansion. The deities are the  
supported.  
Further, divine pride is said to be the main (practice) whilst the meditation on appearances is said to be a (secondary) branch of that.  
Through great familiarity, alternate appearances do not arise for eye awareness etc., when in equipoise on deity yoga. It is stated:  
By consciousness being attach ed to alternate objects,  
potency is lost, alternate objects (appear) because (the pure) is not held.  
Accordingly, through mental awareness strongly engaging alternate objects, the potency of the immediate condition (de ma thag rkyen) to generate (pure) eye awareness and so on 
degenerates, those are temporarily (re zhig) not generated and the generation stage does not 
stop (ordinary) appearance to sense awareness. ( Pgs. 22 –  23 of the root text) 
When the mental consciousness is strongly meditating on the generation stage, there will come a time when ordinary appearance and ordinary concept do not appear to the mental 
consciousness and also do not appear to the sense consciousness.  
 “Through great familiarity ” means through great familiarity with the meditation of (generating) 
divine pride of oneself as the deity, the clear appearance of that and the environment as the 
mandala. Other appearances (also) do not arise to the sense consciousness such as the eye consciousness.  
 
Due to the mental awareness strongly engaging the alternate objects (in this instance, divine 
pride and clear appearance), the potency of the immediate preceding condition for generating 
the pure  eye awareness and so forth degenerates. They are (therefore) “temporarily (re zhig) 
31 Supporting and the supported: the residence and the resident, refers to the inestimable mansion and the deities 
residing in it.  
 
 
Lesson  27          Page 176 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment                                                                   
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
not generated and the generation stage does not stop (ordinary) appearance to sense 
awareness.”   
 
Is the stopping of ordinary appearances to that eye consciousness due to the force of meditation on the generation stage? The answer is “No.” This is due to the (fact that  the) 
power (potency) of the immediately preceding condition for generation of the sense consciousness is weakened because the mental consciousness is completely focussed on divine 
pride and clear appearances –  “through mental awareness strongly engaging alternate 
objects .”   
 
As we have covered previously in (the module on) Lorig , this is the similar to an awareness to 
which an object appears but is not ascertained. For example, when the mind is attracted to an object such as a beautiful form, even though at the same time the ear consciousness may be hearing something, it is not aware of what it is hearing.  We are not saying this is the same but (this example) will give you some idea of what we discussing here.   
 Here, when the mental consciousness is str ongly engaged in (generating)  divine pride and 
clear appearance, the potency of the immediately preceding condition for the generation of the eye consciousness degenerates (weakens). The eye consciousness is not generated and since that is so, there is no  ordinary appearance to that eye consciousness.  
 
Therefore, if one obtains the potency (nus pa) to stop ordinary appearance for mental 
awareness through exalted (khyad par can) appearance, the need for that is established. The 
Mantric Stages states:  
Though  phenomena have not turned into deities, still, uncontrived divine pride is 
generated, and by that the need is established. (Pg. 23 of the root text)  
 From the perspective of the mental consciousness, there is ordinary appearance and ordinary concept. Those are to be stopped through the meditation on the practice of deity yoga in the generation stage. One visualises the environment as an inestimable mans ion and everything 
arises as pure appearance.   Through meditating on the generation stage, through the force of great familiarity, there will come a time when one’s clear appearance and divine pride of oneself as the deity arise naturally without effort. When these two (clear appearance and divine pride) are stable, they 
will overcome any ordinary appearance of oneself and the sense of oneself being ordinary. This 
is what it is meant when it is said the generation stage “stops” ordinary appearance and ordinary concept. We are not relating this to whether or not in reality that phenomenon has transformed into the deity.   Question:  In this context, is “clear” appearance and “pure” appearance to be understood as the 
same thing?  
Answer:  Something appearing clearly and something appearing purely –  they do not mean the 
same thing. We do talk about pure and impure appearances and also clear appearance. Whatever is pure does not necessarily appear clearly. Here, (we are talking) about the pu re 
appearing clearly.  
 
Lesson  27          Page 177 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
From among the two: (1) clear appearance ( gsal snang) and (2) divine pride, when meditating 
on  
(1) clear appearance, analytical meditation (dpyad sgom) is predominant (gtso che) since one 
needs (to practice) analysis by visualizing again and again the aspects of the supported (deities) and supporting mandala.  
(2) After visualising those aspects, since one must generate individual (divine) pride thinking: 
“Really, I am that and that”, (one) must do both.  
It is also said that in order to generate clear appearance one doesn’t need to establish calm 
abiding focused on it first. Nor is it not generated if one does not become familiar with each deity in stages. Nor does one need to become familiar with the perfect meaning (yang dag pa’i don).(Pg. 23 of the root text)  
“Perfect meaning” refers to emptiness. This paragraph is saying that in order to generate clear 
appearance of oneself as deity, one doesn’t have to be familiar with the non- inherent existence 
of the deity.  
 In order to generate the clear appearance of oneself as the deity, one has to apply analytical meditation repeatedly to elicit that clear appearance in the mind. But this does not mean that one must have (achieved) calm abiding before attaining such clear appearance. It also  doesn’t 
mean that if one doesn’t realise the emptiness of the deity, one is unable to have a  clear appearance of oneself as the deity.   
 
Question: What is the criterion of clarity in “clear appearance”?  
Answer:  To be able to see each and every detail.  
 If one places frequent attention on those aspects, indeed clear appearance is generated by just that but one does not attain continuous stable abidance. If there is no stable abidance, one lacks the potency to stop ordinary appearance and conception. Ther efore it is certainly (nges 
par) necessary to establish calm abiding when on the stage of generation and also that should 
be done through the yoga of the subtle drop (phra thig gi rnal ‘byor). (pg. 23 of the root text)  
 It is insufficient to simply have c lear appearance. The mind must abide single -pointedly on that 
clear appearance without which one lacks the power to overcome ordinary appearance and concept. Therefore there is a need to develop this single- pointed concentration which is done 
during the subtle generation stage.  
 
It is necessary to achieve calm abiding on the generation stage (and the time for doing so is) 
when one has obtained little control over primordial awareness (wisdom). This does not mean that one who has attained little control ove r primordial awareness necessarily achieves calm 
abiding but in (answer to the question when  calm- abiding is achieved),  one attains calm 
abiding at that time of the path.  
 
 
Lesson  27          Page 178 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
The complete meditation on the hand symbol/implement or the supporting mandala and  
supported (deities) inside the subtle drop is not a meditation for beginners nor for those 
attaining a little primordial awareness but there is need for this meditation after having obtained a little control over primordial awareness. Therefore, at the time of the first two cases, the mind holds the hand symbol/implement or the subtle drop of the upper and lower (opening). ( Pg. 23 of the root text)  
 
The meditation on the subtle generation stage basically entails meditating on the complete 
supporting mandala and supported deities within the subtle drop which is placed at the lower opening of the central channel, the secret place (at the place of union of the deity father and mother).  
 When meditating on the subtle drop at the secret place, the size of the subtle drop is one mustard seed. In terms of the Guhyasamaja practice, one meditates that within that subtle drop is the complete mansion of Guhyasamaja together with the 32 deities.   
 When one can maintain that clear appearance and stable abidance of the visualisation in the subtle drop for four hours, that is the measure of having achieved stability on the subtle generation stage.  
 When one can do that for as long as one wants, a month, a year, then one will have 
consummated (come to the end of) the subtle generation stage.  
 
Further, in the first two cases, meditation of the subtle drop is predominantly meditation on 
the realization/sadhana (of self  generation) and is for the purpose of severing (mental) sinking 
and agitation. Establishing the stable abidance of the mind encompassing (dril) (everything) in 
the subtle drop is said to be done from the third level (of those who have a little control ove r 
primordial awareness).  
 Although calm abiding is not established in the case of beginners, when the mind forcefully holds letters or subtle drops etc., at the upper and lower openings (steng ‘og sna sgo gnyis), the navel (lte ba) or the heart (snying kha), this acts as a condition for the winds to gradually 
abide internally through the essential points (gnad) of the place of meditation (sgom pa’i gnas) 
and the support of the focal object.  
Although the focal objects of analytical meditation are  many and mixed (bsres pa), by the 
might (mthu) of gradually taming the winds one is capable (nus) of quickly stopping mental 
agitation when meditating on the supreme victory mandala (dkyil ‘khor rgyal mchog)
32 and so 
on. Therefore this is different to the system for establishing concentration explained in the Perfection Vehicle
33 and the three lower classes of tantra.  
32 Supreme Vic tory Mandala (dkyil 'khor rgyal mchog): one of the three types of concentration practiced during 
generation stage in which one visualises and places all deities of a particular practice in their respective place of 
the mandala by imagining them as having e manated from the bodhichitta drop of the principal deity in union with 
the consort.  
33 Perfection Vehicle: the Sutric Vehicle.  
 
Lesson  27          Page 179 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment                                                                   
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
That is because, in the case of the Perfection Vehicle and the lower tantras even though one 
attains (rnyed) actual (mtshan nyid pa) calm abid ing, if analytical meditation becomes excessive 
it is said that the previous (stable) abidance (of mind) diminishes. However in this case, though one meditates through mixing many (types of) analytical meditation when one has not yet attained calm abiding,  it is said that stable abidance is attained.  
The coarse generation stage is consummated because when one arrives at the third level (gnas skabs) because it is said that after attaining (rnyed) mental stability through (all) encompassing 
drop yoga one med itates on the subtle (stage). And when one (achieves) stability on the subtle 
(drop) of the upper opening
34 it is said that calm abiding is obtained. ( Pg. 23 - 24 of the root 
text)   
When one is a beginner and when one has obtained a little primordial awaren ess, the 
meditation of the subtle drop exists, for example, meditating on a small vajra at the upper opening of the central channel or the subtle drop at the lower opening of the central channel. These meditations on the subtle drop are done primarily for the purpose of severing laxity and excitement.  
 The meditation of establishing the stable abidance of the mind on the supported and supporting mandala inside the subtle drop at the lower opening of the central channel only  
occurs when one has gained a little control over primordial awareness.  
 Although calm- abiding is not established in the case of beginners, through their meditation on 
the objects of observation - the letters (syllables), subtle drop or hand implements -  together 
with the places of meditation [i.e., the object of observation placed on the essential points of the body such as the upper and lower openings and so forth], they act as the condition for the winds to gradually abide internally.  
 As a beginner, there are many other meditations to do such as (the meditation of the) supreme victorious mandala and so forth. Although they have to perform analytical meditation on many objects, they are able to establish stable abidance.  
 The Perfection Vehicle and the t hree lower classes of Tantra have a different way of 
establishing concentration. This is because in the case of Perfection Vehicle and the three 
lower classes of Tantra, even when one has already attained fully qualified calm -abiding but 
engages in too muc h analytical meditation, “ it is said that the previous (stable) abidance of the 
mind diminishes. ” This can happen even when one has calm -abiding. Of course, one doesn’t do 
too much analytical mediation before the attainment of calm- abiding.  
  
However in t he case of (the practice of) Highest Yoga Tantra, although one meditates through 
mixing many types of analytical meditations when one has yet to attain calm- abiding, stable 
abidance is achieved.  
 
34 Upper opening (sten sgo): is located between the eyebrows.  
 
Lesson  27          Page 180 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment                                                                   
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
The attainment of calm -abiding and special insight in Highe st Yoga Tantra is a little 
complicated. There are different views as there are some who say that calm- abiding and 
special insight are achieved simultaneously  (in Highest Yoga Tantra) while others say that they 
are achieved in stages.  
 In the Perfection Ve hicle and the three lower classes of Tantra, calm- abiding and special insight 
are achieved separately. It is not possible for calm- abiding and special insight to be achieved 
simultaneously.  
 Now I want to highlight the things you must know (from this lesson):  
• What the objects of abandonment of the generation stage are, i.e., ordinary appearance and ordinary concept. As to what these two are, you can discuss further (this Sunday).  
• When does one meditate on and achieve calm -abiding, i.e., at the subtle generation 
stage?  
     Interpreter: Ven. Tenzin Gyurme

LESSON NO: 28   DATE: 16TH JULY 2009  
 
We will spend five minutes in silence to transform our minds into a virtuous state so that this 
session of listening to the Dharma can be transformed into a virtuous activity. We should think 
of how fortunate we are to have the opportunity to do these studies and with that recognition and understanding, generate delight and enthusiasm.  
 
The more we study the teachings of the Buddha, the more doubts we can elim inate. The more 
doubts we eliminate, more ignorance is removed. More ignorance we remove from our minds, 
the closer we will be to enlightenment. When we think about what we are doing here, it is actually very meaningful.  
 
The fact we have this opportunity  to learn the teachings of the Buddha is actually due to the 
kindness of this human rebirth which we have now and which gives us the chance and 
opportunity to study and practise. If we are now in the lower realms, it will be difficult for us to 
do this. This is something we have to remember all the time.  
 
All of you know intellectually what I have said so far. You have heard it many times. But what you have to do is to internalise what you have heard so that when you reflect on these things, you should hav e some feeling for them and they  should not only stay up there in the brain but 
actually move your heart and mind. That has to come from your side and it is your personal responsibility to do this.  
 In the context of studying this (present) text, it is v ery difficult for us to know its full meaning, 
i.e., the meaning of each and every single word and to eliminate (every) doubt or qualm we 
may have with respect to them.  
 But there is a way of studying this. For example, before you can read, you first have  to know 
the alphabet, without which you cannot read. Likewise here, we are trying to get an overview (a rough idea) of the manner of progressing on the (Tantric) path.  
 
There are some students who are unduly worried. It doesn’t mean that when you read va rious 
other texts (and commentaries) they are going to benefit you especially when you are just a beginner. As a beginner, I think reading too many texts sometimes may not be (so) beneficial. 
As you have to start with something as a basis (for your studies ), you should first use whatever 
is in the text. When we take a particular text as a base, for example the one we are using here 
and then you read other books that help your understanding of this text, then that is beneficial. But (some students) are not l ike that. They read (a bit) here, (a bit) there. In the end, they get 
nothing. Maybe they understand a little bit here and there but they don’t understand the root text.  
 There are some (students) who say (the root text) is very tough. Even the language a nd words 
are tough.  That is true. It is tough because we are beginners.  I think the style of learning here accords with the name of this program, Basic Program. We are just looking at the basics. Just like someone learning and picking up a new language, one starts from the basic building blocks 
 
Lesson  28          Page 182 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
of the language, its alphabet.  
 
Likewise here, when we study Tantra, we are trying to get a rough understanding or overview of Tantra such as learning the phrases, words and so forth. When we study in such a way, once we get this rough understanding, then interest will come. That interest will motivate you to look at other materials. This is something you should keep in mind. It doesn’t matter whether you understand each and every single word or not but what we are  trying to achieve here is a 
rough understanding and to get some idea of what we are studying. That is sufficient for our purpose.  
 Actually this topic is not easy. Even in my case, though I have completed my studies, whenever I look at (this topic), ther e are more things that I do not know than what I do know. When you 
listen to the teachings, you have to pay attention. Then, you will learn something. So keep your mind alert.  
 
***************  
 Highest Yoga Tantra can be divided into (1) the generation stage and (2) the completion stage.  
 There are three levels of practitioners who meditate on the generation stage:  
1.  The beginner is someone who is able to attain clear appearance of the mandala  together 
with the deity that he is meditating on. He does this  gradually and is able to achieve clarity but he is unable to achieve such  clarity within a short period of time. (Generally speaking, within half an hour).                                                      
 Take a person who is doing the practice of Guhyasamaja. He meditates as he goes through that sadhana, moving from the concentration of initial preparation to that of the supreme victory mandala and then the supreme victorious activity. He is able to meditate clearly in a gradual way, but he is unable to achieve clarity within half an hour.  
 2.  The practitioner who has attained a little primordial awareness (also translated as “those on whom a little wisdom has descended”) is able to gai n clear appearance of the mandala and the 
deity he is meditating on within a short period of time, i.e., within half an hour. That practitioner also visualises the subtle deities of the sources, i.e., the various deities that are placed at  different parts  of the body.  He is able to gradually see them clearly but he is unable 
to do so within half an hour.  
 3.  The practitioner who has attained a little control over primordial awareness is able to see very clearly:  
• the coarse mandala together with the coarse deity within half an hour and  
• the subtle deities of the sources at the various parts of the body within half an hour.  
 The practitioner who has attained perfect control over primordial awareness has achieved stability with respect to the clear appeara nce of not only the coarse mandala and the coarse 
deity but also achieved stability with respect to the clear appearance of the subtle deities and is able to abide in that state of stability for 1/6 of the day, i.e., four hours. That person has 
 
Lesson  28          Page 183 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
achieved stability of the coarse generation stage .  
 
When that practitioner can maintain that stability of the clear appearance of both the coarse and subtle deities for a month or even a year, then the practitioner is said to have consummated the coarse generation s tage . 
 The first two levels of practitioners - the beginner and those on whom a little wisdom has 
descended - are those practitioners who also meditate on the subtle drop. For example, there 
is a meditation visualising the hand implements at the upper opening of the central channel. There is also a meditation on the subtle drop at the lower opening of the central channel at the secret place. They meditate on these for the purpose of eliminating laxity and excitement. They visualise the hand implements of th e deity at the upper opening of the central channel in order 
to eliminate laxity. In order to eliminate excitement, they meditate on the subtle drop at the lower opening of the central channel at the secret place.  
 The third level of practitioners, who has  attained a little control over primordial awareness (or 
who have attained slight mastery with respect to wisdom) also meditate on the subtle drop but (they do so) for the purpose of achieving a single -pointed mind or calm abiding.  
 Once the practitioner has consummated the coarse generation stage, he meditates on the 
subtle drop to achieve calm- abiding. For example, he meditates on a five -pointed blue vajra the 
size of a mustard seed at the upper opening of the central channel. When he can maintain the clarity of this visualisation for four hours, this is the measure or sign of having achieved calm 
abiding . After that, he moves on to meditate on the subtle generation stage.  
 How (does one) practise on the subtle generation stage ? Does one achieve calm abiding when 
one is practising the subtle generation stage?  
 Using the meditation on the five -pointed blue vajra at the upper opening of the central channel 
as the basis, one does the practice of emanating and gathering back.  One visualises emanating 
(the vajra) exponentially, i.e., one becomes two, two becomes four and so forth, till the vajras 
gradually cover one’s entire face and fill the whole space in front of oneself. One then gradually gathers back all the vajras and dissolves them into the small ( five-pointed blue)  vajra. The 
sequence of gathering back is the same as the sequence of emanating.  
 
After performing this practice of emanating and gathering back all these subtle hand 
implements - the five-pointed blue  vajra in the case of the Guhyasamaja p ractice - one 
visualises that blue  vajra transforming into the entity of the holy body in the aspect of 
Akshobhyavajra, having three faces and six arms.  
 With that as the basis, one again performs the practice of emanating and gathering back in the same w ay. In the end, when everything has gathered back, one visualises that Akshobhya 
dissolves into the concentration being at one’s heart.  
 Following that is the meditation on the subtle drop at the lower opening of the central channel, specifically at the s ecret place of the union of the father and mother.  
 
Lesson  28          Page 184 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
• One visualises a very small subtle drop the size of a mustard seed (inside that secret place).  
• One (then) visualises the entire mandala and the deities of Guhyasamaja inside that drop.  
• One places one’s mind (there) to achieve clarity and stability.   
• Once one has achieved stability with respect to the clarity (of that visualisation), one 
(moves on to) meditate on the secret place of the principal deity within the mandala that is 
inside the subtle drop  
• There is also a subtle drop within that secret place of that principal deity.  
• One then visualises the entire mandala and the deities of Guhyasamaja inside that subtle 
drop.  
 
When one can maintain stability with respect to the clarity of the entire visualisation for four hours, then one will have  achieved stability of the subtle generation stage.  
 When one can maintain the stability and clarity of that entire visualisation for a month, a year, one has consummated the subtle generation stage . If one asserts that calm abiding and 
special insight can be achieved simultaneously, then I guess this will be the time when one achieves that realisation.   ●  The meditation on the subtle drop is for achieving calm abiding.  
●  Meditating on the practice of emanating a nd gathering is for the purpose of  achieving the 
subtle generation stage.  
●  When one has consummated the subtle generation stage, one has achieved special insight.  
 You have to remember the stages that I have described today. There are many (different) views 
with respect to these (stages) and it is quite difficult to come to a definitive conclusion as there are many (different) expert (views).  
• One achieves calm abiding when one has obtained a little control over primordial 
awareness.  
• After one has achieved calm abiding, one continues to meditate on the subtle generation 
stage.  
• When the subtle generation stage is consummated, one achieves special insight.  
 
Question: During the subtle generation stage, through the force of meditation, if the winds enter, abide and dissolve in the central channel, would that whole yoga become a completion stage?  
Answer : Generally, it is said that at the subtle generation stage, even through the strength of 
meditation, the winds cannot enter, abide and di ssolve in the central channel.  
 But it is (also) said that for trainees of sharp faculties, it is possible that even during the subtle generation stage, the winds do enter, abide and dissolve in the central channel. But that is still not called the comple tion stage. There are qualms (regarding this issue). To keep it simple for 
now, (we will say that) during the subtle generation stage, the winds will not enter, abide and dissolve in the central channel.  
 
 
Lesson  28          Page 185 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
Question: But wasn’t there an earlier description of emanating and dissolving the winds in the 
central channel?  
Answer : That description relates to the emanation and withdrawal of the hand implements. 
One gathers back whatever one has emanated but it is not the winds. Also, when there is the 
absorption in to the concentration being at one’s heart, that is also done at the level of 
visualisation. It is not as if the winds are entering the central channel.  
 
What we have described here is the process whereby calm abiding and special insight is 
achieved separa tely. Calm abiding is achieved first followed by one achieving special insight 
later. When one reaches the end of the subtle generation stage, then one moves on to the completion stage.  
 It is said that for a practitioner who has consummated the subtle ge neration stage, his entire 
visualisation is so stable that even when he is meditating in the middle of an open field, all his surroundings become the celestial mansion of the deity. Because of the stability of his realisation, even if it rains, the rain do esn’t enter his mandala. Due to the stability and power of 
his visualisation and concentration, such a practitioner can even walk on water because he can visualise all the subtle atoms of the water to be as hard as vajras.  
 Of the five Highest Yoga Tantri c paths to enlightenment, the person who has consummated the 
subtle generation stage is on the first path, the path of accumulation.  
 As long as you understand what is the coarse generation stage and its related meditations and what is the subtle generatio n stage and its related meditations, that is sufficient. We are not 
going into the details of a specific deity practice because that is not the subject matter of this module. If you want to learn that, then we need to have a separate class.  
When we follow the self -generation rite (or sadhana), the yoga of repetition (i.e., mantra 
recitation) comes only after the meditation of the subtle generation stage. When one has consummated the subtle generation stage, then one recites mantra and engages in the yoga o f 
repetition. Because one has achieved the full -fledged union of calm abiding and special insight,  
whatever mantra one recites will actually yield the power (of that mantra). (At our level), no matter what we recite, nothing happens.  
 Geshe -la: Only the m outh gets tired!  
The coarse generation stage is consummated because when one arrives at the third level (gnas 
skabs) because it is said that after attaining (rnyed) mental stability through (all) encompassing 
drop yoga one meditates on the subtle (stage). And when one (achieves) stability on the subtle 
(drop) of the upper opening
35 it is said that calm abiding is obtained.  
Then, by the wisdom of individual investigation (so sor rtogs pa’i shes rab) repeatedly performing dispersal and gathering, one attains t he peerless (phul phyin) concentration of the 
union of calm abiding and insight (zhi lhag zung ‘brel gyi ting nge ‘dzin). And when yoga such as this becomes stable it is posited as (‘jog pa) the consummation of the generation stage which is 
35 Upper opening ( sten sgo):  is located between the eyebrows.

stated in the Mantric Stages to be the system of both36 (traditions of) Guhyasamaja. ( Pg. 24 of 
the root text )  
Whatever I have explained earlier is the meaning of these two paragraphs. Lama Tsong Khapa 
mentioned in one of his texts that this is the order of the visualis ations: one performs 
emanating and gathering back on the basis of the subtle implements at the upper opening of 
the central channel. When stability is achieved with respect to that, one meditates on the 
subtle drop at the lower end of the central channel. This is not so clearly explained in (other 
texts) as to when calm abiding is achieved, the meditations to be performed and so forth.  
 
After this is the elimination of qualms: Whether one posits achieving the stability with respect 
to the coarse and subtle generation stages to be the same or different from consummating 
these two (stages)?  
One Scholar says: When the subtle generation stag e is consummated, calm abiding is obtained.  
Ngawang Palden: But this does not teach the full limit (mtsham) of new obtainment (because) 
by training in that way one obtains stability on each of the coarse and subtle generation stages. 
The mode of positing t heir individual measure of consummation should be known from the 
presentation of Grounds and Paths of our teacher, Yangchen Gawa’i lodro (dbyangs can dga’ 
blo).  
The explanations of each measure of consummating the obtainment of stability are formulated in accordance with Penchen Choegyen’s (pan chen chos rgyan) clear presentation of Khedrup 
Rinpoche’s (mkas grub rin po che) intention. Still, there is some basis for analysis because the 
Mantric Stages states:  
Positing that one consummates the generation sta ge when such yoga (has become) stable, is 
the system of the two (traditions) of Guhyasamaja.  
And the Illuminating Lamp states:  
Beginner’s concentration is the yoga of the first stage and abiding in that is (attaining) stability in that or the consummation of that.  
Also the Analysis of the Array of the Four Commentaries states just that. But except for these 
(explanations) it does not appear that the terms ‘stability’ and ‘consummation’ are clearly and individually explained (gsungs). (Pgs. 24 –  25 of the root text)  
There is another qualm which is brought up and has to be eliminated. There are some who may wonder: The main meditation during the initial preparation is (focussed on) the deity and not so much on emptiness. Our response  is: No, that is not true.  Emptiness has to be meditated on 
at all times.  
In this context one says: It follows that there is no need for the three concentrations, such as 
36 Two tr aditions of Guhyasamaja Tantra: Arya Nagarjuna's tradition (asserting thirty -two deities) and Acharya 
Jnanapada's tradition (asserting nineteen deities).  
 
Lesson  28          Page 187

Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
the initial preparation (dang po sbyor ba) etc., to meditate on emptiness because apart from a 
few cases such a s training in primordial awareness or the special resolution (lhag mos) 
subsumed within clear light, generally (pha cher) in the context of the initial stage, the method of meditating on the appearance (snang phyogs) of the deity circle is explained as the  main 
(practice).  
Ngawang Palden: There is no pervasion because in the context of the three concentrations, one must cultivate (skyong) the clear, profound, non -dual yoga determining the meaning of non-
inherent existence through focusing on the deities as their focal object. The Mantric Stages 
states:  
The yoga entering just that state (de kho na nyid) of selflessness of the mind possessing the aspect of the circle (of deities) was explained by Peldre Dorje (dpal ‘bras rdo rje) as the general function (spyi lgro) of the three concentrations and this was the opinion of Yeshe Shap (ye shes 
zhabs). Indeed in the context of the first stage one mainly meditates on the appearance (snang 
phyogs) of the circle of the deities, yet one trains in everything being like an illusion through 
inducing strong determination of the non- inherent existence of all dharmas. Then having 
meditated on the circle of deities, one enters emptiness which is the referent object (‘dzin 
stangs) of the discernment determining the meaning of non -inherent existence through 
focusing on the deities as the focal object. Then also the profound, clear, non- dual yoga of the 
appearance of the held aspect: the supporting (mandala) and  supported deities, acts to 
meditate intermittently?? (res kyis) on the realisation.  
The profound, clear, non -dual yoga in the continuum of one on the generation stage and the 
yoga without signs (mtshan med kyi rnal ‘byor) of the lower tantras are superior in strength to 
the path comprehending emptiness in the Perfection vehicle, from the perspective of method and wisdom being one in nature, because the Ocean of Attainments of the Generation Stage  
states:  
The discernment meditating on non- inherent existence  through focusing on the circle of deities 
in the mandala generates both the nature of the accumulation of merit through focusing on the circle of deities in the mandala and the accumulation of primordial awareness through generating (awareness) possessing the referent object of non- inherent existence.  
And:  
The discernment ascertaining non- inherent existence through focusing on the circle of deities 
as its focal object is one hundred times superior in its potency to act as an antidote to grasping at true ex istence than for example the discernment ascertaining non- true existence through 
focusing on a sprout.  
The etymology (sgra bshad) of the generation stage, the method of how it meditates, the 
differences between each of the deities and the different modes o f explanations set forth by 
each of the siddhas do not appear to be explained clearly in other texts. Therefore they are to 
be understood through the Mantric Stages alone. (Pgs. 25 –  26 of the root text)

Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
Even though you may not completely understand these  paragraphs, what you have to know is 
this: “ The profound, clear, non- dual yoga in the continuum of one on the generation stage and 
the yoga without signs (mtshan med kyi rnal ‘byor) of the lower tantras are superior in strength 
to the path comprehending e mptiness in the Perfection vehicle .” Why? Because “ from the 
perspective of method and wisdom being one in nature .”  
 We have now completed the generation stage. We will move on to the completion stage.  
 Geshe -la: From now on, I will (teach) like this. You must read otherwise there is no point, you 
won’t understand anything. I am not going to read the details because it takes too long. At the end of the day, everyone is just confused.   You need to learn some of the basics. You must get some kind of real und erstanding not only of 
the words but getting some knowledge (of what they mean).   I think I will try this way because when I read the sentences, they do not make any sense to you and make you more confused. But in order for you to understand (what is being taught), you must read. I will change to this different way (of teaching) but in order (for it) to benefit you, you must read. If you don’t read, you won’t get anything (from the class). I am encouraging everyone to read. It doesn’t matter whether you un derstand or not but you must read (the text).  
Question: It is said that the union of calm abiding and special insight is achieved on the Highest 
Yoga Tantric path of accumulation but for the three lower Tantras as well as the Sutric path, 
the union of calm abiding and special insight is only achieved at the path of preparation. 
(Please clarify this point)?  
Answer : This is an extremely contentious issue. In the Sutric vehicle, it is not said that those 
who have achieved the union of calm abiding and special insight are necessarily  on the path of 
preparation. T here are outsiders who are non -Buddhists who achieve calm abiding and special 
insight.  
 
It is clearly (stated) in the teachings that for those of definite Mahayana lineage, i.e., those who enter the Mahayana path from the beginning, when those bodhisattva s achieve the union of 
calm abiding and special insight focussing on emptiness , then they also achieve the heat level 
of the path of preparation simultaneously.  
 No one is asserting that they have achieved the path of preparation simultaneously when they 
achieve the union of calm abiding and special insight. When one achieves the union of calm 
abiding and special insight, that does not mean that one has achieved calm abiding and special insight focussing on emptiness. This is an extremely contentious issue . If we don’t elaborate, 
then it is quite difficult.   Here, in the Highest Yoga Tantric path of accumulation, it is said they have achieved the union of calm abiding and special insight but do they mean the union of calm abiding and special insight focuss ing on emptiness?  
 
 
Lesson  28          Page 189 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
According to Aku Sherab Gyatso, he says that the person on Highest Yoga Tantric path of 
accumulation achieves the union of calm abiding and special insight but this is not the union of calm abiding and special insight focussing on emptin ess.  
 There are also some masters who say that when they achieve the union of calm abiding and special insight focussing on emptiness, they also achieve the union of calm abiding and special insight focussing on the deity’s body. Although they have achiev ed this, this does not mean 
they are on the Highest Yoga Tantric path of preparation. This is extremely complicated.  
 Question: What is the deity’s body made of?  
Answer : The deity’s body that is meditated on in the generation stage is just an imagined bod y, 
a meaning generality, a mere creation of the mind. It is not a coarse body or form. On the generation stage, (everything is done at the level of) visualisation and nothing really happens. It starts from there. When one  moves to the next level, one can become a real deity. It will be very clear when we look at the completion stage.  
 Question: With reference to pg. 23 of the root text, in the first paragraph, “Accordingly, through mental awareness strongly engaging alternate objects….,.” what does “altern ate 
objects” refer to?  Answer:  This can be understood as “other objects.” In this instance, it can be taken to refer to 
the deity’s body.  When the mental consciousness is strongly engaged on the deity’s body, for 
example, due to that, the power of the im mediate preceding condition will degenerate the 
sense consciousness.  
 
Question: Does this mean the practitioner is not using his sense consciousnesses in his daily 
routines?  
Geshe -la: When you say you can see when the eye is opened, that is not necessarily  so. For 
those in deep meditation, (when) they are really concentrating on their object (of meditation), (even) with their eyes opened, you can wave your hands in front of them, they will not see (you). This is because the immediate preceding condition for  the generation of the eye 
consciousness has degenerated.   Only when they come out of meditation will they be able to see ordinary appearances. But during the time of meditation, their minds are fully concentrated on the object (of meditation) and ordinar y appearance will not appear to them.  
 Question:  (inaudible)   
Answer : The main objects of abandonment of the generation stage are ordinary appearance 
and grasping. That is overcome when one has achieved stability on the generation stage. The meditation on the generation stage is done with the mental consciousness, not the sense consciousness. For someone who has achieved stability on the generation stage, to the mental consciousness of that practitioner, there is no ordinary appearance and concept.  
 For someone who has consummated the subtle generation stage, he is able to  remain in the 
state of meditation for a month or a year. Whatever appears is pure appearance to that mental consciousness. At that time ordinary appearance also do not arise to the sense consciousness.  
 
Lesson  28          Page 190 
 
This transcript is restricted to those who have received a Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre                    Grounds and Paths of Secret Mantra 
FPMT Basic Program       Transcript of the Oral Commentary by Geshe Chonyi  
 
 
When the practitioner arises from meditative equipoi se, the sense consciousness starts to 
manifest again. Then ordinary appearances will arise. It will be quite sad if our teachers don’t recognise us!   Please read up on the completion stage for the next class. The first section gives you the definition of the completion stage but the rest is just settling qualms and debates on the 
definition.  
 The next important section is the “ Enumeration and definite order of the five stages”  from page 
28 to the second paragraph on page 29. You must know this. Think care fully as you read 
because the entire meaning of the completion stage is summarised in these one and a quarter pages.
